title: AI in International Sales at Kärcher
text:
Welcome
This is the reading companion to the AI in International Sales at Kärcher workshop.
It follows the Kärcher Sales Cycle from end to end, from acquisition to follow-up, and treats AI not as a drawer of disconnected tool demos but as support for one continuous sales process.






TipHo to use this book



Each chapter matches one workshop slide deck, in the same order and with the same number. Read it cover to cover to follow the whole cycle, or jump straight to the step you need.

title: Introduction
text:
The Sales Cycle
The book opens with three short reference sections to dip into as needed:
Its heart is the Sales Cycle, whose chapters lean on the same small set of shared references: the cycle itself and a handful of sales frameworks.
Before the details, here is the whole journey on one page. The customer moves from left to right through five buying stages; your nine sales steps sit underneath, each one an answer to the question the customer is asking at that moment.
The five stages come from the customer’s side of the table. Your nine steps are how you meet them: one step in Awareness, three as the customer weighs options in Consideration, two while they pin down requirements in Evaluation, two as they decide, and one to make the choice stick in Adoption. The middle of the map is widest on purpose, that is where most of the selling work happens.

title: Introduction
text:
The Kärcher sales cycle mapped to the customer's buying journey
A two-lane overview. The top lane shows five customer buying stages (Awareness, Consideration, Evaluation, Decision, Adoption) with the question the customer asks at each. The bottom lane shows the nine Kärcher sales steps, grouped under the stage each one serves.

The customer moves left to right through five buying stages. Each of your nine sales steps answers the question the customer is asking at that point.









NoteThe frameworks behind the cycle





Optional background, not a workshop step. The chapters teach the idea, not the acronym; open this when you want the shared reference list in one place.








Framework
Where
What it is




Level-3-Selling
Module 0
Sell business outcomes and long-term value, not single products


Guiding Principles
throughout
Earn the Right, Involve, Be Customer Centric — the behaviour standard for AI use


BANT
Step 2
Budget, Authority, Need, Timing — qualification; a hypothesis until confirmed


Buying Center
Step 3
The stakeholder map: decision-maker, purchasing, user, plus a secondary role


DISC
Step 4
Dominant, Influential, Steady, Conscientious — an observation framework read live in the conversation, not guessed from public profiles


SNIP
Step 5
Situation, Need, Impact, Pay-off — a funnel for sharper questions


SPEICS
Step 5
Service, Performance, Economy, Image, Comfort, Safety — the dimensions of the whole need


FABC
Step 6
Feature, Advantage, Benefit, Check — the structure of a value argument


TCO
Step 6
Total Cost of Ownership — value, quantified







How each chapter is built
Every Sales Cycle chapter follows the same shape, so once you know the pattern you can move through any of them quickly. (The reference sections up front — Foundations, the AI Toolkit, and Workspace Basics — are shorter how-tos you read once.) Two parts are not panels: the journey map opens each chapter with the current step highlighted, and the chapter body then works through the step with worked examples built on the example account, NorthStar. Everything else is one of the panels below — and a panel’s look tells you at a glance whether to act on it or simply read it. Here is the whole set, shown as it really appears:


KEY POINTS open the chapter: the few things worth taking away. If you read nothing else, read this.



Recipe · a hands-on shortcut

Open

the AI tool to use.

Prompt

a ready-to-copy prompt

Save as

what to do with the result — a chapter can have more than one card.









TipCopy into the … Gem



A ready-to-copy prompt; the copy icon means paste it straight into the tool.



Do this · once
A single thing to carry out right then, such as a one-time setup. Do it before you read on.



YOUR STEPS is the hands-on exercise near the end of the chapter.
Run the steps yourself — the bordered card makes the “now you try it” part unmistakable.








A plain note like this — only a thin yellow edge, no solid header — is background to read, not a task.









NoteWhat the research says





At the very bottom of each chapter, collapsed by default: the evidence and sources behind the advice, for when you want to dig deeper.



The quick way to navigate: a panel with a solid dark or yellow header — KEY POINTS, RECIPE CARD, DO THIS, YOUR STEPS — wants your attention or action; a quiet panel with only a thin yellow edge is there to read.


Every recipe works two ways
Recipes ask you to attach the Doc a previous step produced. You have two ways to get that Doc, and both are correct:

Practice on the case. Every NorthStar Doc already exists, ready-made. Open the one the recipe links, make a copy to your Drive (File → Make a copy), and attach the copy. You never have to build an earlier step first to try a later one.
Do it for real. Attach the Doc you saved in your own previous step, for your own account.

Same recipe, same prompt — only the attached Doc changes. The full set of ready-made NorthStar Docs lives in the example-docs appendix.

title: 1  Reply to a Customer Email
text:
1.1 What you need
A reply is sitting in your inbox and you cannot find the right words. This is the fastest win in the whole toolkit: Gemini sits inside Gmail, you give it a short brief, and you get a draft you only have to read, fix, and send. Nothing goes out on its own.

title: 1  Reply to a Customer Email
text:
The email — the message open in Gmail, or the practice email below.
Your facts — what you want to say, in a few plain words. Gemini asks; you answer.

title: 1  Reply to a Customer Email
text:
1.2 Let Gemini interview you
Paste and run — nothing to fill in:






TipThe reply brief



Role: You are a helpful assistant who writes clear, friendly customer emails.
Task: Help me reply to a customer email. First interview me, one question at a time: what is the customer asking (read the open email, or ask me to paste it), and what do I want to say. Then write the reply.
Context: I work in sales at Kärcher, the cleaning-technology company.
Format: Three short paragraphs, friendly and professional, no jargon.
Check: List anything you assumed about the customer or the product so I can confirm it before I send. Do not state any specs, prices, dates, or commitments I did not give you.

title: 1  Reply to a Customer Email
section: 1.3 No email at hand? Practice on this one
text:
1.3 No email at hand? Practice on this one
A made-up customer inquiry — copy it and paste it when Gemini asks for the customer’s email.
From: Sandra Willems, Site Manager, Van Dalen Facility Group
Subject: Machine demo next week — three questions

Dear Mr. Bauer,

Thank you for your visit last week. Before we confirm the demo, three questions:

1. Could we move the demo from Tuesday to later in the week? Our cleaning team lead is back from leave on Thursday and should really see it.
2. Is there an operator guide in Dutch we could look at beforehand?
3. Does the demo include a short introduction for our cleaning team, or is that a separate booking?

Best regards
Sandra Willems
Site Manager, Van Dalen Facility Group
Your facts for the reply: the demo can move to Thursday next week at 10:00; an operator guide in Dutch exists and you will attach it; a short on-site introduction for the cleaning team is part of every demo.

title: 1  Reply to a Customer Email
section: 1.4 Read it once before you send
text:
1.4 Read it once before you send
The draft saves you the blank page, not the judgment. Three checks, ten seconds:

Does it say what you actually want to say — and does the tone sound like you?
Did it invent a detail you did not give it — a date, a spec, a promise? Fix it or cut it.
Look at the assumptions Gemini listed and confirm the ones that matter.

Do this often? Build yourself a helper Gem with fixed instructions — see Gems. The full Gmail page, including thread summaries, is Gmail.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 2  Catch Up on a Long Email Thread
text:
2.1 Ask Gemini, not your memory
Twenty replies, five minutes before the call. Instead of scrolling, ask Gemini for the state of things — what has been decided, what is still open, and who owes the next step. Two ways in: from inside the thread, or without opening it at all.
Two clicks, one paste:
Any busy conversation from your own inbox works as practice — a scheduling back-and-forth, a quote clarification. Everyone has one.
crumbs: 2  Catch up on a Thread

title: 2  Catch Up on a Long Email Thread
text:
In Gmail, click the Gemini icon at the top right — the chat panel opens on the right.
Paste the prompt into the chat and run it. Have a specific conversation open? Say “this email thread” instead of “my last email thread”.







TipThread in five bullets



Summarise my last email thread in five bullets: what has been decided, what is still open, and who owes the next step.
crumbs: 2  Catch up on a Thread

title: 2  Catch Up on a Long Email Thread
section: 2.2 Even faster: don’t open the thread at all
text:
2.2 Even faster: don’t open the thread at all
Gemini is connected to your Gmail, so you can ask about your mail without finding the thread first. Replace [name or company] and run it in Gemini:






TipAcross your mailbox



Summarise my recent emails with [name or company] in five bullets: what has been decided, what is still open, and who owes the next step.
crumbs: 2  Catch up on a Thread

title: 2  Catch Up on a Long Email Thread
section: 2.3 Scan it against the mail once
text:
2.3 Scan it against the mail once

Do the “decided” points match your memory? Spot-check one against the original mail.
Asked across your mailbox? Glance at which emails it pulled — the right thread, the full picture?
Note who owes the next step. If that is you, that is your to-do.

The full Gmail page, including reply drafting, is Gmail.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher
crumbs: 2  Catch up on a Thread

title: 3  Research a Customer
text:
3.1 Open the Gem, name the company
A company name and a website are enough. The Account-Briefing Gem runs a deep research pass across the public web and comes back with a briefing: who they are, what they care about, and a first idea of how Kärcher could help.
Fill in the brackets and run it. It shows its research plan first — start that, and the research takes a few minutes on its own:
Open the Account-Briefing Gem — for practice, pick any real company you know.

title: 3  Research a Customer
text:
TipAccount briefing



Build an account briefing for [company name] ([website]) in preparation for a Kärcher Professional sales conversation.

title: 3  Research a Customer
text:
3.2 A briefing is a hypothesis

Click two or three of the cited sources — do they actually say what the briefing says?
Facts about size, sites, or plans: treat them as to-verify until a source confirms them.
The “how Kärcher could help” idea is your starting hypothesis for the first conversation, not a fact.

How Deep Research works is Deep Research; the full move in the sales cycle — including the pursue-or-pass decision — is Lead Research.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 4  Get Ready for a Customer Meeting
text:
4.1 Let Gemini interview you
A meeting is coming up and you want to walk in prepared. You do not have to write a brief — Gemini interviews you: which company, who you are meeting, what the meeting is about. You answer in plain words, and you get five topics the customer is likely to raise and five good questions to ask — raw material for a human conversation, not a script.
Paste and run — there are no blanks to fill:
No meeting this week? Answer the interview with the practice case: Van Dalen Facility Group — Sandra Willems, Site Manager — a machine demo next week.

title: 4  Get Ready for a Customer Meeting
text:
TipThe meeting-prep interview



Role: You are a customer-meeting prep assistant.
Task: Help me prepare for a customer meeting. First interview me: ask me one question at a time — which company I am meeting, who the person is and their role, and what the meeting is about. Then prepare me.
Context: I work in sales at Kärcher, the cleaning-technology company.
Format: After my answers, give me (1) five topics the customer is likely to raise, and (2) five good questions I should ask. Short bullet points.
Check: Flag any point that assumes a fact I did not give you, so I can check it before the meeting.

title: 4  Get Ready for a Customer Meeting
section: 4.2 Keep what fits, drop the rest
text:
4.2 Keep what fits, drop the rest

Keep the topics that fit this customer; drop the generic ones without guilt.
Pick the two or three questions you would actually ask — and make them yours.
Look at the flagged assumptions: anything worth checking before the meeting?

Preparing the human side of a meeting in full — attitude, rapport, reading the person — is Conversation Prep, Step 4 of the cycle.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 5  Turn Meeting Notes into a Follow-up
text:
5.1 Let Gemini interview you
The meeting went well; the notes are a mess. Paste them as they are — bullets, fragments, half sentences — and Gemini turns them into a tidy follow-up note: what was agreed, what is open, one clear next step. Sent while the meeting is still warm.
Paste and run — there are no blanks to fill:
No meeting notes at hand? Practice with a quick demo debrief: demo at Van Dalen went well, Anna liked the handling; open: power outlet in the basement, delivery time for two machines; agreed: quote for two machines next week, propose a training date; send the Dutch operator guide.

title: 5  Turn Meeting Notes into a Follow-up
text:
TipThe follow-up brief



Role: You are an assistant who turns rough notes into a tidy customer follow-up.
Task: Help me follow up after a customer meeting. First ask me to paste my rough notes, and ask who the customer is if my notes do not say. Then write the follow-up.
Context: I work in sales at Kärcher, the cleaning-technology company.
Format: A short follow-up note to the customer: a greeting, a two-sentence recap of what was agreed, the open points, and one clear next step.
Check: List anything you inferred from my notes, and do not add prices, specs, or promises I did not write.

title: 5  Turn Meeting Notes into a Follow-up
text:
5.2 Your notes, nothing more

Does the recap match what actually happened in the meeting — nothing added, nothing dropped?
Look at what Gemini says it inferred: promises, dates, or specs you did not write get cut.
Is the next step the one you want? Then make the tone yours and send it.

Keeping deals moving after the meeting is Follow-up Steering, Step 9 of the cycle.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 6  Set Up a Simple Account Tracker
text:
6.1 Ask for the structure
The lightweight home for your accounts is a plain Google Sheet — and you do not have to design it. One prompt builds the structure with example rows showing the format; you replace them with real accounts and it is yours.
In a blank Google Sheet, click Ask Gemini (top right) and run:
Gemini often shows a small plan first — a few steps and an Approve button. Just approve it, and the tracker gets built.

title: 6  Set Up a Simple Account Tracker
text:
TipBuild your account tracker



Create a simple account tracker with these columns: account, segment, stage, value, last-contact date, next step, note. Add three example rows so I can see the format.

title: 6  Set Up a Simple Account Tracker
section: 6.2 Make it yours in two edits
text:
6.2 Make it yours in two edits

Rename or add columns to match how you actually work — the tracker serves you, not the template.
Replace the example rows with two or three real accounts while the format is fresh.
Keep one rule: every account gets a next step. An empty next-step cell is a signal.

With the tracker filled, the other data moves open up: find insights, the pipeline at a glance, and the full Sheets page is Google Sheets.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 7  Find Insights in a Sales Sheet
text:
7.1 What you need
You have a Google Sheet full of accounts and numbers, and a simple question you would normally dig out by hand: which deals went quiet? With your data in a table, you do not calculate — you ask. Gemini reads the sheet and answers in plain words; no formulas needed.

title: 7  Find Insights in a Sales Sheet
text:
Your sheet — a table with your accounts: one row per account, columns like stage, value, last contact, next step.
Your question — what you want to know, in normal words.

title: 7  Find Insights in a Sales Sheet
section: 7.2 Ask your data a plain question
text:
7.2 Ask your data a plain question






TipAsk your data a plain question



Here is my account sheet. Which accounts have had no contact in the last three weeks and have no defined next step? Use only what is in the sheet.


Same move, other questions: “What is my pipeline value by segment?” — “Which deals have been stuck in the same stage the longest?”

title: 7  Find Insights in a Sales Sheet
section: 7.3 No sheet of your own yet?
text:
7.3 No sheet of your own yet?
Use the ready-made practice tracker: twelve fictional accounts with stages, values and next steps. Open it, then File → Make a copy, and work on your copy.

Open the practice tracker

title: 7  Find Insights in a Sales Sheet
text:
7.4 AI sorts, you decide
A wrong answer sends your attention to the wrong place. Treat the answer as a hypothesis you confirm, not a verdict:

Does the list match what you know about these accounts?
Read the assumptions it states — for example, which date it treated as “today” — and correct them if they matter.
Spot-check one or two rows in the sheet before you act on the list.

The full Sheets page — building a tracker, formulas in plain words, ready-made Gems — is Google Sheets. The sales version of this move, ranking your own accounts to decide where the week goes, is Pipeline analysis.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 8  See Your Pipeline at a Glance
text:
8.1 One prompt, one new tab
Where does the money sit? With your accounts in a Sheet, one prompt turns the list into a compact overview — pipeline value by stage and by segment — without a single formula.
Gemini creates the summary on a new tab by itself — nothing to insert, nothing to place. If it shows a small plan with an Approve button first, just approve it. Then open the new “pipeline value” tab at the bottom of the sheet: your pivot table is there.
No sheet of your own yet? Use the practice tracker (File → Make a copy) — it has stages, segments and values ready to summarise.

title: 8  See Your Pipeline at a Glance
text:
TipPipeline at a glance



What is my total pipeline value by stage and by segment? Give me the result as a new table in a new tab called pipeline value. Use only what is in my data.

title: 8  See Your Pipeline at a Glance
section: 8.2 Trust it, then verify one total
text:
8.2 Trust it, then verify one total

Pick one small segment and add its values by hand — does the total match?
Read the assumptions it states (excluded rows, currencies) and confirm the ones that matter.
The table says where the money sits — what you do about it stays your call.

The full Sheets page is Google Sheets. The sales version of this move — ranking your own accounts to decide where the week goes — is Pipeline analysis.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 9  Turn Your Sheet into a Short Report
text:
9.1 Ask for the story, not the table
The numbers live in your sheet; the people you report to want the story. One prompt turns your account data into a short written status report — plain text, ready to paste into an email or a Doc.
Open your sheet, click Ask Gemini (top right), and run:
No sheet of your own yet? Use the practice tracker (File → Make a copy) — it has everything the report needs.

title: 9  Turn Your Sheet into a Short Report
text:
TipStatus report from the sheet



Summarise this account sheet as a short status report I can paste into an email: total pipeline value by stage, the three biggest open deals, and the accounts with no recent contact or no next step. Use only what is in the sheet.

title: 9  Turn Your Sheet into a Short Report
text:
9.2 A report others act on

Verify one number by hand — the biggest deal or one stage total — before anyone quotes it.
Read the assumptions it states (like which date counts as “today”) and correct what matters.
It is a snapshot of the sheet, not a forecast — say so when you send it.

Prefer a table instead of prose? That is Pipeline at a Glance. The full Sheets page is Google Sheets.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 10  Let Gemini Write the Formula
text:
10.1 Say what you want, get the formula
The part of Sheets most people dread — and the easiest to hand over. You say what you want counted or summed, in plain words; Gemini returns the formula and tells you where to put it.
Same move for sums: “Write a formula that sums the value of all accounts in the ‘Negotiation’ stage.” On the practice tracker (File → Make a copy), the count prompt above should find two accounts.

title: 10  Let Gemini Write the Formula
text:
TipCount in plain words



Write a formula that counts how many accounts are in the "Proposal Sent" stage.

title: 10  Let Gemini Write the Formula
text:
10.2 Paste it, then sanity-check once

Count or add the rows by hand once — does the formula’s result match?
If the cell shows an error, paste the error message back to Gemini and let it fix the formula.
Formulas live: when your data changes, the number updates on its own — that is the point.

The full Sheets page — building a tracker, asking questions, tidying data — is Google Sheets.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 11  Clean Up Messy Data
text:
11.1 Ask for a cleaned copy on a new tab
Exported lists and imports arrive messy: dates in three formats, segment names spelled four ways, values as text with currency symbols. Cleaning that by hand is exactly the work you can hand over — as long as the original stays untouched until you have checked the result.
If Gemini shows a small plan with an Approve button first, just approve it.
No messy data at hand? Use the Sales-Data practice file with deliberately broken formatting — mixed date formats, inconsistent names, values as text (File → Make a copy first, then clean your copy).

title: 11  Clean Up Messy Data
text:
TipClean the import



Here is my imported data. Create a cleaned version as a new table in a new tab called clean data: dates in one format (YYYY-MM-DD), consistent segment names, and values as plain numbers. List every change you made.

title: 11  Clean Up Messy Data
section: 11.2 The original stays until you checked
text:
11.2 The original stays until you checked

Compare two or three rows against the original — same account, same value, same date?
Read the change list: renames and reformats are fine; changed values are not.
Keep both tabs: the clean one is for working, the original stays as your reference.

The full Sheets page is Google Sheets.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 12  Improve an Existing Presentation
text:
12.1 What you need
A deck that does the job but looks rough: crowded slides, topic-label titles. Gemini sits inside Google Slides and can rebuild a cluttered slide in one click — in your deck’s own brand and style. The message stays yours.

title: 12  Improve an Existing Presentation
text:
Your deck — the presentation open in Google Slides, or your own copy of the practice deck.
Your message — what the slide should say. Gemini restructures; the message stays yours.

title: 12  Improve an Existing Presentation
text:
12.2 One click: Enhance this slide
Select the slide that needs work, click Ask Gemini (top right), and choose Enhance this slide. Gemini analyzes your deck and proposes a rebuilt version — in your deck’s brand, colors, and fonts, and fully editable afterwards (a normal slide, not an image). Not happy? Run it again for another take.
Then hold the result against your original: the main message and your confirmed facts stay, repetition gets shorter, and nothing new appears.
No Enhance option in your panel? Ask for it in words instead:






TipRebuild a slide



Rebuild this slide with a clearer structure. Keep the main message and all confirmed facts, shorten repetition, and do not add new content. Keep the deck's colors and fonts, and use only rectangular shapes — no rounded corners.

title: 12  Improve an Existing Presentation
section: 12.3 No deck to improve right now?
text:
12.3 No deck to improve right now?
Use the practice deck: a short, fictitious sales update whose slides are deliberately rough — the ten-bullet feature slide is your raw material. Open it, then File → Make a copy, and improve your copy.

Open the practice deck

title: 12  Improve an Existing Presentation
section: 12.4 The title states the answer, not the topic
text:
12.4 The title states the answer, not the topic
Enhance makes a slide cleaner; it cannot decide what the slide says. Before you accept:

Are the main message and your confirmed facts still there — repetition shortened, nothing new added?
Does the title state the answer, not the topic? “Product overview” names a topic; a strong title tells the audience what to take away.
Kärcher brand check: graphic elements are rectangular. If Gemini added rounded corners, fix the shapes or run it again.
Still crowded? Run Enhance again, or split it: one idea per slide.

The full Slides page — building a deck from scratch, generating images and the rules that apply — is Google Slides. Building a full customer deck is Presentation Demo, Step 7 of the cycle.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 13  Translate Sales Material for Your Market
text:
13.1 A whole Doc: one click in Docs
A sales text written in one language, needed in another. For a whole Google Doc, one click does it: Tools → Translate document creates a translated copy with the formatting kept. For short text that should sound natural in the market — an email, a slide’s text — Gemini adapts instead of translating word-for-word.

title: 13  Translate Sales Material for Your Market
text:
Open the Doc, then Tools → Translate document.
Name the copy, pick the target language, click Translate.
A translated copy opens as a new file — your original stays untouched. Remember: this is raw machine translation, so the check below matters all the more.

title: 13  Translate Sales Material for Your Market
section: 13.2 Short text: let Gemini adapt it
text:
13.2 Short text: let Gemini adapt it
Paste and run:






TipThe translation brief



Role: You are a translator for B2B sales material.
Task: Help me adapt a sales text for another market. First ask me to paste the text, and ask which language and market it is for. Then translate it.
Context: I work in sales at Kärcher, the cleaning-technology company. Keep all product names and model designations exactly as they are.
Format: The translated text, ready to use: natural for the market, professional in tone, same structure as the original.
Check: List anything that did not translate cleanly — idioms, units, legal or price wording — so I can review it. Change no facts, numbers, or product names.


No text at hand? Take the practice email from Reply to a Customer Email and translate it into your market’s language.

title: 13  Translate Sales Material for Your Market
text:
13.3 Read it like a local

Numbers, units, and product names: unchanged? The list Gemini gives you is where to look first.
Does it sound like something you would say in that market — or like a translation?
Customer-facing? Have a native-speaking colleague glance over it before it goes out.


Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
14.1 First, the split
The same kind of customer email arrives again and again, and you handle it by hand the same way every time. That routine is what Workspace Studio is for: describe it once, in plain words, and a standing flow runs every time — while every decision stays with you.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
One-off · Gmail. A single email that needs an answer now: draft it with Gemini in Gmail — see Reply to a Customer Email.
Recurring · Studio. The same kind of email, again and again: describe the routine once in Workspace Studio, and it runs every time.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
14.2 The example flow

An email from a customer arrives.
The flow classifies it: quote request, complaint, order-status question — or other / unclear.
You get a short Google Chat message with the category and a one-line summary.
You decide what happens next — the flow never answers the customer on its own.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
14.3 What you need

A repeating email — one kind of email you handle again and again: quote requests, complaints, order-status questions.
Your routine, already clean — you already handle it by hand, the same way every time. Flows automate habits, not chaos.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
14.4 Describe it, don’t program it
You do not click the flow together step by step. In Workspace Studio you describe the whole task in one prompt — Gemini builds the flow from it, and you adjust the steps afterwards.






TipDescribe the flow



When a new email from a customer arrives in my inbox, classify it as a quote request, a complaint, an order-status question, or "other / unclear", and send me a message in Google Chat with the category and a one-line summary. Do not reply to the email.



Open Workspace Studio, paste your description into Describe a task for Gemini, and click Create.
Review the steps Gemini built — you can edit, reorder, delete, or add steps.
Keep the decision with you: the flow notifies — it never replies to the customer on its own.
Use the flow’s built-in Test run to see what it would do, then turn it on — and check the first real Chat messages against what you would have said.

A good description is specific: name the app (“send me a message in Google Chat”, not “message me”), use real email addresses instead of “my manager”, and give exact times for scheduled flows (“every Monday at 7:30”, not “weekly”). More in Google’s tips for creating flows with AI.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
text:
14.5 More flows worth building
The same pattern — describe it, let Studio build it, keep the decision — covers more of a sales week than inbox triage. Ideas worth trying once the first flow runs:

Triage your inbox. “If an email contains a question for me, label it ‘To respond’.” The sorting happens on its own; you answer in one block.
A Monday-morning nudge. “Every Monday at 7:30, send me a message in Google Chat reminding me to update my account tracker.” Scheduled flows keep the routine honest.
Action items become tasks. “Create tasks from action items in my email.” Follow-ups from customer visits stop living in your head.
Watch a shared file. “When the quote document in Drive is edited, send me a message in Google Chat.” You see movement on a deal without checking.

Flows can also start from a template in Studio’s gallery, a working flow can be shared with the team like a Drive file, and Studio reads attachments too — it can pull details like an order number out of an email before it notifies you. Kärcher already builds with Studio: Google’s launch post features a Kärcher workflow that cut feature-idea drafting time by 90 percent.

title: 14  Classify Incoming Emails and Notify Me in Chat
section: 14.6 Automate after the habit, not before it
text:
14.6 Automate after the habit, not before it
The rule that keeps flows useful, as three checks:

Is the manual routine already clean and repeatable? Automating a messy process just makes the mess faster.
In the first days: do the categories match what you would have said? If not, sharpen the trigger and action wording.
One-off mail stays in Gemini in Gmail — a flow is only for the routine that repeats.

The shape of a flow, common stumbles, and where automation fits in your first 30 days: Workspace Studio and the Action Plan.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 15  Foundations
text:
15.1 AI takes the grunt work, you keep the judgment
The promise of AI in sales is narrow and useful: it takes the repeatable preparation, drafting, and structuring work off your plate so your energy goes where it counts, which is trust, outcomes, and the customer.
A fair word before we begin: this can feel like a lot, new tools and new habits at once. So this module keeps things simple and introduces one idea at a time, each on its own short page. You are not expected to take it all in on the first read. Start here with the mindset, then move through the pages in order or jump straight to the one you need.
AI is not here to make the customer conversation colder or more automated. It is here to compress the hours you lose to research, summaries, and first drafts, so you arrive better prepared and spend more attention on the parts of selling that should stay yours. Understanding the customer, shaping value, and building trust do not get delegated.

title: 15  Foundations
text:
15.1.1 What eats your week
We asked colleagues across the international sales organization where their time really goes. These three jobs came out on top:

Pipeline and data analysis scored highest (4.1 out of 5) and was the top frustration.
Meeting preparation and research came second (3.9 out of 5).
Proposals and content creation came third (3.7 out of 5).

These are exactly the repeatable, time-heavy, still quality-sensitive tasks that AI is built for.
One worry also came through loud and clear in the same survey: reliability (whether you can trust the numbers AI produces). We address it through the quality gate. It is the reason you stay in the loop, not a reason to avoid the tool.

title: 15  Foundations
section: 15.2 What generative AI is good at, and what it is not
text:
15.2 What generative AI is good at, and what it is not
Generative AI is strongest when you need a first structured version quickly. Give it a clear instruction plus the right information (context), and it returns a fast first draft. In practical sales work that means summarizing messy notes, turning rough input into a usable structure, and reframing the same point for a different stakeholder.
What it cannot do is guarantee truth just because it sounds confident. The model has no memory of your customer or your last call unless you put that context into the prompt, and its training data can be out of date, so it can sound definitive even when it is wrong. A polished sentence can still damage credibility if the fact underneath it is not real.
So the operating model is simple, and it holds for every step of the cycle: use AI to create a strong starting point, then apply your own judgment before anything goes further. AI gives you a draft, not a free decision.

15.2.1 Trust, verify, use
Because a confident answer is not a verified answer, one rule governs everything customer-facing. Trust the draft as a starting point, verify every claim that matters, and only then use what holds up. That is the whole discipline in three words.
The stop sign is just as important as the rule. Three things should make you pause: AI can invent facts and figures that sound certain, it can miss your account context entirely, and its information can simply be out of date. None of that is a reason to avoid AI. It is the reason you keep a human in the loop.
The stop condition is blunt on purpose: no evidence, no send. If you cannot stand behind a number or a claim, it does not go to a customer; you revise first. This single rule sits behind every workflow in the rest of the training, and the quality gate turns it into two concrete steps.






NoteWhat the research says





Optional background, not a workshop step. It is here for anyone who wants the evidence behind the approach this page takes. Skip it and you lose nothing you need for the actual work.
Augmentation is real, and the biggest gains go to the less experienced. In a field experiment with 5,179 customer-support agents, access to a generative-AI assistant raised productivity by about 14% on average, with the largest gains for newer, lower-skilled workers, because the tool spreads the tacit know-how of the best performers (Brynjolfsson, Li & Raymond 2023). In a controlled study of professional writing tasks, ChatGPT cut the time spent and raised the quality of the output at the same time, while narrowing the gap between weaker and stronger writers (Noy & Zhang 2023). That is exactly the “fast, usable first draft” this module promises, and the broader marketing-and-sales literature frames the same shift as a division of labor between human and machine rather than a replacement of the human (Davenport et al. 2020).






NoteThe market view: where the hours go



Industry estimates point the same way as the team survey. Sellers spend only a minority of their week actually selling, with the rest absorbed by preparation, research, and administration, and analysts estimate that generative AI could free a meaningful share of that time and lift sales productivity, if output is checked rather than trusted blind (McKinsey 2023). Treat such figures as a direction of travel, not a promise: the gain is real, but it is conditional on the discipline in this module.

title: 16  NorthStar Facility Services
text:
16.1 Our opportunity: a pilot of three battery scrubber driers
Throughout the book we follow one account, NorthStar Facility Services, a fictional B2B facility and contract-cleaning company in the Benelux region, based in Rotterdam, in the Building Service Contractors (BSC) industry, one of Kärcher’s twelve target industries. It is the running example behind every step of the sales cycle, so the chapters can show real work instead of abstractions.
One practical consequence, worth knowing up front: because NorthStar is fictional, there is nothing real to research live. So every NorthStar Doc the recipes mention comes ready-made — open it, make a copy to your Drive, and work with the copy (the full set is in the example-docs appendix). When a chapter runs a tool live, it points it at a real company and says so, like the one ISS demo in Step 2.
NorthStar runs a mixed, aging fleet across many sites. Machines break down mid-shift, and the company wants standardization, uptime, and service continuity.
The opportunity we work is a pilot of three battery scrubber driers (the Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack); a broader rollout opens only after the pilot proves out.

title: 17  Level-3-Selling: sell outcomes, not just products
text:
17.1 At Level 3, service is part of the value
Level-3-Selling is the commercial lens behind the whole training, and it explains why AI and good selling fit together rather than pull against each other. The idea is a shift in the center of gravity of the conversation.
At Level 1 and Level 2, the exchange stays transactional. Product, price, and availability lead, the customer treats you as a vendor, and you are easy to swap out. This layer still exists, but much of it can be standardized, and AI will automate more of it fast. That is precisely why it pays to move up. At Level 3, business outcomes lead instead of product specifications: the conversation shifts toward operating impact, service continuity, and long-term value, the customer sees you as a partner, and the relationship is defended on value rather than on price.
AI matters here for a specific reason: it removes the low-value drafting and sorting work, which gives you more time for the parts that create strategic relevance. In other words, AI should not make you more transactional. It should free you to operate more consistently at Level 3, which is the zone where a human sales professional stays irreplaceable.
At Kärcher, Level 3 means more than selling a machine. It means presenting a complete solution that includes service, continuity, and the commercial logic over time. Service is not an afterthought bolted on at the end; it belongs in the value story from the start, and total cost of ownership (the full cost over the machine’s life, not just the purchase price) is often the strongest bridge from a product feature to real business value.
There is a simple test you can apply to any offer or argument: if the customer takes the service out, does the business case get weaker? If the answer is yes, service belongs inside the value story from the beginning, not as an optional extra that is easy to negotiate away. (The full value-argument mechanics, including how to quantify total cost of ownership, come back in Value Argumentation.)

title: 17  Level-3-Selling: sell outcomes, not just products
text:
NoteThe mindset line



If you remember one sentence from this module, make it this one:

“AI removes drafting work. You keep the judgment, trust, and customer relationship.”

AI is useful because it removes workload, not because it replaces the human parts of selling. That is exactly why AI and Level-3-Selling reinforce each other instead of competing.








NoteWhat the research says





Optional background, not a workshop step. It is here for anyone who wants the evidence behind the approach this page takes.
“Sell outcomes, not products” has a research pedigree. From the customer’s side, a “solution” is not a bundle of products but a relational process: understanding requirements, integrating, deploying, and supporting after the sale (Tuli, Kohli & Bharadwaj 2007). And for a manufacturer like Kärcher, combining goods with services into a hybrid offering is a well-documented way to grow and defend value, which is the formal case for “service belongs inside the value story” (Ulaga & Reinartz 2011). The finer mechanics of value-based selling and total cost of ownership come back in Value Argumentation.

title: 18  What you may use, and the quality gate
text:
18.1 What you are allowed to use
This page holds the one discipline that travels with every AI step in the cycle: what you are allowed to put into the tools, and the short check you run before anything goes to a customer.
In our approved tools, signed in with your Kärcher Workspace account, you are allowed to work with real customer information. You do not have to worry that the platform will misuse it or train its models on it: your Workspace contract takes care of that.
What stays with you is light but real: share only the customer information the task actually needs, and verify the output before it reaches the customer. The tools keep the data confidential; judging what is relevant to put in, and whether the answer is true, is still your call.

title: 18  What you may use, and the quality gate
text:
18.2 Three principles, one quick check
The Kärcher Guiding Principles are practical filters you run over any draft before it reaches a customer:

Earn the Right — only send what adds real value. If the draft is generic, weak, or unsupported, it has not earned the right to leave your desk.
Involve — prepare with AI, but talk with the customer. AI helps you arrive better prepared; it does not replace confirming and developing the need in the conversation.
Be Customer Centric — tie every point to a real customer need. An argument that sounds impressive but is not linked to a confirmed outcome is still weak. Relevance comes before rhetoric.

Together they give you a fast review standard: does it add genuine value, does it support rather than replace the real conversation, and does it connect clearly to a customer outcome? If one of those checks fails, revise before you send.

title: 18  What you may use, and the quality gate
text:
18.3 The quality gate: Prompt · Validate
For anything that will reach a customer, run one short routine. Learn it once, use it always:

Prompt — use the prompt guide as a starting point, and give the tool only the context the task needs, so the input quality is high.
Validate — verify the output against the source and cut anything unsupported. Confident output is not verified output, and the trap is worst with public web sources, where the model blends fragments into a fluent story that is partly inferred: keep what the sources support, mark the rest as unconfirmed.

The gate has two steps rather than one because quality is not a single check at the start. You raise the quality of the prompt, then you check the truth of the output, and each step catches a different kind of risk.
The normal sign-off rules still apply on top of this: formal proposals, total-cost documents, and anything touching pricing, legal, or contract terms go through the usual manager or specialist approval before they reach a customer.

title: 18  What you may use, and the quality gate
text:
18.4 Try it yourself
One short drill puts the hard rule to work: no evidence, no send.

Try it · make the call
Read this draft follow-up to NorthStar:

“Dear Mr. Meijer, I confirm the Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack is the ideal solution for NorthStar. With a 450 mm working width and 1,800 m²/h area performance it is among the most productive scrubber driers in its class. Our standard quote is attached.”

Decide Go (useful and verified), Revise (close, but not yet), or Stop (wrong or unsafe), then justify it with one quality reason and one Level-3 reason.

As you debrief, ask the two questions that matter:

Was there anything in the draft you could not stand behind without checking it first?
What would make you Stop rather than Revise?

The expected answer is Revise. On the quality side, it states product specifications as confirmed fact without any check, and it jumps straight to a quote. On the Level-3 side, there is no business outcome, no link to the downtime pressure NorthStar described, and no confirmed need behind the recommendation. The draft is a perfectly fine starting point, which is exactly the point: AI gave you a fast first version, and your judgment turned it from “send” into “not yet.”

title: 18  What you may use, and the quality gate
text:
18.5 Next: meet the tools
That completes the foundation: the mindset, the selling lens, and the rules give you the why and the guardrails. What is still missing is the how — the tools themselves. The next part, Your AI Toolkit, introduces the tools and shows you the few clicks each one takes, followed by short tours of the everyday Google Workspace tools. The sales cycle itself begins right after, once the toolkit is in your hands.






NoteWhat the research says





Optional background, not a workshop step. It is here for anyone who wants the evidence behind the approach this page takes.
Confident is not correct, which is why judgment stays with you. When 758 consultants used GPT-4, quality rose roughly 40% on tasks inside the model’s “jagged frontier” of competence, yet on a task chosen to fall outside that frontier, AI users were markedly more likely to reach the wrong answer, because they followed fluent, confident output over the edge of what the model could actually do (Dell’Acqua et al. 2023). This is not a fringe risk: people systematically under-check automated aids and absorb their errors, a pattern known as automation bias and complacency (Parasuraman & Manzey 2010). The good news is that the fix matches the prompt guide: small prompts that encourage you to pause and think, known in the research as “cognitive forcing functions,” measurably reduce over-reliance, whereas simply adding an explanation does not (Buçinca et al. 2021). That is the Check step and the Go / Revise / Stop gate, doing real work.




Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 19  Your AI toolkit: four tools
text:
19.1 Gemini and Gems
Four tools sit behind this whole training. We introduce each tool one at a time below, and you meet it again later at the exact point in the cycle where it is first needed.
Gemini is the general-purpose assistant: you type a request and get a draft back. Think of it as a new colleague, fast and capable, who knows nothing about your work, so you brief them from scratch every time.
A Gem is that same colleague after one good briefing. You load in your best instructions and a few reference documents once, and it sticks: from then on you just name the account and it already knows how we work. You do not build these yourself; you receive one ready-made Gem for each step of the cycle.
Some of those Gems do their best work by researching first — going out and gathering current facts from the sources you point them at, and citing them — before they draft. The mode that does that is Deep Research, and because we lean on it so often it has its own page.
→ Learn more about Gemini and Gems on the Gems page.
→ The research mode several of these Gems run on has its own page: Deep Research.

title: 19  Your AI toolkit: four tools
text:
19.2 NotebookLM
NotebookLM works only from the documents you give it, and nothing else. Think of it as an expert who has read exactly those sources. Ask a question and the answer comes straight from those pages, with the source shown.
And it does more than answer: from the same sources it can summarise them, pull the facts into a table, or draft a briefing or even a presentation, each grounded in what you uploaded.
That source-anchoring is what makes it the natural home for the reliability questions from the survey: you get outputs you can trace back to the page, not confident guesses.
→ Learn more about NotebookLM on the NotebookLM page.






NoteGem vs NotebookLM





A Gem works from everything it knows, while NotebookLM stays inside the documents you give it and shows its source.

title: 19  Your AI toolkit: four tools
text:
19.3 Workspace Studio
Workspace Studio turns a task you keep repeating into a standing routine that runs on a trigger — when an email from X about Y arrives, do Z — so the work happens without you starting it by hand.
It is no-code: you describe the trigger and the action in plain language, and the result still waits for your approval before anything sends.
→ Learn more about Workspace Studio on the Workspace Studio page.

title: 20  Gems
text:
20.1 What a Gem is, and where Gemini fits
A Gem is the tool you reach for at almost every step of this training. Each step ships one ready-made, waiting in your Workspace.
For the training you never need to build a Gem yourself; you open one you were given, feed it, and save its answer. If you are curious how to build your own, there is an optional note at the end.
Behind every Gem sits Gemini, the general-purpose assistant in your Kärcher Workspace (gemini.google.com): you type a request and get a draft back. A blank Gemini chat is the “new colleague” — useful for quick, one-off questions, but it knows nothing about your work, so you brief it from scratch every time.
A Gem is that same colleague after one good briefing. Instructions and a few reference documents are loaded in once, and they stick: from then on you just name the account and it already knows how we work. You do not build these yourself; you receive one ready-made Gem for each step of the cycle.
For the real work in this training you open a Gem, not a blank chat. Each step’s Recipe Card gives you a direct link, and clicking it opens that Gem already briefed and ready. That link is your way in — so the fastest way to understand a Gem is simply to open one, which is exactly what you do next.

title: 20  Gems
text:
20.2 Try a shared Gem once
Before the real sales workflow starts, try the Prompt Optimizer: it needs no customer data and no document attachment, and you reach it straight from a link, with no menus to navigate.

Recipe · Open and test a shared Gem

Open

Prompt Optimizer ↗.

Do

Send this short test prompt:

Prompt

Improve this prompt: give me tips for handling price objections.

Then

Skim what the Gem changes — that is a Gem doing its job. Having opened it once, you can now also find it again under My Gems (next).



This test is the exception, not the normal workflow: you paste one harmless line only to activate and understand the shared Gem. In the real chapter tasks, the Recipe Card tells you which Gem to open and which document to attach.

title: 20  Gems
text:
20.3 Find a Gem again
Opening a Gem from its link is the “first door in”, and there is a reason that matters: a shared Gem usually does not appear in your own overview until you have opened it once. After that first use you can find it again without the link — through the settings (gear) icon at the bottom left of Gemini, then Gems.






Note📸 Screenshot



Two steps: first click the gear icon at the bottom left of Gemini. Then, in the menu that opens, click Gems.



Gemini home screen with the bottom-left settings menu open and Gems highlighted.




Next, you see the Gem Manager. The Gems you have already opened appear under My Gems.






Note📸 Screenshot



Use this overview to open the right prepared Gem for the step you are working on.



Gem Manager overview with several Kärcher Sales Gems listed under My Gems.

title: 20  Gems
section: 20.4 Feed it a document, not a wall of text
text:
20.4 Feed it a document, not a wall of text
The Gem already knows how to do the job. What it needs from you is the input for this account — your briefing, your notes, your need profile, a spec sheet. You attach that as a document rather than pasting it, so the input can be as long as it needs to be and you keep a clean record of exactly what the Gem read.
A Gem reads many formats — a Google Doc, a PDF, a Word file, a slide deck, a spreadsheet, even an image — straight from Drive or uploaded from your computer. In this training the input is usually a Google Doc, because each step exports its answer as a Doc that becomes the next step’s input (more on that below); but you are not limited to Docs.

In the Gem’s prompt box, click the + (add / attach) and choose Add from Drive, or upload a file from your computer.
Pick the document for this account — for example your NorthStar briefing Doc, or a PDF spec sheet.
Type your short request, or use the prompt printed on the Recipe Card, and send.







Note📸 Screenshot



Inside the Gem, use the + menu in the prompt box and choose Add from Drive.



A Kärcher Sales Gem with the plus menu open and Add from Drive selected.

title: 20  Gems
section: 20.5 Save the answer as a document
text:
20.5 Save the answer as a document
In some situations it is useful to save the Gem’s answer as its own document — for example when a later step will build on it. The clean way to do that is to export it to a Google Doc:

At the bottom of the Gem’s answer, open Share & export.
Choose Export to Docs — Gemini creates a new Google Doc in your Drive.
Give it a clear name (account + step), so you can attach it at the next step.







Note📸 Screenshot



After Gemini creates the result, open Share & Export and choose Export to Docs.



Gemini result with the Share and Export menu open and Export to Docs selected.




You will not do this every time. If you only need to read the answer, copy a few lines, or the output belongs somewhere else (a row in your account sheet, a slide), just skip the export. But where one step genuinely feeds the next, this Doc-to-Doc handoff keeps a clean trail of the work in one account folder, instead of a scroll of lost chat history.






NoteOptional: build your own Gem





You never need this for the training — every step ships a ready-made Gem. But once you have a prompt you reuse for your own recurring task (a weekly summary, a standard reply), it can be worth saving it as your own Gem so you do not rebuild it each time.
To build one: open the Gems list and choose New Gem. Give it a Name, an optional short Description, and in the Instructions write what it should do — the Role · Task · Context · Format · Check guide works here too. Under Knowledge you can add a few reference files (a price list, a product factsheet) so it always has them to hand, and the pane on the right previews the Gem as you go. Save it, and it appears under My Gems, ready to reuse like any shared Gem.






Note📸 Screenshot



The New Gem editor: give it a Name, write its Instructions, and add reference files under Knowledge. The pane on the right previews the Gem as you build it.



Gemini’s New Gem editor showing the Name, Description, Instructions, and Knowledge fields, with a live preview pane on the right.




One line stays clear: for the steps in this training, use the prepared Gems you were given. Building your own is for your personal, repeating tasks on top of that.

title: 21  Deep Research
text:
21.1 What it is
Several of your Gems do their best work by going out and researching before they answer — gathering current facts from the sources you point them at and citing them. The mode that does that is Deep Research. You can switch it on yourself in any Gemini chat, and you also meet it already switched on inside the Gems whose job is to find things out. This page explains what Deep Research is, how to choose where it looks, and the rhythm of using it well — including what a Recipe Card means when it says “Deep Research is on”.
A normal Gemini chat answers from what the model already knows, in seconds. Deep Research does something different: it goes and researches. It breaks your request into a short plan, runs many searches across the sources you point it at — the public web, or your own Gmail and Drive — reads the pages it finds, and writes them up into one long, structured report — with inline citations you can click to reach the original source.
The closest everyday picture is handing a brief to a research analyst. You do not get an instant verbal answer; you get a sourced memo back a little later, organised into sections, with its references attached so you can check the parts that matter. That trade — slower, but broad and traceable — is the whole point of the mode.

title: 21  Deep Research
text:
21.2 Where you meet it
You can run Deep Research yourself from a blank Gemini chat — switch on the mode and pick your sources (see below). In this training, though, you most often meet it as the engine inside several prepared Gems — the ones whose job is to gather current facts:

the Account-Briefing Gem in Research & Qualification, which turns a company name and website into a sourced eight-part briefing;
the Industry-Research and Lead-Finder Gems in Acquisition, which draft an industry knowledge base and a ranked, sourced shortlist of accounts.

So when a Recipe Card notes that “Deep Research is on”, it means that Gem will run this mode for you. Inside a Gem you do not have to set anything up — you give the Gem the specifics it asks for and let the mode do the finding. Understanding the rhythm below is enough.






TipRunning it yourself



You are not limited to the prepared Gems. In any Gemini chat you can run Deep Research directly:

Switch on Deep Research — the mode chip next to the message box.
Open Sources and choose where it looks — the web, your Gmail, your Drive, or Chat. Point it at the public web to research the outside world, or at your own Gmail and Drive to pull together what you already have.
Type your research question and send it.
Give it a few minutes — it comes back with the same structured, cited report.

title: 21  Deep Research
text:
21.3 How a run goes

You give it the specifics. The Gem already holds the structure and the Kärcher context; you add what only this case needs — the account and its website, or the industry and region.
It plans first. It turns your brief into a short research plan: what to search for and from which angles. This is why a good brief matters — the plan is only as aimed as the request behind it.
It searches and reads. It runs many searches across the sources it was pointed at and reads the pages. This is the part that takes minutes — a normal chat answers in seconds because it is not going anywhere; Deep Research is deliberately slower because it is actually out reading.
You can step away. It works in the background. Start the run, go prepare something else, and come back to a finished report rather than watching a spinner.
It returns a structured, cited report. Long, sectioned, with citations on the central claims. Each citation links to the page it came from.
You verify, then decide. Deep Research can be fluent and wrong. Click the sources behind the two or three claims that actually carry a decision, mark what is verified versus still to-verify, and only then act on it. The finding is the Gem’s job; the judgement is yours.







Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: a Deep Research run inside a Gem — the short research plan visible, the run in progress, and the report building up with citation chips.








NoteDeep Research vs NotebookLM





Both give you sourced answers, but they work from different places. Deep Research goes out and gathers — from the sources you pick, whether that is the live public web or your own Gmail and Drive — and brings cited sources back. NotebookLM stays strictly inside the documents you uploaded and never reaches beyond them. Use Deep Research to find and pull together what you need; use NotebookLM to work within a fixed set of documents you already have.









TipCommon stumbles




Expecting a quick answer. It is built to take minutes. For a one-line answer, a normal Gemini chat is the right tool — Deep Research is overkill.
Leaving it on the wrong source. Deep Research only looks where you send it. If the answer lives in your own Gmail or Drive, point it there in Sources; if you need the outside world, point it at the web. Working signed in to your Kärcher Workspace, your own data is fair game — share only what the task needs, then verify the result like any other output.
Trusting it unchecked. A confident, well-cited report can still be wrong. The citations are there so you click them — verify the claims that carry a decision.

title: 22  NotebookLM
text:
22.1 Open the notebook
The toolkit page gave you the mental model: NotebookLM is the expert who has read exactly the sources you gave it, and shows its source for every answer. This page is the hands-on half. In this training you mostly open a notebook that was shared with you and ask it questions — you do not have to build one to get value on day one.
NotebookLM lives at notebooklm.google.com, signed in with your Kärcher Workspace account. There is one shared notebook for the whole training — every source you need across all the steps is already loaded into it. The Recipe Cards link straight to it; clicking opens the notebook with its sources in place, listed down the left.

title: 22  NotebookLM
text:
Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: an open notebook — sources panel on the left, the chat box in the middle.

title: 22  NotebookLM
section: 22.2 Ask a question in plain words
text:
22.2 Ask a question in plain words
You do not need special syntax. Type the question the way you would ask a well-read colleague, and send it.

“What does the survey say about reliability concerns?”
“Summarise the key points on price objections from these sources.”
“Pull the main needs for this account into a short table.”

Because the answer is built only from the loaded sources, it will not drift off and invent facts the way a blank chat can. If the sources do not cover something, NotebookLM tells you so rather than guessing.






Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: an answer in the chat with numbered citation chips visible in the text.

title: 22  NotebookLM
section: 22.3 Click the source to check it
text:
22.3 Click the source to check it
This is the move that makes NotebookLM trustworthy. Each answer carries small numbered citations. Click one and the source panel jumps to the exact passage the claim came from, so you can confirm it with your own eyes before you use it. That traceability is the whole point: you get answers you can stand behind, not confident guesses.

title: 22  NotebookLM
text:
22.4 More than answers
From the same sources, the Studio panel on the right can do more than reply in chat. Ask it — or use the panel — to summarise the sources or draft a briefing, each grounded in what was uploaded with the sources still one click away. It also turns those sources into a data table — the Studio output this training builds on directly, so it gets its own walkthrough next.

title: 22  NotebookLM
section: 22.5 Build a table from your sources
text:
22.5 Build a table from your sources
This is the Studio move you reach for first in the cycle: turning a pile of documents into one clean table you can work from. It is exactly how the Pipeline Analysis chapter assembles an account sheet out of old proposals, notes and exports — but the same three steps build any table you need.
Open Studio → Data. NotebookLM fills the table from your sources, with a citation behind every cell and a blank where the documents are silent.






Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: the Studio panel on the right with Data open and the empty “Customize Data Table” box.


1. Say what the table should hold. In the Customize Data Table box, name the columns and what one row stands for — in plain words, the way you would brief a colleague.

Build one table of my accounts with these columns: account, segment, stage, value, last-contact date, next step. One row per account, take every value only from the sources, and leave a cell blank where the documents do not say.







Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: the “Customize Data Table” box with the prompt typed in, ready to generate.


2. Check it like any other answer. The table comes back grounded in the sources, a citation in each cell. Click a cell’s source to confirm it — the same trust move as in chat. A blank is a feature: it means the documents did not say, so you fill that gap yourself instead of trusting a guess.
3. Export to a Google Sheet. When it looks right, send it straight to Google Sheets in your Kärcher Workspace. From there it is your own working document — the cockpit the pipeline recipe runs on.






Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: the finished table with citation chips in the cells and the “Export to Google Sheets” button.








TipCommon stumbles




Treating it like Gemini. NotebookLM will not draft from general knowledge; it answers from the loaded sources only. That limit is the feature.
Skipping the citation check. The number is there so you click it. For anything customer-facing, verify the passage before you rely on it.
Asking beyond the sources. If the notebook does not contain the answer, add the right source or switch to a Gem — do not push NotebookLM to guess.

title: 23  Workspace Studio
text:
23.1 The shape of a flow
This is the one tool you will not touch on day one. Workspace Studio sets up a standing routine that runs on a trigger — “when an email from X about Y arrives, do Z” — so a repeatable task happens without you starting it by hand. You meet it for real at the end of the cycle, in the action plan, once the manual steps it replaces are already a habit. This page is here for that moment: the shape of a flow and the few clicks to set one up.
Before any clicks, hold the three parts in mind — they are all a flow is:

title: 23  Workspace Studio
text:
The trigger — the incoming pattern that starts it: an email from a specific contact, or about a specific subject.
The action — the repeatable step you would otherwise do yourself: summarise the message, draft a standard reply, file it to the right place.
The approval — the result waits for your decision rather than going out on its own. The human check never leaves you.

title: 23  Workspace Studio
text:
23.2 Set up your first flow

Open Workspace Studio from your Kärcher Workspace.
Describe the whole flow in one prompt in Describe a task for Gemini — for example, “when a new email arrives from a customer about a quote, summarise it and draft a reply using my standard structure, and wait for my approval before anything is sent.” Then click Create.
Gemini builds the flow’s steps from your description — review them, and edit, reorder, or add steps as needed.
Make sure the result waits for your approval before it sends, never sending automatically.
Test it on one real example, then turn it on.







Note📸 Screenshot



Add a screenshot here: a simple flow with the trigger, the action, and the “wait for approval” setting visible.

title: 23  Workspace Studio
section: 23.3 One rule before you automate anything
text:
23.3 One rule before you automate anything
Standing automation comes after the habit, not before it. First make the manual workflow clean and reliable by hand, for a few weeks. Only once a task has proven itself genuinely repetitive and stable does it earn a trigger. Automating a messy process just makes the mess faster — so the habit comes first, always, and the standing flow is the reward you unlock later.






TipCommon stumbles




Automating too early. If the manual version is not yet clean and repeatable, a flow just speeds up the mess. Earn it first.
Letting it send on its own. Always keep the approval step. The flow drafts; you decide.
Reaching for it for one-off mail. A single message stays in Gemini in Gmail. Workspace Studio is only for the same routine that repeats.

title: 24  A prompt that works
text:
24.1 The five-part guide
One skill sits under every AI step in this training: writing a prompt that gives you something useful back. It is learnable, and it is mostly about the two things people skip. This page gives you the guide, then shows it in action on a real request.
It helps to picture the AI model as a capable junior assistant. A new assistant does good work only when you brief them well: a vague one-liner gets a generic reply, while a clear task with the background only you have gets something you can use. The model is no different. It cannot read your mind, only your brief, so the better the task description you give it, the better the job it can do.
Most weak output does not come from missing the right magic words. It comes from a missing context and a missing check. A simple five-part guide fixes most of that. Use it as a helpful starting point, not as a form you have to complete every time:
You do not need a perfect prompt on day one. Write it, review the output, tighten the next round. The guide is a helper, not a rulebook. The value is not in filling every label, it is in making the request clear enough that the next answer is more useful than the first.
The two parts people skip are exactly the two that matter most. Context is what makes the output specific to your account rather than generic, and Check is what keeps you honest about what the model assumed versus what it knew. Leave those out and you get a fluent, confident, forgettable draft.

title: 24  A prompt that works
text:
Role — the perspective the AI should write from.
Task — the one concrete output you want.
Context — the facts only you know, which the model cannot, so you supply them.
Format — the length, structure, and tone of the output.
Check — an instruction to list its assumptions, so you know what to verify.

title: 24  A prompt that works
text:
24.2 A prompt example
The five parts above are just labels; what matters is what you write under each one. The clearest way to see that is to watch the same request go from weak to strong, with every part filled in.
Take a simple, everyday case. Martin Bauer, a Kärcher sales professional based in Germany, wants to figure out where AI could actually save him time, so he asks Gemini for an overview of ways to use it in his daily sales work. Here is the prompt he might reach for first:

“How can I use AI in my sales job?”

It is a fair question, but it gives the model almost nothing to work with: no clear role, no concrete output, no context about who Martin is or what his week looks like, no format, and no check at the end. So Gemini returns a generic, tool-brochure list that could apply to anyone in any industry, and Martin still has to do the real thinking himself.
Here is the same request rebuilt with the prompt guide:






TipThe same request, rebuilt with the prompt guide



Role: You are an AI-productivity coach for B2B field sales who knows which everyday sales tasks AI genuinely helps with and which it does not.
Task: Give me a short, prioritized overview of concrete use cases for using Gemini in my daily sales work, so I know where it is worth starting.
Context: I am Martin Bauer, a Kärcher Professional sales professional based in Germany. I sell professional cleaning equipment and services to B2B accounts (industry, logistics, building services). My week is mostly account research, preparing for customer meetings, writing follow-up emails, and drafting proposals. I am new to AI and use Gemini in our Kärcher Workspace.
Format: A compact table, one row per use case — task · how Gemini helps · what to watch out for — about eight rows, ordered by how much everyday time each one saves. Plain business English.
Check: List the assumptions you made about my role and my tasks that I should verify, and flag any use case where AI is likely to mislead me if I do not check its output.


Read the two side by side and the jump is obvious. The stronger version gives the AI a useful coaching perspective, the exact output Martin wants, the context only he knows (his job, his market, his weekly tasks), a usable format, and a check, so what comes back is a prioritized shortlist he can act on rather than a generic brochure.






NoteWhat the research says





Optional background, not a workshop step. It is here for anyone who wants the evidence behind the approach this page takes.
A structured prompt beats a vague one, and it is a learnable technique. Reusable prompt patterns, such as giving the model a persona, a clear task, a context block, and an explicit output format, map directly onto Role · Task · Context · Format · Check (White et al. 2023), and the wider field of prompt engineering is now catalogued as a discipline in its own right rather than a bag of tricks (Schulhoff et al. 2024). The takeaway is the one above: structure, not magic words, and a better second draft.

title: 25  Gmail
text:
25.1 The two everyday moves
Email is where most of a sales day actually happens, so it is the most useful place to have help. Gemini sits inside Gmail: it can draft a reply for you and boil a long, tangled thread down to what matters, right there in the inbox. None of it sends on its own — it drafts, you read and send.

title: 25  Gmail
text:
Draft a reply. Open a message, choose Help me write, and say what you want: “Draft a short, friendly reply confirming the meeting on Thursday and asking for the attendee list.” You get a first version to adjust.
Summarise a long thread. Open the Gemini side panel on a busy thread and ask “Summarise this thread: what has been decided, what is still open, and who owes what.” — far faster than scrolling twenty replies before a call.

title: 25  Gmail
text:
25.2 Copy and try






TipDraft a reply



Draft a brief, friendly reply to this email: confirm I can join Thursday, and ask them to send the attendee list and the current quote.








TipSummarise a long thread



Summarise this email thread in five bullets: what has been decided, what is still open, and who owes the next step.

title: 25  Gmail
text:
25.3 The one habit
The draft is a starting point, not a sent email. Read it once: does it match what actually happened, is the tone right, did it invent a detail you did not give it? Fix, then send. That check is the whole difference between fast and careless.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 26  Google Meet
text:
26.1 How it works
The hardest part of a good sales call is paying full attention and capturing what was said. Google Meet can take that second job off you: switch on “Take notes for me” and Gemini writes the notes, the summary, and the action items while you stay in the conversation. You get a tidy document afterwards instead of half-remembered scribbles.
It can also produce a transcript and, in many setups, a recording — but the notes-and-summary document is the part you will use every day.

title: 26  Google Meet
text:
Start or join a Google Meet call.
Open the activities/tools menu and turn on “Take notes for me” (everyone is notified it is on).
Run the meeting normally — no typing.
After the call, the notes, summary, and suggested next steps land in a Google Doc, shared with the organiser.

title: 26  Google Meet
text:
26.2 The one habit
AI notes are a faithful record of what was said, including anything said loosely or wrongly in the room. Before you treat them as account truth, skim and fix: correct a misheard figure, delete an aside, and make sure the agreed next step is actually the one you agreed.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 27  Google Docs
text:
27.1 The four everyday moves
Most of your written work already lives in Google Docs — visit notes, account briefings, the documents you attach to a Gem. The useful news is that Gemini sits inside Docs: the same assistant you met in the toolkit is one click away in a side panel, so you can draft, shorten, or summarise without leaving the page. This chapter is just the handful of everyday moves worth knowing.
You do not need a special prompt format here — you ask in plain words, the way you would ask a colleague. These four cover almost everything:

title: 27  Google Docs
text:
Draft from a brief. Give it the gist and let it write the first version. “Write a short visit summary from these notes, in three paragraphs: what we discussed, what they need, and the agreed next step.”
Rewrite what you have. Select the text, then ask for a change. “Make this shorter and more direct.” · “Rewrite this in a warmer tone.” · “Turn these bullet points into two clean paragraphs.”
Summarise a long document. Open the side panel in any Doc and ask “Summarise this in five bullet points” or “What are the three decisions in this document?” — useful for a long account history before a call.
Turn rough notes into something usable. Paste your raw notes and ask it to structure them: “Organise these notes into a clean account briefing with headings.”

title: 27  Google Docs
text:
27.2 Copy and try
Open any Doc, click the Gemini side panel, and try one of these — then edit the result so it sounds like you:






TipDraft a clean summary



From these notes, write a short visit summary in three short paragraphs: what we discussed, what the customer needs, and the agreed next step.








TipTighten a draft you already have



Make this text shorter and clearer, keep the meaning, and remove anything repetitive.

title: 27  Google Docs
text:
27.3 The one habit
