Gemini gives you a fast first draft, not a finished document. Read it once before you send or save: fix anything that does not match what actually happened, and cut what you do not need. The draft saves you the blank page — your judgment is still what makes it correct.

Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 28  Google Sheets
text:
28.1 Get the table built
A spreadsheet looks technical, but a Sheet is just a table: your information in rows and columns. For sales it is the simplest place to keep your accounts — no setup, just a tab you can open in seconds. And like Docs, Gemini sits inside it, so the things people avoid in spreadsheets — building the table, writing formulas, and making sense of the numbers — are now things you can ask for in plain words.
You do not need to know formulas to start — you need about ten rows. Three things remove the friction:

title: 28  Google Sheets
text:
Build a starter table. Ask for the structure instead of designing it. “Create a simple account tracker with columns: account, segment, stage, value, last-contact date, next step, note.” You get the headers ready to fill.
Write a formula in plain words. This is the part most people dread, and it is the easiest to hand over. “Write a formula that counts how many accounts are in the ‘Proposal’ stage.” It returns the formula and where to put it.
Tidy what is messy. “Clean up this column so the dates are all in the same format.”







TipBuild your account tracker



Create a simple account tracker with these columns: account, segment, stage, value, last-contact date, next step, note. Add three example rows so I can see the format.

title: 28  Google Sheets
section: 28.2 Then ask your data a question
text:
28.2 Then ask your data a question
This is where “data analysis” stops sounding like a job for someone else. With your data in a table, you do not calculate — you ask. Give Gemini the sheet and ask in normal words, the same way you would ask a colleague “which of these deals hasn’t moved in a month?”:

“Which accounts have had no contact in the last three weeks?”
“What is my pipeline value by segment?”
“Which deals have been stuck in the same stage the longest?”

You did not write a formula. You asked a question and got a readable answer — the same one you would have dug out by hand, in a fraction of the time. The phrase “use only what is in the sheet” keeps the answer grounded in your data instead of a confident guess.






TipAsk your data a plain question



Here is my account sheet. Which accounts have had no contact in the last three weeks and have no defined next step? Use only what is in the sheet.

title: 28  Google Sheets
text:
28.3 Bring in a ready-made Gem
Asking freely is one way to work; the other is to open a ready-made Gem right beside your table. A Gem is Gemini with fixed instructions for one recurring job (you met these on the Gems page), and a Gem built for spreadsheets reads the sheet you have open.
The first time, add the Gem to your account. Open its shared link and ask it one throwaway question to get going — “What is your purpose?” is enough. That first use is what makes it appear in your own Gems list; you only do this once per Gem.
After that, you reach it straight from any Sheet:

Click Ask Gemini (top right) to open the Gemini panel.
Open More options (the menu icon, top left of the panel).
Under Gems, click View all and pick the Gem you want.
Type your prompt — it works on the open sheet automatically, with nothing to attach or paste.

title: 28  Google Sheets
text:
28.4 AI sorts, you decide
This is the part where trust matters most, because a wrong answer sends your attention to the wrong place. Treat every answer as a hypothesis you confirm, not a verdict you accept: does it match what you know? did it flag the gaps instead of inventing values? Gemini does the reading and sorting in seconds — the judgment about what to do next stays yours.






The sales version of this — ranking your own accounts to decide where the week goes — is Working with Your Data.




Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 29  Google Slides
text:
29.1 The everyday moves
When you need a few slides — a short update, a quick internal share — Slides is where it happens, and Gemini can give you a first draft to react to instead of a blank deck.
Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 29  Google Slides
text:
Draft a starter deck. Ask for the skeleton instead of building it slide by slide: “Create a five-slide outline for a short customer update on a new cleaning-equipment offer.” You get scenes to fill and reorder.
Generate an image for a slide. Use Help me create to make a simple visual where you would otherwise hunt for stock.







TipDraft an outline



Create a 5-slide outline for a short internal update: the situation, what changed, the impact, the recommendation, and the next step.








NoteA note on AI-generated images



Keep visuals authentic and follow Kärcher’s content guidance: no AI-generated infographics or fake product shots, use real product and brand material, and label AI-generated media for internal use. A generated image is fine as a quick placeholder — not as a stand-in for the real product.








For a real customer presentation, use the prepared Presentation Gem in the Presentation chapter.

title: 30  Google Vids
text:
30.1 Three ways to start a video
Sometimes a short video lands better than another email. Google Vids is the video tool in your Workspace, and it is built for exactly that: turn a deck or a few notes into a watchable, narrated video without any editing skills. For sales that means a more personal touch — a quick update, a screen walkthrough, a recap after a call — in minutes, not an afternoon.
You do not build a video from a blank timeline. You pick the starting point closest to what you already have:

title: 30  Google Vids
text:
From a deck. Import an existing Google Slides presentation — each slide becomes a scene you can narrate. The fastest route when the content already exists.
Record yourself. Use your camera, your screen, or both — for a personal message or a quick walkthrough of an offer or document.
From a brief, with AI. Describe what you want and let Vids draft the scenes; you can point it at a Google Doc or Slides with @ so it works from your real content.

title: 30  Google Vids
text:
30.2 Typical sales moves

A short update video to a customer instead of a long email.
A screen recording that walks a contact through a quote or a document.
A recap after a meeting, so the next steps are clear and personal.







NoteA note on AI-generated media



Vids can generate images, voiceover, and clips. Keep it authentic and follow Kärcher’s content guidance — use real product and brand material, and label AI-generated media as the course explains.

title: 30  Google Vids
text:
30.3 Learn it properly
This page is just to place Google Vids in your toolkit. The hands-on skills — importing a deck, recording, adding AI voiceover, and sharing — have their own dedicated course:






TipOpen the Google Vids course



Google Vids Training ↗ — the full step-by-step course. The tool itself lives at vids.google.com ↗.



Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 31  Pipeline Analysis
text:
31.1 Read three signals, not the whole sheet
Here is the whole sales cycle you are about to learn. This chapter sits before it: the nine steps each work a single account, but first you decide which accounts deserve your week at all.
The foundations named the three jobs that eat your week, and pipeline and data analysis was the biggest — so this chapter builds the skill for it, before you walk a single account through the cycle.
The skill is general: you put your own data in front of AI, ask a plain question in normal words, and read the answer with judgment. The most valuable place to use it is your own book of accounts, where every Monday the real question is not “how do I work one deal” but “which of my deals get my time this week?”
NotebookLM is the move when your accounts are scattered across many files — the NotebookLM page walks through building the table step by step, with screenshots. If you keep less, start simpler:
Keep the sheet in your Kärcher Workspace — it is your own account data and an approved place to hold it — and keep two fields honest above all: the last-contact date and the next step. Those two are what make the ranking worth trusting.
With your accounts in a sheet and the Gem in your list, here is the weekly move it powers:
You do not need a scoring model to prioritise. Three plain signals do most of the work, and the Gem reads them for you so they stop hiding in the middle of a long list:
The accounts that should worry you are the ones with no recent contact and no next step. They are not lost. They are forgotten, and forgotten accounts leak revenue silently — no drama, just a deal that slowly cools while your attention is elsewhere. Surfacing those is half the value of the whole exercise.
One worry about AI here runs as deep as the time the job costs today: can you trust the numbers it gives back? The answer is the quality gate you use everywhere — AI does the sorting and surfacing in seconds, you check the ranking against the sheet, and you decide where the week goes. The Gem is built to stay explainable for exactly that reason.

title: 31  Pipeline Analysis
text:
Stage and momentum — where the deal sits, and whether it is actually moving or has been parked in the same stage for weeks.
Last-contact date — the single most useful field you keep. A long gap is the clearest early sign that an account is going quiet.
Next step — is there a defined, owned next action, or none at all?

title: 31  Pipeline Analysis
text:
31.2 The Pipeline Priorities Gem
The Pipeline Priorities Gem reads your account Sheet, ranks where the week should go, and flags the stalled and no-next-step accounts. Ask it a specific question instead — your mix by segment, which deals slipped, where the whitespace is — and it answers from the same sheet. Everything it returns is explainable, so you can check it.
Use it right inside your account Sheet, where your data already lives: open the Gemini panel, pick the Gem under More options → Gems → View all, and type your prompt — nothing to attach or paste. (New to opening a Gem inside a Sheet? Google Sheets walks through the one-time setup.)
Prompts you can copy and try. You supply your own account Sheet; the ranking, the flags, and the answers are what the Gem produces. Run one, then try a variation.
Start with the standard weekly move:






TipCopy into the Pipeline Priorities Gem



Here is my account Sheet. Rank my open accounts by where my time pays off this week, and flag the ones with no recent contact and no next step. Use only what is in the sheet.


When the week is short, ask only for the accounts that have gone quiet:






TipCopy into the Pipeline Priorities Gem



From my account Sheet, list only the accounts with no contact in the last three weeks and no defined next step. For each, give one line on why it matters and the next action. Use only what is in the sheet.


Ask a plain question of the same data when you want a read rather than a ranking:






TipCopy into the Pipeline Priorities Gem



Using my account Sheet, what is my open pipeline value by segment, and which two segments am I thinnest in? Answer only from the sheet and name the figures you used.


Or ask for a short top list when you only want the few deals to push first:






TipCopy into the Pipeline Priorities Gem



From my account Sheet, give me the top five accounts to focus on this week, one line of reasoning each, plus the accounts I am about to let go cold. Use only what is in the sheet.


The full prompt it carries:






NotePipeline Priorities Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a personal pipeline and sales-data analyst for a Kärcher Professional salesperson.
You reason over the salesperson's own book of accounts, keep every recommendation explainable so they
can sanity-check it against the sheet, and leave the call about where to spend the week to the
salesperson.

Task: When the salesperson shares their own account list (one row per account or deal, with columns
such as account, stage, deal size or potential, last-contact date, next step, note) or asks a
question about it:
  1. Read where each account sits: its stage, how long since the last contact, and whether a next
     step is defined or missing.
  2. By default, rank the open accounts by where the salesperson's time pays off this week — the deals
     close to a decision, and the ones quietly stalling — and flag two things plainly: accounts with
     no recent contact and accounts with no defined next step.
  3. If the salesperson asks a specific question of the data instead (mix by segment, which deals
     slipped, totals, where the whitespace is), answer that from the sheet.
  Ask only when a missing fact would change the answer; otherwise work from the rest and name the gap.
  If no list or data is given, ask for it rather than inventing accounts or figures to fill it.

Context (what you already know): The two most telling fields in an account list are usually the
last-contact date and whether a next step exists — an account with no recent contact and no defined
next step is not lost, it is forgotten, and it leaks revenue quietly. The ranking you produce is a
hypothesis for the salesperson to confirm, not a verdict.

Format: For the weekly priority job, return a short ranked shortlist the salesperson can act on: each
account with one line on why it ranks where it does and the single next action, with the stalled and
the no-next-step accounts grouped so they stand out. For a data question, give a short, direct answer
and name the figures you used. Return only that, no preamble. Respond in the same language the
salesperson writes in.
Example (illustrative — adapt to the real sheet, don't copy):
  This week, in order:
  1. NorthStar Facility Services — pilot at decision stage, contacted 4 days ago. Next: confirm the
     service-start owner so the proposal can go out.
  2. Delta Logistics — proposal sent, value high, but no contact in 26 days. Next: a short check-in
     before it goes cold.
  Stalled / no next step: Harbor Clean (no contact in 40 days, no next step), Vientro (stage unchanged
  6 weeks). Flagged for you to decide, not dropped.

Check:
- Work only from the data the salesperson provides; when a field is missing, name what you would need
  instead of inferring it, and keep each ranking explainable so they can sanity-check the reasoning
  rather than trust a black box.
- The salesperson decides where the week goes; you prepare the priorities and surface what is easy to
  miss.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent accounts, numbers, dates, or account
  history that the sheet does not contain.

title: 31  Pipeline Analysis
text:
31.3 Try it yourself
Use a sheet you actually keep — this only pays off when it is your real book. No sheet yet? Build one first with the recipe card and the start options at the top of this chapter.


Build the shortlist. Open your account Sheet (or the example sheet ↗) and launch the Pipeline Priorities Gem ↗ from the Gemini panel (Ask Gemini → More options → Gems → View all). Ask where the week should go, then read the ranked shortlist and the flagged accounts.
Check before you trust. Does the ranking match what you know? Did the Gem flag missing fields rather than fill them in? If a top account is ranked on a hopeful assumption the sheet does not support, that is a Revise, not a Go.
Ask one data question. Run one plain question of the same sheet — your mix by segment, or which deals have stalled — and confirm the figures against the sheet.
Act on one line. Take the single account the Gem surfaced that you had quietly forgotten, and do its one next step this week. That is the whole return on the routine.


As you debrief, ask the two questions that matter most:

Was the ranking explainable — could you trace each one to the sheet — and did it surface an account you would have missed?
This was real customer data in your Kärcher Workspace, so analyzing it directly is fine; did you still verify before you let it steer your week?







Leading a team? The same skill scales from your own book up to steering a whole team’s pipeline, forecast, and coaching — see the Leadership Track.

title: 31  Pipeline Analysis
text:
31.4 In summary
Pipeline and data analysis is the job that eats the most time in sales, and it is a foundational skill you build before the cycle begins. It starts one step earlier than the analysis itself: getting your accounts into a single Sheet, which NotebookLM can assemble from documents you already have. Then you point the Pipeline Priorities Gem at that sheet and ask, in plain words, where your week should go — it reads three simple signals (stage and momentum, last contact, next step) and hands back a short ranked shortlist with the forgotten accounts flagged. AI does the sorting and surfacing; the judgment about where the week goes stays firmly with you.
Next, the sales cycle begins: acquisition starts filling that pipeline with new leads worth your time.






NoteWhat the research says





The moves in this chapter rest on the same research base as lead prioritization, read down to the scale of a single salesperson’s book.
Prioritizing accounts is a learnable, supportable discipline. Model-supported prospect and lead prioritization has a long track record in B2B sales (D’Haen & Van den Poel 2013), and the principle “profile before you score” — understand the account before you rank it — applies to your own open book just as it does to one fresh lead (Wu et al. 2026). The Gem does not replace that judgment; it does the sorting so you can apply it faster.
The recommendation has to be explainable. A ranking is only useful if you can see why an account sits where it does and correct it when the data is wrong; explainable recommendations are what make a salesperson actually trust and use them (Bohanec et al. 2017). That is why the Gem is built to show its reasoning against the sheet rather than hand back a black-box score.
Differentiating attention is key account management in miniature. Homburg, Workman & Jensen (2002) show that firms must treat their most important accounts differently and more intensively than the rest, and that how effort is concentrated drives real performance differences. Deciding which of your own accounts get your scarce time this week — instead of treating all of them the same — is exactly that idea, scaled down to one seller and a simple Sheet.






ImportantWhat the evidence does — and does not — prove



These are mechanisms, not a study of an AI pipeline-triage routine. D’Haen & Van den Poel (2013) and Wu et al. (2026) are about model-supported prioritization in general; Bohanec et al. (2017) is about explainability and trust; Homburg et al. (2002) is about key account management at the firm level. None is a trial of “ask a Gem to rank your weekly book.” The ranking stays a human judgment: the AI sorts and surfaces, you verify against the sheet and decide.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 32  Acquisition
text:
32.1 Social selling is positioning, not pitching
On the map above, this is the customer’s Awareness stage: most have not even said “we need to do something” yet. Acquisition sits at the very front of the funnel, before a lead even exists, and this step is the work that creates one.
There are two roads to that lead, and this chapter covers both:
Most reps use both, and both end the same way, with a named account to research in the next chapter.
It is easy to hear “social selling” and picture cold outreach moved onto a new channel. That is the wrong picture. Cold outreach interrupts a stranger, leads with the product, and asks for their time before it has given them anything. Social selling flips the posture: you earn attention first, you share useful industry insight rather than a sales message, and you give value before you ask for anything. Same channel, very different stance.
Long before any conversation, a facility-services decision-maker is reading, scrolling, and noticing who seems to understand their world. People buy from experts they already trust, and when you are that visible expert the first contact often runs the other way: the customer reaches out to you. Social selling is simply making sure that when your customers look, your useful insight is there.

title: 32  Acquisition
text:
Cold pitch
Expert positioning




Interrupts a stranger
Earns attention first


Talks about the product
Shares useful industry insight


Asks before it gives
Gives, then gets asked

title: 32  Acquisition
section: 32.2 One knowledge base, then a stream of posts
text:
32.2 One knowledge base, then a stream of posts
Here is what makes social selling sustainable instead of a treadmill. The mistake people make is posting random thoughts that disappear within a day. The fix is to anchor everything to one substantial research base, then mine it for short posts over time.

Knowledge base — one industry briefing, the substance, kept for your own use.
Posts — short, frequent, each one shares an insight and invites a conversation.
Effect — visibility now, credibility that lasts.

The briefing is not a product brochure, it is your expert knowledge base: it names a real challenge the industry recognizes, describes what good looks like, and only then brings in the Kärcher angle. For a BSC (Building Service Contractor) briefing, that challenge might be the chronic labor shortage and turnover that make consistent cleaning quality hard to guarantee across sites; “what good looks like” is dependable output that does not hinge on always having enough trained hands; and the Kärcher angle, brought in last, is equipment and service that cut manual effort and keep results standardized. You keep the briefing for yourself; what reaches your audience are the short posts you draw from it. One strong briefing per target industry anchors months of posting.

title: 32  Acquisition
text:
32.3 Twelve target industries
The briefing is always written for one specific target industry, never for everyone at once, because the language and the pains differ from one to the next. Kärcher addresses twelve: Agriculture, BSC (Building Service Contractors), Construction, Automotive, Offices, Industry, Transport, Health Care, Public Services, Retail, ReCa, and Hospitality. You build one knowledge base per industry. Our running example, NorthStar Facility Services, is a BSC account, so a BSC briefing is acquisition for exactly the kind of customer we follow through the rest of the book.

title: 32  Acquisition
section: 32.4 The two Gems: Industry-Research Gem and Post-Gem
text:
32.4 The two Gems: Industry-Research Gem and Post-Gem
The two inbound Gems work as a pair, in research-then-posts order. The Industry-Research Gem drafts your knowledge base; the Post-Gem turns it into a stream of posts. Together they give you the whole motion in about ten minutes.

32.4.1 The Industry-Research Gem drafts your knowledge base
The Industry-Research Gem (“Industry Research (Kärcher Sales)”) runs in the Gemini app and uses Deep Research to gather current, public industry evidence before it drafts. You name your industry and it drafts a structured briefing: the industry challenge, what good looks like, the Kärcher angle, and the practical takeaways. Your job is not to write from a blank page; it is to give the Gem the right industry, then review and sharpen what it produces.
Prompts you can copy and try. You never describe the industry’s problems yourself — that is what the Gem researches. You only tell it which industry, and optionally how to narrow it. Open the Gem, paste one in, run it, then try a variation. Start with the book’s case industry — BSC, narrowed to a subsegment and region:






TipCopy into the Industry-Research Gem



Research the BSC (Building Service Contractors) industry for me, focused on facility-services providers in the Benelux.


Even shorter, just name the industry and let the Gem pick a sensible scope; it states the assumptions it made up front, so you can correct them:






TipCopy into the Industry-Research Gem



Draft a briefing for the BSC (Building Service Contractors) industry.


The method is not tied to our case industry — any of the twelve works the same way. Health Care is the example the Gem itself suggests; Transport shows yet another corner:






TipCopy into the Industry-Research Gem



Research the Health Care industry for me, focused on hospitals in Germany.








TipCopy into the Industry-Research Gem



Research the Transport industry for me, focused on logistics hubs in the DACH region.


You can also attach a report you trust and have the Gem research around it, or ask for the full long-form whitepaper instead of the short briefing.






NoteIndustry-Research Gem — full prompt





Role: You are an industry research analyst and expert writer. You turn public and approved evidence
into honest, well-sourced analysis: you weigh how strong each piece of evidence is, actively look for
counter-evidence instead of optimising for a tidy consensus, name conflicts between sources, and stay
upfront about what you could not verify.

Task: When the Kärcher salesperson names a target industry, produce one internal industry briefing for it,
scoped for cleaning operations. Kärcher's twelve target industries are: Agriculture, BSC (Building
Service Contractors), Construction, Automotive, Offices, Industry, Transport, Health Care, Public
Services, Retail, ReCa, Hospitality.
  1. Settle the scope first, asking only where a missing piece would materially change the work and
     otherwise assuming and labelling it:
     - target industry (ask if none is given);
     - operational subsegment, if the industry is broad (e.g. Health Care → hospitals / clinics /
       nursing homes; Transport → rail / bus depots / logistics hubs / airports; Industry → food
       production / metalworking / warehousing);
     - geography (assume Germany/DACH when the salesperson writes in German; otherwise ask);
     - depth: a focused briefing by default; produce the full long-form whitepaper only if the
       salesperson explicitly asks for it.
  2. State a short scope note (industry, subsegment, geography, depth, key assumptions); for a full
     whitepaper also a brief research plan (search directions, source priorities, recency window,
     output structure). Then proceed — don't wait for approval unless a missing input would change the
     result; ask at most three clarifying questions in total.
  3. Gather evidence. Read attached materials first: treat public ones as citable source evidence, and
     treat Kärcher-internal or customer-specific ones as background context that shapes your questions
     only. Then use Deep Research on public sources to update, validate, and fill gaps. Prioritise
     primary and official sources — industry associations, regulators and standards bodies, statistical
     offices, peer-reviewed or analyst work, documented operator cases. Avoid vendor blogs, unsourced
     thought-leadership, and listicles unless they carry primary data. Prefer sources from the last
     ~2 years; use older ones only for fundamentals, and flag dated evidence.
  4. Actively seek the other side: contested points, failed projects, and where a popular claim is only
     anecdotal. Where sources conflict, name the conflict instead of silently choosing one.
  5. Synthesise across sources — patterns, tensions, and conclusions several independent sources
     support — through one lens: what each finding means for professional cleaning operations, not a
     general industry overview. Don't summarise source by source. Then draft from that synthesis.

Context: You work for a Kärcher Professional salesperson. Kärcher Professional supplies professional
cleaning equipment and services to B2B operations, so the lens that matters on any industry is hygiene,
safety, uptime, labour efficiency, sustainability, and operating cost. What you draft is the
salesperson's own internal reference for getting genuinely expert on one industry and seeing where
Kärcher can help — not a document they publish or send to customers — so evidence quality and honesty
matter more than polish. (Discovery, conversation prep, and the value argument are later steps with
their own Gems.)

Format: A structured internal document — by default a focused briefing of ~1,500–2,500 words; only when
the salesperson explicitly asks, a full long-form whitepaper — with these sections:
  1. Title
  2. Scope and assumptions (industry, subsegment, geography, depth)
  3. Executive summary
  4. Industry challenges and trends relevant to cleaning operations
  5. Operational cleaning-critical zones and workflows (where, when, by whom, under what constraints)
  6. What good looks like
  7. Implications for hygiene, safety, uptime, labour efficiency, sustainability, and operating cost
  8. The Kärcher angle, outcome-level only
  9. Contested points and counter-evidence
  10. Open questions and assumptions to verify
  11. Source list with links and publication dates
Use inline citations on every central claim, figure, regulation, case, and contested point; the source
list at the end is not a substitute. Tag each central claim with evidence type (official statistic /
regulation or standard / peer-reviewed study / analyst report / operator case / expert view /
inference), a confidence level (high / medium / low), and a one-line reason. Expert, plain, internal
tone: no marketing buzzwords, no pitch. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.

Check:
- Separate established fact, evidence-backed pattern, plausible inference, open assumption, and
  to-verify claim. Ground every figure in a named, dated source; when a number would help but you can't
  source it, mark it "to-verify" rather than state it. Make no recommendation the source base doesn't
  support.
- In the Kärcher angle, introduce no new claims: only map operational needs already evidenced above to
  outcome-level areas where professional cleaning may matter (efficiency, hygiene, availability,
  sustainability, safety, cost). Name no specific Kärcher models, specifications, prices, ROI, customer
  results, or competitive superiority unless an attached Kärcher-approved source supports it.
- Use only public or approved evidence in the output; the salesperson verifies key facts before relying
  on it, since Deep Research can be confidently wrong. Keep internal context internal — never reproduce
  customer names, account data, prices, contract terms, or unpublished results.
- If Deep Research is unavailable or the evidence is too thin for a sourced result, say so and draft
  only an outline rather than improvising one.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never present invented statistics, case results, or
  specifications as fact.





32.4.2 The Post-Gem writes the posts
A single briefing is not one post, it is many. Each idea inside it can become its own short post: one observation, one statistic, one practical tip, written so it stands on its own and ends with a question or an invitation. That is how you stay visible week after week without inventing new material.
The Post-Gem (“Social Posts (Kärcher Sales)”) runs as a Gemini chat. You hand it a briefing (the Google Doc you saved, added from Drive) or just an industry theme, and it drafts short posts that each share one insight and invite a conversation, plus practical posting tips: how to hook a reader, how often to post, what opener to use. Give it two or three of your own past posts and it writes in your voice; otherwise it drafts plain, human posts you edit into your own words, never brochure language, because a post that sounds like a brochure does the opposite of building trust.
Prompts you can copy and try. Here too you don’t tell the Gem what insight to write — it lifts the ideas out of your briefing. You decide how many posts, which platform, and whose voice. The standard move points it at the briefing you just saved, added from Drive rather than pasted, and is the example the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Post-Gem



Use the briefing I added from Drive as my research base. Write three short LinkedIn posts from this, each with one insight and a question.


To get your own voice, paste or attach two or three of your past posts so it matches your tone:






TipCopy into the Post-Gem



Here are three of my own past posts so you can match my tone. Then use the briefing I added from Drive and write three short LinkedIn posts, each with one insight and a question.


Selling in China? Ask for the WeChat version instead of LinkedIn:






TipCopy into the Post-Gem



Use the briefing I added from Drive. Draft a short WeChat Official Account post plus a one-to-one WeCom follow-up message, each sharing one insight and inviting a conversation.


No briefing yet? Name a theme and it drafts from general industry knowledge, flagging anything that still needs a real source. Want a fuller plan? Ask for five posts with a line of posting tips under each.






NotePost-Gem — full prompt





Role: You are an industry expert and social writer who helps a Kärcher sales professional sound credible online.
You write in the person's own voice, lead with one concrete, useful idea rather than engagement
bait or a product pitch, and treat expert positioning as a long game built on trust, not reach.

Task: When the salesperson provides a research source (for example a Gemini Deep Research run or
their own notes) or names an industry theme, produce a set of short social posts and the tips to
post them:
  1. Draft three short posts. Each lifts one useful point that stands on its own, is written in
     the salesperson's own voice (not generic marketing language), and ends with a genuine
     conversation opener — a question or an invitation to compare notes and connect.
  2. Under each post, add one line of posting tips: the hook, the cadence (how often to post), and
     the call to action.
  3. If the salesperson names the platform, adapt lightly: LinkedIn for Western B2B; for China,
     frame for WeChat (an Official Account article plus a one-to-one WeCom follow-up), where
     buyers often check a supplier's presence before agreeing to meet.
  4. After the three posts, add one short note: what the salesperson should personalize before
     posting, and which numbers, named standards, or compliance claims (if any) still need verifying.
  If nothing is attached and no theme is given, ask for a theme or a research source first; don't
  invent an industry angle to fill the gap.

Context: You support a Kärcher Professional salesperson who wants to show
up as an industry expert on social media (for example LinkedIn, or WeChat in China). The research
source is their internal knowledge base, not a published report: the posts draw their substance
from it, but never present it as a public document or offer it as a download. Voice comes from
examples — if the salesperson attaches a few of their own past posts, match that tone; if not,
write plain, first-person language and tell them to edit the drafts into their own words.
Consistency matters more than any single post, because most buyers are passive on social at any
given time.

Format: One idea per post, and genuinely short — a few short paragraphs the reader skims in under
a minute. Open with something concrete (a customer question, a field observation, a common
mistake), not an abstract industry statement. Plain, first-person language; avoid jargon and the
usual LinkedIn-AI tells — "game changer", "unlock", "leverage", "deep dive", "in today's
fast-paced world", "it's not about X, it's about Y" (in either direction) — and their equivalents in the salesperson's
language. Useful and on-brand, never salesy or hollow. Return only the posts, their tips, and the
closing note — no preamble or commentary. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.

Check:
- Build each post from the salesperson's attached source and from general, defensible industry
  knowledge; if no source is attached, reason from that general knowledge and invent nothing. End
  every post on a conversation, not an offer to send a report or whitepaper — the source is the
  salesperson's internal knowledge base, not a publishable asset.
- Cite numbers to the attached source; mark a useful-but-unsourced figure — or any named standard,
  regulation, or compliance claim (e.g. a DIN norm, a reimbursement framework) — as a visible
  "to-verify" placeholder for the salesperson to check, rather than stating it. The salesperson
  decides what is accurate and what gets posted.
- Reserve a hard stop for what breaks trust if wrong: never invent customer names, stories,
  events, or examples (use neutral framing such as "a pattern I keep seeing is…" instead); and
  name no specific product models, technical specifications (such as area per hour, decibels, or
  dimensions), or prices — concrete product detail belongs to the later sales steps and the
  product factsheet (YelloFile).

title: 32  Acquisition
section: 32.5 The outbound route: find accounts to approach
text:
32.5 The outbound route: find accounts to approach
Social selling waits for the right people to raise their hand. Sometimes you don’t want to wait: you have a market you know Kärcher should be winning in, and you want real accounts in it worth approaching. That is the outbound route. It is not cold-pitching a stranger, it is disciplined research that tells you who is worth your attention before you spend any. Either way you end up with a named account to research in Step 2.
The Lead-Finder Gem (“Lead Finder (Kärcher Sales)”) runs in the Gemini app and uses Deep Research. You name a market (an industry, and optionally a region, a subsegment, and a rough size band), and it returns a ranked, sourced shortlist of candidate accounts. For each one you get what they do, why they fit Kärcher in business-outcome terms (uptime, continuity, standardization), a buying signal if it found one, and a source so you can check. It judges fit against the same ideal customer profile the Account-Briefing Gem uses, so the shortlist already speaks your fit logic.
One discipline matters here more than anywhere. A lead finder’s worst failure is a plausible company that does not actually exist. The Gem is built to list only accounts it can ground in a real source, and to flag what it is unsure of, but you still verify each account before you act on it.
Prompts you can copy and try. You only ever tell it which market; it finds the candidates. Start with the example the Gem itself suggests, an industry and a region:






TipCopy into the Lead-Finder Gem



Find candidate accounts in the BSC (Building Service Contractors) industry in the DACH region.


Narrow it to a subsegment and a size band when you know exactly the corner you sell into:






TipCopy into the Lead-Finder Gem



Find candidate accounts in the Transport industry, focused on logistics and distribution centres in Germany with more than three sites.


Want fewer, sharper names instead of a long list? Ask for the top handful only:






TipCopy into the Lead-Finder Gem



Shortlist just the five strongest candidate accounts in the Retail industry, focused on grocery and supermarket chains in Germany, and tell me why each one first.


Already have a list to work from (an association’s member directory, your own territory notes)? Attach it and let the Gem research around it.






NoteLead-Finder Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a B2B prospecting and market-mapping analyst supporting a Kärcher salesperson. You turn
public and approved evidence into an honest, sourced shortlist of candidate accounts to approach: you
weigh how strong each fit signal is, actively flag weak or doubtful fits instead of padding the list,
list only organisations you can verify exist, and stay upfront about what you could not confirm.

Task: The salesperson names a market they want to open up (a target industry, ideally with a region,
a subsegment, and a rough size band). Produce a ranked shortlist of candidate accounts in that market
that plausibly fit Kärcher, so the salesperson knows whom to research and approach first. This is the
step before research: each shortlisted account is a starting point, not a qualified lead.
  1. Settle the scope first. Read every market through the attached ideal customer profile. If only an
     industry is given, assume a sensible scope from the language the salesperson writes in (region,
     and a size band from the attached market context) and state it, so they can correct you. Ask at
     most three clarifying questions, and only where a missing dimension would materially change the
     shortlist.
  2. State a short scope note (industry, region, subsegment, size band, key assumptions) and a brief
     search plan (where you will look, source priorities, recency window). Then proceed — don't wait
     for approval.
  3. Find candidates from current public sources. Read attached materials first: treat the ideal
     customer profile and your market context as the fit logic, and any public material (association
     member lists, industry directories, trade press, company sites, registries) as citable source
     evidence; treat Kärcher-internal or customer-specific material as background that shapes your
     search only — don't cite or leak it. Prioritise official and primary sources over listicles.
     Prefer evidence from roughly the last two years; flag older signals.
  4. Actively seek disqualifiers, not just fits: too small to support a pilot, no real cleaning need,
     cleaning fully outsourced with no influence over equipment, locked into a competitor contract,
     outside the professional segment. Drop a candidate you cannot ground, or flag it as thin.
  5. Rank by fit strength and how fresh and concrete the buying signal is. Synthesise across sources;
     don't list source by source.

Context (what you already know, so the salesperson never has to type it):
- Kärcher Professional sells professional cleaning equipment, service, and standardization, and it
  sells at Level 3: on business outcomes (uptime, continuity, hygiene and compliance, total cost of
  ownership over a machine's life), not on product specifications. Judge fit through that lens.
- A fit is a hypothesis built from public information, confirmed later in the research conversation,
  never a verdict. The shortlist prepares outbound; the salesperson verifies and decides whom to
  approach.
- Fit signals, buying triggers, and disqualifiers live in the attached ideal customer profile; the
  market-specific facts (priority segments, in-scope size, local competitors) live in the attached
  market context. If the market context is missing, reason qualitatively and state the assumption.
- There is no value hypothesis or BANT yet at this step — those take shape once a single account goes
  into the Account-Briefing Gem in the next step. Here the job is only to surface the right candidates.

Format: Return a ranked shortlist of about six to ten candidates (fewer, honestly flagged, if the
evidence is thin), each as one compact block, so the salesperson can act on it fast. By default keep
the whole list to about 600–900 words; expand only on request.
  - Scope and assumptions — industry, region, subsegment, size band, key assumptions, one-line search
    plan.
  - Then, per candidate: company (legal name + website); one line on what they do and their scale;
    why it fits (the ideal-customer-profile signals matched, in business-outcome terms, not product
    terms); buying signal or trigger if you found one (new site, tender, expansion, aging fleet,
    labour shortage), else "none found"; confidence (high / medium / low) with evidence type (official
    filing or registry / industry directory or association / trade report / news / inference) and a
    one-line reason; source link and date.
  - Close with "research these first" (the top two or three and why) and a short note on any thin or
    uncertain entries.
Use inline citations on every company, scale figure, and signal; the source list is not a substitute.
Plain, internal, analyst tone: no marketing buzzwords, no pitch. Respond in the same language the
salesperson writes in.
Example (illustrative — shows the shape of one entry, not a real account; replace with verified
companies):
  [Candidate company] (legal name, website) — operates [n] sites across [region], [what they do].
  Why fit: multi-site, cleaning is operationally critical to their SLA, likely aging fleet — points to
  uptime and standardization, not a single machine. Signal: new distribution centre announced [date].
  Confidence: medium (news + company site; multi-site is verified, fleet age is inference). Source:
  [link, date].

Check:
- Ground every candidate in a named, dated public source, and list only organisations you can verify
  exist. When a name would fit but you can't verify it, leave it out rather than guess — a short,
  real list beats a long, invented one.
- Separate verified fact, evidence-backed inference, and to-verify claim; mark fit and any scale or
  signal you couldn't confirm as a hypothesis to check in research. When a fact isn't findable, say so
  plainly instead of filling the gap.
- Judge fit against the attached ideal customer profile and market context. If no market context is
  given, reason qualitatively, state any market assumption you made (for example what size you treated
  as in-scope), and ask one targeted question only if the shortlist genuinely hinges on a market fact
  you lack. Don't open with a questionnaire.
- The salesperson verifies each account before any outreach and owns whom to approach, since Deep
  Research can be confidently wrong. If Deep Research is unavailable or the evidence is too thin for a
  sourced result, say so and return a shorter, honestly-flagged list or an outline.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never present an invented or unverifiable company,
  signal, statistic, or contact name as fact, keep prices and part numbers out (argue in business
  terms), and never reproduce confidential customer or account data in the output.



Once you have a shortlist, pick the account you want to pursue and take its name and website into the Account-Briefing Gem in Step 2, which researches and qualifies it into a briefing and a Go or No-Go.

title: 32  Acquisition
section: 32.6 Public sources, but you still check
text:
32.6 Public sources, but you still check
You work from public sources throughout, so there is little to protect: keep customer names and prices out of the posts, and keep the outbound shortlist internal research, never something you publish. The one rule that does the work is verification.






ImportantThe real guardrail here is your quality bar



The output has to read as genuine expert help, on-brand and useful, never salesy or hollow. Any claim you would post gets verified first, exactly as the foundations quality gate requires. The Gems draft fast; you make sure what goes out is worthy of your name.









Do
Stop




Public industry knowledge
No customer names or prices in public posts


Genuinely useful, on-brand
No salesy, hollow posts


Mark anything to verify
No unverified claims posted

title: 32  Acquisition
text:
32.7 Try it yourself
Run the real workflow once, end to end: draft a knowledge base, save it, and let the next Gem read it straight from Drive, exactly the way you would at your desk.

A · Practice on the case industry (BSC)
1. Draft your knowledge base. Open the Industry-Research Gem ↗ and name an industry; the Gem researches the challenges and structures the briefing for you:






TipCopy into the Industry-Research Gem



Draft a briefing for the BSC (Building Service Contractors) industry.


2. Save it to Google Docs. Export the draft into a Google Doc and name it (for example “BSC Briefing” — see the example ↗). That Doc is your knowledge base, and it is what the next Gem reads.
3. Turn it into posts, straight from Drive. Open the Post-Gem ↗, add your Google Doc from Drive instead of pasting any text, then ask:






TipCopy into the Post-Gem



Use the BSC briefing I added from Google Drive as my research base.

Draft three short LinkedIn posts that each lift one idea from it, are useful on their own, plain and
human (not a brochure), and end with a question or invitation to start a conversation. Add one line of posting tips per post.


Compare your drafts with the example posts ↗. Want them in your own voice? Give it two or three of your own posts to match your tone. No posts yet? Take the drafts as a starting point and edit them into your own words.
B · Do it for real: your industry and market
4. Run the same loop for the industry you actually sell into:






TipCopy and edit with your own industry



Draft a briefing for the [your target industry] industry.


5. Or try the outbound route. Open the Lead-Finder Gem ↗ and turn a market you sell into straight into a shortlist of accounts to approach (see the example ↗):






TipCopy and edit with your own market



Find candidate accounts in the [your target industry] industry in [your region].



Then carry the strongest name into the Account-Briefing Gem in the next chapter. As you debrief, ask the questions that matter: does the briefing read as expert help rather than a pitch? Do the posts share a genuine insight and invite a conversation? And on the outbound list, is every account real and sourced, or did any need verifying before you would act on it?

title: 32  Acquisition
text:
32.8 In summary
From acquisition you leave with two things: a briefing draft for your target industry, your knowledge base, and a posting plan, a repeatable rhythm of short posts that each invite a conversation. The point of both is consistency. A single post is forgotten by the weekend; a knowledge base plus a plan keep you visible over the weeks and months it takes for a buyer to notice you and reach out. Take the outbound route as well and you leave with one more thing: a sourced target shortlist, named accounts ready to research in Step 2.
When your posts and knowledge base do their job, attention turns into inquiries: someone comments, sends a message, or asks a question. That inbound inquiry is your first real lead, and it is exactly what enters the next step, Research & Qualification. Each account on your Lead-Finder shortlist is the same kind of entry: a name to research next.
One thing to be clear about for the value-hypothesis thread we follow through the whole training: at this point there is no value hypothesis yet. You do not yet know enough about the specific account to say why Kärcher matters to them. That first hypothesis takes shape in Step 2, once you research the lead. Acquisition’s job is to produce the lead in the first place, so that there is something concrete to research.






NoteWhat the research says





Social selling is now a well-studied discipline, and the evidence lines up with everything above. It is a distinct set of activities, sharing content, building a network, listening, and nurturing relationships, that measurably supports sales performance, not just “being on social media” (Terho et al. 2022). Crucially, it works indirectly: through the trust and customer insight it builds, not through reach alone (Itani et al. 2017), and a meta-analysis across many studies confirms the effect is real but conditional on doing it well (Ladeira et al. 2026).
Thought leadership is the engine. In a large buyer survey, around 95% of buyers are not in-market at any given moment, yet strong thought leadership makes them reconsider their options, while most of what they read is rated mediocre (Edelman & LinkedIn 2024). That is exactly why your knowledge base has to be genuinely expert, and why the quality bar stays with you.
And a dose of realism: B2B buyers are often passive on social and still lean on private channels and existing relationships (Ancillai et al. 2024). Acquisition is a long game of staying visible and useful, not a switch that produces leads on demand.






NoteLinkedIn in the West, WeChat in China



The model travels; the channel does not. In Western B2B this plays out on LinkedIn; in China the same expert-positioning and research-then-posts logic runs on WeChat (Official Accounts and content, with one-to-one follow-up in WeCom), where buyers routinely check a supplier’s presence before agreeing to meet. Effective social selling looks different in a multinational than in a purely local firm (Schmitt et al. 2024), so match the platform to your market.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
text:
33.1 From a blank page to a briefing and a decision
On the map above, the customer is entering Consideration and beginning to ask “what’s out there to help?”. Before you reach out, you work out whether this account is worth your time, and why Kärcher might matter.
A lead has landed, but right now it is only a company name and a hunch. The job of this step is to turn that raw lead into two things: a briefing you could walk into a meeting with, and an explicit decision about whether the account is worth your time at all. Out of that research comes the first answer to “why might Kärcher matter to this customer?” That answer is your first value hypothesis, the thread you carry through the rest of the cycle, and it starts as a sentence you write here.
The shift in this step is from gathering to deciding. The old way is to open a dozen tabs and hope a picture forms, and then still judge whether the account is worth it. The AI way is to ask for a sourced first briefing in minutes, so your effort goes to the two things only you can do: verifying the claims that matter, and making the call.
The tool that does the finding is Deep Research, an AI mode that searches many public sources and cites them, so the report comes back structured and traceable. You do not learn Deep Research in detail today. You learn to drive one prepared Gem that has it switched on.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
section: 33.2 The Account-Briefing Gem does the research for you
text:
33.2 The Account-Briefing Gem does the research for you
Rather than writing a long prompt from scratch every time, this step uses a prepared Gem: a saved AI expert you set up once with Kärcher context. This one is the Account-Briefing Gem, and it already holds our ideal customer profile (with the priority segments and scope set per market), what Kärcher Professional sells and how we talk about service, a briefing template, and a short BANT guide. Deep Research is on by default.
One thing about that context: part is universal, part is local. The Kärcher fit logic is built in for everyone; the market-specific facts (which segments are priority where you sell, what size of account is worth a pilot, the competitors you meet) are set once for your market by your local sales team. So if the Gem flags an assumption or asks a short question about your market, that is by design, not a gap.
The Gem is built and maintained centrally and shared with you read-only, so everyone works from the same structure. The workflow is deliberately short: open the Gem, give it the company name and its website, get a structured, sourced briefing plus a first Go/No-Go.
It returns one consistent structure every time, so nothing important gets skipped:








#
Section
What it gives you




1
Company snapshot
Size, footprint, business model


2
Sites & operations
Where cleaning, hygiene, and uptime matter


3
Pain & need hypotheses
Likely pressure on cost, downtime, standardization, safety


4
Buying signals & triggers
Recent moves, growth, tenders, timing


5
Competitive / incumbent context
Who is already supplying them


6
Kärcher fit
The equipment + service + standardization angle (the first value hypothesis)


7
Open questions
What to verify in the meeting


8
Qualification (BANT) + Go/No-Go
An explicit recommendation, with reasons



Two sections carry a flag. The Pain & need and Kärcher fit sections are hypotheses, not facts, and the Gem marks them that way. Open questions is gold: it hands you the gaps public sources could not close and turns them into questions you ask the customer live.
Prompts you can copy and try. You supply only the company and its website, and optionally the segment you sell into or a source you already trust. You never describe the account’s pains, fit, or Go/No-Go yourself — that is exactly what the Gem researches and drafts. One note on the company: the prompts here point at ISS (issworld.com), a real facility-services company, because a live research run needs a real company to research — NorthStar is fictional (more on that just below).
Start with the simplest version: a company and its website. This is the example prompt the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Account-Briefing Gem



Build an account briefing for ISS (issworld.com).


Add a one-line description of what they do, so the Gem scopes the right entity from the start:






TipCopy into the Account-Briefing Gem



Build an account briefing for ISS (issworld.com), the facility-services and contract-cleaning company, in preparation for a Kärcher Professional sales conversation.


If you already have a source you trust, attach it first (an industry report or your own account notes), then ask the Gem to use it as a starting point and research around it:






TipCopy into the Account-Briefing Gem



I've attached an industry report and my own account notes. Use them as background, then research and build an account briefing for ISS (issworld.com), in preparation for a Kärcher Professional sales conversation.


Behind every one of these prompts sits the same full instruction set the Gem carries. You normally never see it; it is shown here for reference:






NoteAccount-Briefing Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a B2B account-research and qualification analyst supporting a Kärcher 
salesperson. You turn public and approved evidence into an honest, sourced briefing: you weigh how
strong each piece of evidence is, actively look for counter-evidence instead of optimising for a
tidy story, name conflicts between sources, and stay upfront about what you could not verify.

Task: The salesperson names a company they want to approach (a name, ideally with a website).
Produce a sourced account briefing that prepares a first sales conversation, plus a first BANT
(Budget, Authority, Need, Timing) qualification and a clear Go/No-Go.
  1. Settle the scope first. If only a name is given, find the official site yourself. If the
     company name is ambiguous, state which entity you researched (legal name, country, website)
     so the salesperson can correct you. Assume the salesperson's geography from the language they
     write in; ask only where a missing fact would materially change the briefing or the Go/No-Go.
  2. State a short scope note (the entity researched, geography, key assumptions) and a brief
     research plan (where you will look, source priorities, recency window). Then proceed — don't
     wait for approval; ask at most three clarifying questions in total.
  3. Gather evidence. Read attached materials first: treat the central knowledge files (ideal
     customer profile, value & offer map, BANT guide, your market context) and any other public
     material as citable source evidence, and treat Kärcher-internal or customer-specific material
     as background context that shapes your questions only — don't cite or leak it. Then use Deep
     Research on current public sources to update, validate, and fill gaps. Prioritise official
     and primary sources — the company's own filings and site, industry bodies, regulators,
     statistical offices, reputable trade press. Avoid unsourced thought-leadership and listicles
     unless they carry primary data. Prefer sources from roughly the last two years; flag older
     evidence.
  4. Actively seek the other side: facts that weaken the fit, contested points, signals that argue
     No-Go. Where sources conflict, name the conflict instead of silently choosing one.
  5. Synthesise across sources through the Kärcher lens (below); don't summarise source by source.

Context (what you already know, so the salesperson never has to type it):
- Kärcher Professional sells professional cleaning equipment, service, and standardization, and it
  sells at Level 3: on business outcomes (uptime, continuity, total cost of ownership over a
  machine's life), not on product specifications. Read every account through that lens.
- The questions that usually decide a Kärcher account: where cleaning, hygiene, and machine uptime
  affect this customer's own operations, cost, and risk. Apply the lenses that fit the company in
  front of you; not every lens fits every business. Judge fit against the attached ideal customer
  profile and value & offer map, and qualify against the attached BANT guide.
- BANT here is a hypothesis built from public information, confirmed later in the discovery
  conversation, never a verdict.
- The briefing prepares the salesperson; they verify the key facts and own the Go/No-Go.

Format: Return the briefing in exactly this eight-part structure, so nothing important is skipped.
By default keep it to a focused briefing of about 1,000–1,500 words; expand only on request.
  1. Scope and assumptions — the entity researched (legal name, country, website), geography, key
     assumptions, and a one-line research plan.
  2. Company snapshot — size, footprint, business model.
  3. Sites & operations — where cleaning, hygiene, and uptime actually matter for this company.
  4. Pain & need hypotheses — likely pressure on cost, downtime, standardization, safety (label as
     hypotheses).
  5. Buying signals & triggers — recent moves, growth, new sites, tenders, timing.
  6. Competitive / incumbent context — who is likely already supplying them.
  7. Kärcher fit — where equipment, service, and standardization could improve a business outcome
     (uptime, continuity, total cost of ownership), in business terms, not product terms. This is
     the first value hypothesis; label it a hypothesis.
  8. Open questions + Qualification (BANT) + Go/No-Go — sharp questions to ask the customer live;
     BANT as a hypothesis; then an explicit Go or No-Go with reasons and the one or two facts that
     would flip the call.
Use inline citations on every central claim, figure, signal, and contested point; the source list is
not a substitute, so close with one (links and publication dates). Tag each central claim with
evidence type (official filing or statistic / regulation or standard / analyst or trade report /
operator case / expert view / inference), a confidence level (high / medium / low), and a one-line
reason. Plain, internal, analyst tone: no marketing buzzwords, no pitch. Respond in the same language
the salesperson writes in.

Check:
- Separate established fact, evidence-backed pattern, plausible inference, and to-verify claim,
  and mark sections 4 and 7 and the BANT read as hypotheses. Ground every figure in a named, dated
  source; mark a useful-but-unsourced, stale, ambiguous, or salesperson-supplied number
  "to-verify" rather than stating it. When a fact is not findable, say so plainly instead of
  filling the gap.
- Lead with the business outcome, not the machine: name a product only as a possible means to an
  outcome, never as the headline, and only when an attached Kärcher-approved source supports it.
- If the salesperson gave no market context, assess fit qualitatively, state any market assumption
  you made (for example what size you treated as in-scope), and ask one targeted question only if
  the Go/No-Go genuinely hinges on a market fact you lack. Don't open with a questionnaire.
- The salesperson verifies the key facts before relying on this, since Deep Research can be
  confidently wrong. If Deep Research is unavailable or the evidence is too thin for a sourced
  result, say so and draft only an outline. End on a decision and a short list of sharp open
  questions, not a tidy summary.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never present invented statistics, internal
  numbers, contracts, prices, or contact names as fact, and never reproduce confidential customer
  or account data in the output.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
section: 33.3 Kärcher fit is your first value hypothesis
text:
33.3 Kärcher fit is your first value hypothesis
This is the most important idea in the chapter. The briefing’s Kärcher fit section answers one question: why might Kärcher matter here? Given this account’s operation, where could equipment, service, and standardization actually help them, in business terms, not product terms. The answer you write there is the first value hypothesis.
Name it out loud, because this hypothesis is the thread that runs through the rest of the training. It shapes the first email in the next step, so the message speaks to a real reason rather than a generic pitch. It shapes the discovery questions in Step 5, where you finally get to test it with the customer. And once the need is confirmed, it becomes the substantiated value argument in Step 6. Right now it is only a hypothesis built from public information, which is exactly why verifying it matters so much.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
section: 33.4 See it work: ISS live, NorthStar prepared
text:
33.4 See it work: ISS live, NorthStar prepared
Here is the convention in full, so the two names never mix up. NorthStar Facility Services is the account we work across the whole training, but it is fictional, so there is nothing real to research: you cannot run Deep Research on a company that does not exist. That is why every live prompt in this chapter points at ISS (issworld.com), a real facility-services and contract-cleaning company in the same industry — the one live demonstration of what a sourced briefing looks like. ISS is only the stand-in; NorthStar stays the account we actually work.
This is the live moment of the chapter. You only name the account; the Gem carries the structure, runs Deep Research, and takes the briefing through to a BANT read and a Go/No-Go.






TipCopy into the Account-Briefing Gem



Build an account briefing for ISS (issworld.com), a facility-services and contract-cleaning
company, in preparation for a Kärcher Professional sales conversation.


Notice the speed: by hand this is an afternoon of work. We do not build on ISS, though; we take the same Gem to NorthStar. Because NorthStar is fictional, we prepared its result in advance, the same eight-part briefing run on its public website, through to a BANT read and a Go/No-Go. This is the document we work from for the rest of the step.
Two parts carry the most weight. Kärcher fit is the first value hypothesis: not a machine pitch but a service-backed standardization story around uptime, mapped onto the pilot of three BR 45/22 scrubber driers. The final qualification section ends in the call, a Go, with the one or two facts (an incumbent contract, or no pilot budget) that would flip it. Every claim is labelled verified or to-verify, so you know exactly what to check.






NoteNorthStar Account Briefing — the prepared Gem output






Simulated Account-Briefing Gem output, built with Deep Research on NorthStar’s public website only. This is the example result we work from after the live demo. Every claim is marked verified (backed by a public page) or to-verify (inferred or not findable). Sections 3 and 6 are hypotheses, not facts.

Source set








Source
Type
Main public signal




Home
Public website page
Benelux industrial and logistics focus, uptime language, pilot-to-rollout logic


Services
Public website page
Cleaning, incident / peak-window support, operator onboarding, service-ready support


Industries
Public website page
Industrial production, logistics, multi-site portfolios


Operations
Public website page
Assessment, standard work, support clarity, scale logic


About
Public website page
Regional operator, multi-site mindset, continuity framing


Contact
Public website page
Rotterdam HQ, contact routes (general, operations, tender), named contacts



Public website only. No CRM, no internal documents, no pricing.


1. Company snapshot

Company: NorthStar Facility Services Benelux (verified)
HQ: Rotterdam, Netherlands (Waalhaven Eastside) (verified)
Footprint: Netherlands, Belgium, Luxembourg (verified)
Business model: Industrial facility services and contract cleaning; a regional operator with a multi-site delivery model (verified)
Positioning: “Services for industrial pace, not generic facility volume” — service-led and uptime-oriented (verified)



2. Sites & operations
NorthStar publicly states three customer environments where downtime and inconsistent routines create immediate pressure (verified):

Industrial production — manufacturing and process sites, where downtime or uneven cleaning quality hits output and readiness.
Logistics — distribution and throughput hubs that need repeatable routines across shifts.
Multi-site portfolios — regional networks that want one operating model instead of local workarounds.

Operations run from a Rotterdam coordination hub with regional, site-level execution, organized around shift-critical operating windows (verified).


3. Pain & need hypotheses (hypotheses from public signals, not confirmed)

Mid-shift downtime — the site repeatedly stresses uptime and shift-change readiness, which suggests equipment failures mid-shift are a felt pain. (inference)
Variation across teams — heavy “standard work” language implies inconsistent local routines they want to remove. (inference)
Onboarding speed — operator ramp-up is framed as part of service quality, so slow onboarding likely costs them. (inference)
Escalation & continuity — repeated continuity and escalation language implies service gaps after launch. (inference)



4. Buying signals & triggers

Recent public updates (March 2026): expanding industrial contract coverage; a “standard-work refresh” for shift-change cleaning windows; the Rotterdam team strengthening new-site launch support (verified — public news). Together these point to active growth and appetite for more standardized routines.
Pilot-to-rollout logic is stated openly: prove one site, then scale (verified). A small first scope is realistic.
Tender route is public (a dedicated Tender Desk), so procurement will formally review scope and price (verified).



5. Competitive / incumbent context

No public signal. The site names no current cleaning-equipment supplier, fleet brand, or competitor. Who supplies them today is to verify in conversation. (not findable — do not assume)



6. Kärcher fit — first value hypothesis (hypothesis)
Given the public picture, the strongest Kärcher angle is not a machine pitch but a service-backed standardization story around uptime:

Reliable battery scrubber driers that cut mid-shift breakdowns answer the uptime language directly.
One standardized fleet plus service supports their “one operating model, not local workarounds” theme.
Operator onboarding and service continuity (a Kärcher strength) sit inside the value story, not beside it.

This maps cleanly onto the pilot of three Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack scrubber driers: prove uptime and standardization on one cluster, then scale with the rollout logic NorthStar already favors. (A hypothesis to test in discovery, not a confirmed need.)


7. Open questions (to ask the customer live)

Which site is the most credible pilot location right now?
What scrubber driers are in use today, and where exactly do they fail mid-shift?
Which pressure windows hurt most: shift changes, incidents, or high-throughput periods?
Who owns the operational decision, and who owns commercial approval?
What pilot result would justify a broader rollout?
Which service elements matter most: onboarding, response time, maintenance continuity, or escalation ownership?



8. Qualification (BANT) + Go / No-Go
BANT is a working hypothesis from public information, to confirm in the discovery conversation (Step 5), never a verdict:

Budget — to verify. No public budget signal; the visible Tender Desk implies a formal procurement process. (inference)
Authority — the public contact structure already hints at a buying center: operations (Lars Meijer, Regional Operations Director), procurement (Sophie Verbeek, Procurement Manager), site (Milan Bakker), and HSE / service (Eva van Dijk). Operational and commercial approval clearly sit with different people. (verified — public contacts)
Need — plausible and well-aligned (uptime, standardization, continuity), but inferred from public language, not confirmed. (hypothesis)
Timing — pilot-first logic and recent growth suggest openness to a small pilot now; no concrete timing is public. (inference)

Recommendation: GO (qualify further). The public fit is strong: an industrial / logistics profile, explicit uptime and standardization language, a stated pilot-to-rollout model, and a visible multi-stakeholder buying center. The account is worth real preparation time.
What would flip it to No-Go: a locked-in contract with an incumbent equipment supplier, or no budget / timing for a pilot this cycle. Both are unknown from public sources, so confirm them early.


Limits

Public website material only. It shows positioning and likely priorities, not confirmed buying intent.
It does not confirm budget, current fleet details, competitor presence, contract timing, or internal approval logic.
Everything marked hypothesis, inference, or to-verify must be checked with the customer before it drives a commitment.


Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher





You can open the same briefing on its own page to read or print it in full.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
section: 33.5 Treat it as a hypothesis, verify what matters
text:
33.5 Treat it as a hypothesis, verify what matters
Confident is not the same as verified. This is the discipline that keeps AI trustworthy in sales: a generative model can sound completely confident and still be wrong, so the Gem cites its sources and marks every claim as verified or to-verify, and your job is to use those labels.



Verified
To verify




Backed by a public source
Inferred or unsupported


Has a source link you can open
Check before the meeting


Safe to build on
Ask the customer live



So you check the two or three to-verify claims that really matter before the meeting, and turn the rest into questions for the customer. The habit is small and it is the whole game: cite, label, check what matters. No evidence, no send.
The same caution applies to the qualification. BANT asks whether there is a Budget, who has the Authority to decide, whether there is a real Need, and what the Timing is. The Gem gives a first BANT read from public information, useful for the Go or No-Go, but from the outside you only see hints.



What public info hints at
What only the conversation confirms




A plausible budget range
The real budget


Likely decision-makers
Who actually signs


A probable need and timing
The need in their own words



Those only become solid in the discovery conversation, Step 5. So BANT is a working hypothesis, never a verdict, and treating it that way keeps you from over-committing on a guess.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
section: 33.6 The point of all this: Go or No-Go
text:
33.6 The point of all this: Go or No-Go
All of this research earns its keep in one decision. The briefing ends with an explicit recommendation, and you make the real call.

Go — pursue, and carry the open questions forward into first contact and discovery.
No-Go — park it, but never silently. Write down why, because a parked account with a clear reason can be revisited when something changes.
Name the one or two things that would flip the call: the missing fact or the trigger that would turn a No-Go into a Go.

A qualification without a decision is just a nicely formatted summary. The decision is the deliverable.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
text:
33.7 Try it yourself
This is the real skill we are teaching. The prepared NorthStar briefing is your worked example: practise the judgment on it, then run it for real on your own next account.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case

Read the ready-made NorthStar briefing ↗ and find the to-verify claims: which two or three would you check before the meeting?
Decide: do you back its Go, and what one fact would flip it to a No-Go?
Say the first value hypothesis in one sentence, in business terms (uptime and standardization, not product specs).

B · Do it for real: your own account

Run the prompt below in the Account-Briefing Gem ↗ for your own next real account, check two facts against their source, write the value hypothesis, and make a genuine Go or No-Go.








TipYour turn — copy into the Account-Briefing Gem



Build an account briefing for [company + website], a [one-line description of what they do], in
preparation for a Kärcher Professional sales conversation.


The Gem gives you the structure in minutes; the judgment stays yours. The temptation is to admire a beautiful, complete-looking briefing and feel prepared, but a briefing that does not change what you do is just decoration. If your research ends in a Go/No-Go and a short list of sharp open questions, it was worth the ten minutes.
As you debrief, ask the three questions that matter: which parts are reliable and which must you verify before the meeting; was the Go/No-Go justified, and what would flip it; and where did the Gem save you the most time.






Leading a team? Qualifying one lead scales up to ranking the whole pipeline — see the Leadership Track.

title: 33  Research & Qualification
text:
33.8 In summary
Research earns its keep when it ends in a decision and a question, not a tidy summary. You now leave this step with four concrete things: a sourced eight-part NorthStar briefing, an explicit Go/No-Go with the one or two facts that would flip it, a short list of sharp open questions for the meeting, and above all the first value hypothesis, why Kärcher might matter here, the thread we follow through the rest of the training. Export the briefing into a Google Doc (for example “NorthStar Account Briefing”); that Doc is what you attach to the Buying-Center Gem next, and from here every step works the same way: read a Doc, produce a Doc.
In the next chapter, Contacts & First Contact, we take that value hypothesis, reach the right people in the buying centre, and draft a role-fit first email that wins the meeting, not the deal.






NoteWhat the research says





The instinct behind this chapter, that research and qualification are a real stage of selling and not just busywork, is exactly how the discipline has always been framed. In the classic account of the sales process, the work before the conversation, finding and preparing for the right prospect, is its own step, not an afterthought (Moncrief & Marshall 2005). What AI changes is who does the heavy lifting inside that step.
The healthiest way to think about that is collaborative intelligence: along the B2B sales funnel, the machine contributes data gathering and pattern-finding while the human contributes judgment, context, and relationship (Paschen et al. 2020).
