That is the division of labour this chapter builds in, and it matches how the field as a whole reads AI in selling, as augmentation that raises the work rather than replacing the seller’s judgment (Singh et al. 2019; Grewal et al. 2024). The same research is candid about the catch: generative tools have real limits on factual reliability and control, which is precisely why the verify habit is not optional.
That catch has a name. Generative language models hallucinate: they produce fluent, confident text that is not always faithful to any source (Ji et al. 2023). Citing sources and grounding the answer in retrieved evidence reduces the problem, but it does not remove it, so a human still checks. This is the evidence behind “confident is not the same as verified.” A related finding from sales specifically: people only trust and use a model’s output when they can see why it says what it says, so explainability, not just accuracy, is what makes an AI recommendation usable (Bohanec et al. 2017). The Gem’s cite-and-label behaviour is built for exactly that.
On qualifying and prioritizing, the research backs the “not every lead deserves your afternoon” stance. Model-supported prospect prediction has been used for years to rank B2B leads by their likely value (D’Haen & Van den Poel 2013), and more recent work sharpens the order of operations: you profile before you score, because a good priority call rests on good research first (Wu et al. 2026). That is the academic shape of this whole chapter. As for BANT, it remains widely used, around half of reps still rely on it, but practitioners are clear that in modern, multi-stakeholder buying it is a starting hypothesis, not a checklist that decides the deal, since budget and the real decision-makers often only emerge during the process (Salesforce / Gartner 2024). Holding BANT as a hypothesis is the evidence-based move, not a compromise.






NoteA market signal, not a gimmick



This is not a fringe use of AI. In B2B sales, the top use cases for generative AI are prospecting and lead generation, and the single most sought-after one is a “smart research assistant”, precisely the account-briefing job in this chapter. The estimated productivity prize across marketing and sales runs to USD 0.8–1.2 trillion, and most commercial leaders expect to use these tools routinely within a couple of years (McKinsey 2023). The skill you practise here is the one the market is already moving toward.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
34.1 You sell to people, not to a company
On the map above, you are still in the customer’s Consideration stage. Several people will shape this decision; your job is to reach the right one and earn a first conversation.
This is Step 3 of the sales cycle, and it is the point where preparation turns into contact. You arrive with two things from the research step: the NorthStar account brief and the first value hypothesis, the early answer to why Kärcher might matter to this customer.
The question now is simple and concrete: who exactly do you reach, and how do you open in a way that earns a reply? The value-hypothesis thread we follow through the whole training lands here for the first time in a real message, and it lands first with the decision-maker.
In B2B you almost never sell to one decision-maker. A purchase like a fleet of cleaning machines is shaped by several people: an operations lead, a procurement manager, the crew on the floor who run the machines. Each weighs the same offer differently, so the classic mistake is to write one generic message to the average of them all, which fits none of them. The skill you build here is to map those people before the meeting, using the raw material the research step already gave you. The goal is modest on purpose: not to close anything, but to win the first conversation.
The Buying Center is simply everyone who influences a buying decision. Three roles carry most of the weight, and each judges the offer by a different question:
Same offer, three different questions. The decision-maker thinks in outcomes; purchasing and the floor want proof. None of this is guesswork about personality — it is role logic, the same for every account you will ever work, which is exactly why a Gem can prepare it for you in minutes.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
Decision-maker — judges it by the business outcome for the operation: less downtime, a clean rollout, something that scales. Often an operations or business lead.
Purchasing — judges it by cost, scope, and risk: is the deal defendable internally?
User — runs the machine day to day, judges it by whether it makes their daily work easier or harder under pressure.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
34.2 The four people behind NorthStar
Theory is easy to nod along to and hard to use, so we make it concrete. NorthStar has four people in its Buying Center, and across the rest of the training you will work with them by name, not as abstract roles. Lars and Sophie are the primary contacts; Milan and Eva are the operational voices who matter especially in the needs analysis. Treat everything here as a working hypothesis from the briefing, not as confirmed fact.


Lars Meijer, Regional Operations Director — the operational sponsor. The decision-maker who creates forward motion, and your first serious contact. He owns operational continuity across the region, so his question is always about outcome: does this reduce downtime without adding complexity, and can it scale beyond one site? He is where the value hypothesis lands first, because it is written in his language: less disruption, more continuity. Angle: impact and outcome.



Sophie Verbeek, Procurement Manager — the commercial gatekeeper. She holds the formal sign-off and tests the deal: is the service component justified, is the scope clear, can the offer be defended internally? To her, an unexplained service line just reads as a higher price. She is not the first person you write to, but a sponsor who loves the solution still needs procurement to say yes. Angle: cost and risk.



Milan Bakker, Site Supervisor — the daily user. Closest to the machines. A unit that fails mid-shift is his problem; a machine awkward to operate slows his crew down. His question is simple: will this be reliable and easy to use under pressure? He may not sign, but his voice carries weight, because sponsor and procurement both look to the people who run the equipment. Angle: the daily work.



Eva van Dijk, HSE and Service Lead — the safe-rollout voice. Secondary here, but not unimportant. She cares about safe rollout, service response time, and training quality. She rarely champions a deal but can quietly stall one with a late safety or training concern, so you keep her in view. She returns properly in the needs analysis. Angle: safe rollout, response time, training.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
section: 34.3 What each role really cares about
text:
34.3 What each role really cares about
The relevance map is the whole point of the Buying Center. The same NorthStar pilot looks attractive for a different reason to each role, and your message has to match the reason, not the average:









Role
Person
The question they ask
What you lead with




Decision-maker
Lars Meijer
Does this reduce downtime and scale cleanly?
Impact and outcome


Purchasing
Sophie Verbeek
Is the cost defendable and the scope clear?
Cost and risk


User
Milan Bakker
Is it reliable and usable under pressure?
The daily work


Safe-rollout voice
Eva van Dijk
Are response time and training handled?
Safe rollout



This is information you already hold from the research step — you are matching role logic, not guessing at personalities.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
section: 34.4 Map the stakeholders with the Buying-Center Gem
text:
34.4 Map the stakeholders with the Buying-Center Gem
You do not build the stakeholder map by hand from a blank page. The Buying-Center Gem (“Buying-Center (Kärcher Sales)”) is a prepared Gemini expert that already knows the Buying Center logic. You hand it the account brief from the research step, and it returns a map: for each likely role, a goal, a pain, a conversation angle, and the risk if you ignore that person.

Input — the NorthStar account brief from the research step.
Output — a role-by-role map: role · goal · pain · conversation angle · risk.
Check — every entry flagged as a hypothesis to verify in the meeting; no invented named people.

You normally just open the prepared Gem and attach your account-briefing Doc; if anything it needs is missing, it asks before it maps rather than inventing it.
Prompts you can copy and try. You never describe the roles or angles yourself — that is exactly what the Gem maps. You only attach your briefing Doc and, if you like, point it at one account or ask it to focus a particular way.
The standard move is to attach your account-briefing Doc from Step 2, then ask. This is the example prompt the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Buying-Center Gem



Using the account briefing I attached, map the Buying Center for a Kärcher Professional pilot at this account.


Shortest version: attach the Doc and just ask for the map. It reads the brief and states any assumption it had to make:






TipCopy into the Buying-Center Gem



Map the Buying Center for this account.


Once you have the map, ask the Gem to turn it into an approach plan, one move per role (you can also name the account explicitly, or have it go deeper on a single role):






TipCopy into the Buying-Center Gem



Using the briefing I attached, map the Buying Center, then tell me how to approach each role: who to reach first and the one angle to open with for each.


The full prompt it carries:






NoteBuying-Center Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a B2B buying-center analyst for Kärcher Professional. You think in roles, not titles:
you read who actually shapes a purchase, what each one judges the offer by, and where a quiet
stakeholder can stall a deal. You treat every read as a hypothesis to test, not a verdict.

Task: Map the Buying Center for one account before the first contact, so the salesperson prepares for
the real people who shape the decision instead of writing to an "average" that fits no one. Work from
the account briefing the salesperson provides. If none is given, ask them to attach it; if they have no
brief yet, ask for the industry, the site setup, and what Kärcher would supply, then continue. Ask only
when a missing fact would change the map; do not invent facts to fill the gap. Then:
  1. Propose the likely Buying Center for a Kärcher Professional pilot. Cover at least the
     decision-maker (operations) and procurement, and add the site/user and HSE/service voices as
     secondary roles where the briefing supports them.
  2. For each role give five things: the role, its goal, its pain, a specific conversation angle to
     open with, and the risk if the salesperson ignores that person.
  3. Mark every entry as a hypothesis to verify in the meeting, and for each role suggest one
     "confirm-or-kill" question the salesperson could ask live to test it.

Context (what you already know): The Buying Center is everyone who influences a purchase. Role beats
title: the org chart does not reveal who really decides. Three roles carry most of the weight, and each
judges the same offer by a different question:
  - Decision-maker (often operations or a business lead) — judges by the business outcome: less
    downtime, a clean rollout, something that scales.
  - Purchasing — judges by cost, scope, and risk: can the deal be defended internally?
  - User (runs the machine day to day) — judges by whether it is reliable and easy to use under
    pressure.
  Add secondary voices where the briefing supports them, for example an HSE/service or
  safety-and-training voice that rarely champions a deal but can quietly stall one. You serve a Kärcher
  Professional salesperson preparing first contact; later steps (first-contact email, conversation prep,
  discovery) have their own Gems.

Format: Output a clean map: for each role, one block with role · goal · pain · conversation angle ·
risk, and the confirm-or-kill question on its own line under the block. Lead each role with its own
logic: outcome for the decision-maker, cost/scope/risk for purchasing, daily reliability for the user.
Return only the map, no preamble. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.
Example (illustrative — adapt to the real account, don't copy):
  Decision-maker (Operations lead)
  · Goal: fewer cleaning-related stoppages across sites
  · Pain: unplanned downtime when a machine fails mid-shift
  · Angle: open on where disruption hurts most, not on a product
  · Risk: pitch features and you never reach the outcome they own
  Confirm-or-kill: "Where does a cleaning stoppage cost you the most today?"

Check:
- Work at the level of roles, using only what the briefing supports; mark every entry as a hypothesis
  to verify live, and treat the map as a sharp starting point for preparation, not truth to act on
  blindly.
- Use a real person's name only if the briefing gives one; if it names no individuals, map the roles
  anyway and tell the salesperson to source the real names separately (a title search, a call to the
  site).
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent named real people, titles, prices, or
  internal facts that are not in the briefing.









TipNo names in the brief? Map roles, not people



Often the research turns up no individuals at all, and that is normal — it is not a gap the Gem should fill by inventing someone. You still map the roles: a mid-size operator has someone who owns uptime, someone who owns the budget, and people who run the machines, whether or not you have their names yet. The map stays a set of role hypotheses. The names come from elsewhere — a LinkedIn title search, a quick call to the site (“who looks after your cleaning equipment?”), or the first conversation itself, where you ask who else should be in the room. Write to the most likely role, and use that first contact to find the real people behind it.


When the map comes back, run the human check, because a confident-looking map is seductive. Read each entry and ask one question per person: what is the single thing I could ask in the meeting that would confirm or kill this hypothesis? If you can name that question, you are using the meeting to test your assumptions instead of defending them. Those confirm-or-kill questions also become some of your best questions in the needs analysis, two steps from now.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
section: 34.5 Now the first email: win the meeting, not the deal
text:
34.5 Now the first email: win the meeting, not the deal
With the people mapped, you write the first contact. The single most important idea is the job of that email: it is a door opener, not a proposal. Its only goal is to earn a reply and a short conversation. It is built directly on the value hypothesis from the research step, so it is relevant from the first line.
It is just as important to be clear about what the email is not. It is not a quote, it does not list specifications, and it does not ask for a decision. The substantiated value argument comes much later, after the conversation has confirmed the real need. Trying to close in the first email is the fastest way to get deleted.
A strong first email does three things and no more. Sales training calls this the opening statement, but the idea is plain, and it works for an email and a call alike:

Purpose. Who you are and why you are reaching out now.
Benefit. One pressure the person actually feels, drawn straight from the value hypothesis, connected to an outcome rather than to a product.
Check. One small next step, a short call, not a full decision.

Most weak openings fail because they skip straight to a pitch before the person has accepted that the conversation is relevant. These three jobs force you to stay short, relevant, and easy to say yes to. The structure is role-neutral in shape but role-specific in content: the Benefit line is what changes from Lars to Sophie to Milan.






TipRole decides the tone, not personality




To Lars (decision-maker) — lead with uptime and rollout, the business outcome.
To Sophie (purchasing) — lead with cost logic and scope clarity.
To Milan (user) — lead with daily reliability on the floor.

That is all role information you already have from the map. What you deliberately do not do here is type the person, reading them as dominant or steady or anything else. That live reading comes in the next chapter, where DISC works from how the person actually replies. Today the email is purely role plus the value hypothesis, and that is enough to be relevant.


The First-Contact Gem (“First Contact (Kärcher Sales)”) drafts the email with Gemini right inside Gmail, so you are not copying text between tools. You give it the stakeholder map, the specific role you are writing to, and the value hypothesis, and ask for a short email built on Purpose, Benefit, and Check. If you leave out the role or the value hypothesis, it asks for them first instead of guessing.

Source — the stakeholder map, the role you are writing to, and the value hypothesis.
Output — a subject line plus one email under about 120 words, structured as Purpose / Benefit / Check.
Rule — one real pressure, no specifications, one clear call to action.

Prompts you can copy and try. You supply three things: who you are writing to, the value hypothesis, and (helpfully) the Buying Center Map. You never write the email yourself — the Gem leads with the right pressure for that role. Start with the decision-maker, Lars, and attach the Buying Center Map so the Gem works from the role logic you already mapped:






TipCopy into the First-Contact Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, write the first-contact email to Lars Meijer, Regional Operations Director — the decision-maker. Value hypothesis: standardizing the cleaning fleet across NorthStar's sites (equipment + service) could cut avoidable downtime.


Writing to a different role? Name that person and give the pressure they actually feel. For Sophie in procurement, lead with cost and scope:






TipCopy into the First-Contact Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, write the first-contact email to Sophie Verbeek, Procurement Manager. Value hypothesis: a clear, defendable scope where the service component is justified, not just a higher price.


Already sent the first email and heard nothing? Ask for a short follow-up:






TipCopy into the First-Contact Gem



Lars Meijer hasn't replied to my first email. Write one short, friendly follow-up that adds a new angle and asks again for a 20-minute call.


You can also tell it the tone you want (warmer, more direct, or shorter).
The full prompt it carries:






NoteFirst-Contact Gem — full prompt





Role: You help a Kärcher Professional key account manager write a first-contact email that wins a
first conversation, not the deal. The email is a door opener, not a proposal: not a quote, no
product specifications, no decision asked for.

Task: When the salesperson gives you the stakeholder map, the specific role or person to write to, and
the value hypothesis from the research step:
  1. First check you have what you need to write: who the email is for (the role or person) and the
     value hypothesis (why Kärcher might matter to them). If either is missing, ask one short
     question to get it before drafting, and do not invent it. The stakeholder map and the account
     briefing are helpful context; invite the salesperson to provide them if they have them, but the role
     plus the value hypothesis is enough to draft.
  2. Draft one short email built on three jobs:
     - Purpose: who you are and why you are reaching out now.
     - Benefit: one real pressure this role actually feels, linked to an outcome (for example
       avoidable downtime across sites tied to standardized cleaning and service), not to a single
       machine.
     - Check: one small next step (for example a 20-minute call to compare where disruption is
       worst), not a decision.
  3. Match the emphasis to the role, not to a personality guess:
     - Decision-maker (e.g. operations) → uptime, continuity, a clean rollout: the business
       outcome.
     - Purchasing → cost logic and scope clarity.
     - User (site/floor) → daily reliability and easy handling.

Context (what you already know): The email is built on the value hypothesis (the early answer to
why Kärcher might matter to this customer) and on the role of the person being written to. Do not
type the person (no DISC or personality reading) — that is the next step; here the tone follows
the role only.

Format: Give a short subject line, then one short email under about 120 words in three short
paragraphs — Purpose, Benefit, Check. One real pressure, one clear next step. No product
specifications, no price, no attachment. Plain, credible, human language — something the salesperson would
actually be willing to sign and send. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.
Example (illustrative — adapt to the real role and value hypothesis, don't copy):
  Subject: 20 minutes on downtime across your sites?
  Hello Mr Meijer, I'm Martin Bauer from Kärcher Professional. [Purpose: why you are reaching out now.]
  [Benefit: one real pressure an operations lead feels — e.g. unplanned downtime when cleaning fails
  across sites — tied to standardized cleaning and service, not to a single machine.]
  [Check: would a short call to compare where disruption is worst be worth 20 minutes?]

Check: Work only from the briefing and the value hypothesis, and mark anything inferred; end by
listing the assumptions the salesperson should verify before sending. Reserve a hard stop for what would
mislead the customer: never invent customer facts, and never name a person who isn't in the briefing.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
34.6 You keep the last word
Before anything reaches a real contact, run three quick tests on the draft:

Does it name a real pressure, something this specific person actually feels, or has it drifted back into generic supplier language?
Has it stayed a door opener, or has it crept toward a pitch with specs and a near-ask for a decision?
The simplest test of all: would you, personally, be willing to sign it and send it?

If any answer is no, you revise before you send. The Gems remove the blank-page work and get you ninety percent of the way in seconds. The last ten percent, the judgment, is yours, and it is exactly where your value as a salesperson sits.

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
34.7 Try it yourself
Produce two artifacts you will reuse in the next chapters.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Map the Buying Center. Open the Buying-Center Gem ↗ and attach the NorthStar account-briefing Doc (copy the ready-made Doc ↗ to your Drive and attach the copy) — that is the whole input; the Gem carries the task. Don’t paste the brief, it is too long; the Gem reads the attached Doc. Cover at least Lars and Sophie, each with a goal, a pain, and an angle, every entry a hypothesis to verify.






TipCopy into the Buying-Center Gem



Using the account briefing I attached, map the Buying Center for a Kärcher Professional pilot at this
account.


Export the map into a Google Doc (for example “NorthStar Buying Center Map” — see the example ↗). That Doc is what you attach in step 2, and you reuse it again in the next two chapters.
2. Draft the first email. The First-Contact Gem ↗ works in Gmail. Attach the Buying Center Map Doc you just exported, name the role you are writing to and the value hypothesis, and ask for a door-opener to Lars. Notice you do not tell it the angle — you give it the role and let it lead with the right pressure. Read the draft (see the example ↗) against the three checks before you would ever send it.






TipCopy into the First-Contact Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, write the first-contact email to: Lars Meijer, Regional
Operations Director — the decision-maker.
Value hypothesis: standardizing the cleaning fleet across NorthStar's sites (equipment + service)
could cut avoidable downtime and unplanned disruption.


B · Do it for real: your own account
3. Map two stakeholders on your own next real account (attach the briefing you saved in Step 2), and write one first email to the person you would actually reach first. If the research turned up no names, map the roles anyway and write to the most likely function — that is the normal case, not a failure.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Which role is most likely to block the deal, and how would you address them?
For each map entry, what one question would confirm or kill the hypothesis?
Is the email still a door opener, or has it drifted into a pitch?

title: 34  Contacts & First Contact
text:
34.8 In summary
You now have the two things this step is built to deliver: a NorthStar stakeholder map with a goal, a pain, and an angle per role, and a role-fit first email to Lars that works toward the meeting rather than the deal, both built on the value hypothesis from research. That email opens the door.
The next chapter, Conversation Preparation, is about getting ready for the conversation itself: the mindset you walk in with, building rapport, and reading the room. That is where DISC comes in, reading the person live from how they actually replied, where Step 3 stayed purely role-based on purpose.






NoteWhat the research says





The chapter’s central claim, that you sell to people and not to a company, is one of the best-established ideas in B2B marketing. Webster and Wind (1972) modelled the organizational purchase as a group decision made in a Buying Center, with distinct roles, users, influencers, buyers, deciders, and gatekeepers, that each weigh the same offer differently. Fifty years on, Ehret, Johnston and Ritter (2024) confirm the buying center is still the signature concept of B2B, while it widens into networked “buying ecosystems”: more people, more outside influence, more digital touchpoints. The role model still holds; reality just got more crowded, which makes mapping the people before you write more important, not less (and the broader research field is mapped in the systematic review by Bilro et al. 2023).
That crowding is why a generic message fails. Consensus, not any single yes, is the real bottleneck: the research behind The Challenger Customer (Adamson et al. 2015) found that stakeholders often cannot even agree among themselves on what the problem is, and that the sellers who win equip an internal “mobilizer” to build that agreement. So the map is not academic, it is how you avoid writing to an average that persuades no one.
And a useful discipline-check: role is not the same as title. Bonoma’s classic “Major Sales: Who Really Does the Buying?” (1982/2006) showed that the org chart does not reveal who actually decides, influence and the real “power line” rarely match the formal hierarchy. That is exactly why every entry on your map stays a hypothesis to verify, never a fact to act on blindly.
When you do reach out, lead with value, not product. Terho, Haas, Eggert and Ulaga (2012) conceptualize value-based selling as orienting the whole interaction around the customer’s business outcome rather than product features, which is precisely what the Benefit line of the first email does, and why it is built on the value hypothesis rather than on a spec sheet.






NoteThe buying group is bigger than you think



Practitioner research puts hard numbers on the crowding. Gartner finds the typical B2B buying group involves roughly 6 to 10 decision-makers, working through four “buying jobs” (problem identification, solution exploration, requirements building, supplier selection); buying groups that reach consensus are about 2.5× more likely to land a high-quality, low-regret deal. The honest flip side: agreement is hard, and a large share of buying teams report real internal conflict during the decision. That is the reality your stakeholder map is built for.


A dose of realism on the email itself. Hard evidence on first-contact emails is mostly practitioner: analysing 20M+ sent messages, Woodpecker (2026) reports that short, well-targeted, genuinely personalized emails, with one or two follow-ups, earn far higher reply rates than generic blasts, though reply rates are modest in absolute terms even at their best. The lesson is not “email works magically”; it is that relevance beats volume, which is the entire point of mapping the people first. (Treat any specific figure as practitioner data to verify, not as a guarantee.)

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
text:
35.1 Good preparation changes the meeting before you speak
On the map above, the customer is still weighing options in Consideration. This is the last move before the meeting, where you prepare the human side of the conversation.
This is Step 4 of the sales cycle, and it is deliberately the shortest. You arrive with everything the earlier steps produced: the NorthStar account brief and the value hypothesis from research, the stakeholder map from the Buying Center step, and a first email already sent to Lars Meijer, who has now replied — turning a mapped role into a named person to prepare for, even if the last step started without a name.
The briefing is sharp, but it cannot do the two things the meeting depends on: getting Lars to trust you, and making you actually hear what he is worried about. AI cannot do those for you either. What it can do is get you ready. So the line that runs through this step is: AI sharpens your preparation, and the conversation stays human.
That makes this step different from the research-heavy ones before it. There is no large workflow here, just three things to prepare before you walk in, one framework to read the person live, and one Gem that gets you ready and then steps back.
Rapport is often treated as something that simply happens in the room. In practice, the quality of that connection is shaped before the meeting even starts. Three things should be in place when you walk in, and weak meetings usually break in one of them.
AI can help you prepare all three. It can pull Lars’s likely priorities and risks into one place, clarify the value logic so your conviction feels natural rather than rehearsed, and remind you what to listen for. What it cannot do is supply the confidence or the conviction itself.

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
text:
Confidence is your own state: prepared, calm, and clear on the goal for the conversation.
Conviction is being able to say why the Kärcher solution and service fit NorthStar, not just recite specifications. For Lars, that means uptime across sites, service continuity, and a low-risk pilot on the three scrubber driers.
Context is knowing what actually matters to the person in front of you.

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
section: 35.2 What AI prepares, what stays human
text:
35.2 What AI prepares, what stays human
The boundary is worth making concrete: everything else in this step lives on the AI side of it, and you have to cross back to the human side to use it.







AI can prepare
Only you can do, live




A fitting opening for Lars
Listen for what actually changes


A short list of what to listen for
Adjust when priorities shift mid-conversation


His context and likely priorities
Decide in the moment: probe, pause, or move on









ImportantPrepared awareness, never scripted warmth



This is the central guardrail of the chapter. Use AI for genuine awareness of Lars’s pressures, never to script warmth you do not feel. Prepared awareness means walking in with real understanding of his likely priorities, so your early questions are relevant. Scripted warmth is the opposite: AI-generated empathy lines, rehearsed curiosity, friendliness with no real listening behind it. Customers sense that very quickly, and it weakens trust instead of building it. The openers you prepare are starting points for a real conversation, not lines to perform.

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
section: 35.3 DISC: a way to read the person live
text:
35.3 DISC: a way to read the person live
The one new idea in this step is DISC, a simple and well-known model of four communication styles. We do not use it to put people in boxes. We use it as an observation framework: a way to read the person in front of you and adapt how you open, how fast you go, and how much detail you give.
The four types fall into two rough groups.








Type
Recognize
Adapt




Dominant
brief, decisive, results-first
lead with the outcome, stay concise


Influential
warm, talkative, big-picture
allow some rapport, paint the vision


Steady
calm, careful, relationship-led
slow down, reassure, take off the pressure


Conscientious
precise, detailed, asks for proof
bring facts, figures, and references



Dominant and Influential types tend to move faster; Steady and Conscientious types prefer a calmer pace and more detail. You get a first hint from how the person replied to your first email: a short, decisive reply points toward Dominant, a warm and expansive one toward Influential, a long and question-rich one toward Conscientious. If Sophie in procurement later replies in that careful, detail-seeking way, that would point toward the Conscientious end, a hypothesis you would still confirm in the room.






WarningTwo guardrails that make DISC safe to use




The type is always a working hypothesis, never a fixed label. You keep checking and adjusting it as the conversation unfolds.
DISC is read live, in the real conversation, not AI-profiled from someone’s public footprint. We never let a tool scan a person online and declare a personality type; that would be both unreliable and uncomfortable. The first read you prepare, including the one the Gem offers from the email reply, is only a starting point you confirm or correct in the first couple of minutes.



This read does real work later. It steers the flow of the needs analysis in the next step: the pace you set, the level of detail you bring, and the tone you take are all shaped by the type you are reading.

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
section: 35.4 The Rapport Gem: prepare the human moment
text:
35.4 The Rapport Gem: prepare the human moment
The Gem for this step is the Rapport Gem (“Rapport (Kärcher Sales)”). You open it, attach the Buying Center Map Doc you built earlier, and for each key stakeholder it returns their likely communication style, one or two genuine openers, a short list of what to listen for, a first DISC type hypothesis read from how they replied to your first email, and a mindset reminder. It deliberately ends with a hand-off line, so the tool stops where the conversation begins.

Input — the Buying Center Map Doc from the contacts step, plus how each person replied to the first email.
Output — per stakeholder: likely style, one or two genuine openers, what to listen for, a first DISC hypothesis labelled as a hypothesis, and a mindset reminder.
Check — openers genuine rather than manipulative, the type kept as a hypothesis rather than a box, and the hand-off line left intact.

Prompts you can copy and try. You attach your Buying Center Map Doc and describe how each person replied to your first email; you never name their type or the openers — the DISC hypothesis and the openers are what the Gem produces. You decide whom to prepare for, and what to tell it about the replies you have.
Start with the version the Gem itself suggests. Attach your Buying Center Map first, then describe how Lars replied to your first email:






TipCopy into the Rapport Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, prepare the human side of my meeting with Lars Meijer, Regional Operations Director. How he replied to the first email: within the hour, three short lines, no small talk — he asked only what a pilot on the three scrubber driers would involve and how quickly it could start.


When more than one stakeholder has replied, describe each reply and let the Gem prepare them all in one pass (or name just one and say the others are still silent, so it offers no type for the rest):






TipCopy into the Rapport Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, prepare me for everyone who has replied so far. Lars Meijer replied within the hour in three short lines, asking only about a pilot on the three scrubber driers. Sophie Verbeek in procurement replied two days later with a long list of questions about pricing, references, and contract terms.


Heading into a specific kind of meeting? Name the setting so the openers and what to listen for fit it:






TipCopy into the Rapport Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, prepare me for a first on-site visit with Lars Meijer at NorthStar Facility Services to walk through a pilot on three Kärcher BR 45/22 scrubber driers. He replied to my first email within the hour, in three short lines, asking only what the pilot would involve and how fast it could start.


Behind every one of these prompts sits the same full instruction set the Gem carries. You normally never see it; it is shown here for reference:






NoteRapport Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a conversation-preparation coach for Kärcher Professional sales meetings. You get the
salesperson ready to connect with each stakeholder as a person, then step back: you read likely style
from real signals, never fake warmth, and never hand over a script for the conversation itself.

Task: Prepare the salesperson, per stakeholder, for a meeting. Work from the Buying Center map and a
short note on how one or more of those people replied to a first-contact email. If the map or the reply
notes are missing or thin, ask for them rather than inventing them. For each key stakeholder, give five
things:
  1. their likely communication style, in plain words;
  2. one or two genuine rapport openers the salesperson could actually use — real and specific, never a
     manipulative line;
  3. a short list of what to listen for in the first minutes;
  4. a first DISC hypothesis. DISC (Dominant, Influential, Steady, Conscientious) is an observation lens
     for how a person tends to communicate. Read it ONLY from how they replied to the first email (brief
     or detailed, decisive or weighing), label it clearly as a hypothesis to confirm live, and if a
     person did not reply, offer no type for them rather than guessing from their role;
  5. a one-line mindset reminder for the salesperson.

Context (what you already know): A Buying Center map lists the stakeholders in one B2B account, each
with a role (decision-maker, purchasing, user, and so on), a goal, and a pain; use the role and pain to
shape the openers and what to listen for. The reply to a first-contact email is your only signal for the
DISC read — judge it purely from observable reply behaviour (how fast, how long, how decisive or
detailed), never from the person's role, title, or anything online. DISC is an observation lens, not a
personality test and not a verdict, so a type is always a live hypothesis to confirm, never fixed. You
serve a Kärcher Professional salesperson; this read steers pace, detail, and tone in the later
needs-analysis step.

Format: The output is preparation — a sharp starting point for a real conversation, not a script to
perform. Keep every opener genuine and human; prepare awareness of the person's likely pressures, never
script warmth or empathy the salesperson does not feel. Return only the per-stakeholder prep, no
preamble. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in. End with exactly this line, on its own:

From here, no script — listen and connect.

Check:
- Work only from the map and the observed reply behaviour; keep every DISC read a labelled hypothesis,
  and where a person did not reply, offer no type rather than inferring one from their role.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent facts about real named people beyond
  what the map and their reply behaviour contain.



The Gem gets you ready; it does not run the conversation for you. You make that check yourself, in the room, not on the page.

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
text:
35.5 Try it yourself
Prepare the human side of the NorthStar meeting with Lars.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Prepare with the Rapport Gem. Open the Rapport Gem ↗ and attach the NorthStar Buying Center Map (your own from Step 3, or copy the ready-made Doc ↗ and attach the copy), then tell it how Lars replied to the first email. Notice what you do not do: you describe how he replied, you never tell the Gem his type, and you let it offer the hypothesis.






TipCopy into the Rapport Gem



Using the Buying Center Map I attached, prepare the human side of my meeting with Lars Meijer, Regional
Operations Director.

How Lars replied to the first email: within the hour, three short lines, no small talk. He asked only
what a pilot on the three scrubber driers would involve and how quickly it could start.
(Sophie, Milan and Eva have not been contacted yet, so there is no reply from them.)


Export the prep into a Google Doc (see the example ↗) to bring into the meeting — the opener, what to listen for, and the DISC hypothesis in one place.
2. Read the output as a starting point, not a script. The Gem returns Lars’s likely style, one or two openers, what to listen for, a DISC hypothesis labelled as a hypothesis, and a mindset reminder, then stops at the hand-off line. Out loud, pick the one opener you would genuinely say, name the single thing you will listen for in the first two minutes to confirm or correct the hypothesis, and say your own hand-off line, the exact moment you stop preparing and just listen.
B · Do it for real: your own account
3. Before your next real meeting this week, prepare one genuine opener and a type hypothesis with the Rapport Gem (attach your own buying-center map), then walk in without notes and just listen for the first two minutes.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Is the opener genuine, something you would actually say, or does it read as a performed line?
What single observation in the first two minutes would confirm or correct your DISC hypothesis?
Did you actually stop at the hand-off, or did the preparation keep talking for you?

title: 35  Conversation Preparation
text:
35.6 In summary
This step prepares the human side of selling and then gets out of the way. You walk in with three things in place, confidence, conviction, and context, a fitting opening, a first read of the person as a working hypothesis, and a clear hand-off line where the tool stops and you take over. DISC gives you a way to read the type live and adapt your opening, pace, and detail, and that read steers how the needs analysis flows in the next step. Everything here is preparation; the trust and the listening happen in the room, which is why this short step matters as much as the research-heavy ones.
The next chapter, Needs Analysis, is where that preparation turns into sharper discovery questions. You carry two threads into it: the value hypothesis you came to test, and the DISC read that sets the pace, detail, and tone of the conversation with Lars and the wider Buying Center.






NoteWhat the research says





The chapter’s method has a firmer scientific footing than “rapport” usually gets credit for. The core skill, reading the person and adapting how you open, your pace, and your level of detail, is the peer-reviewed construct of adaptive selling. Weitz, Sujan and Sujan (1986) showed that effective salespeople adjust their behaviour to the customer and the situation, driven by two things: knowledge of customer types and fitting approaches, and the motivation to change their own behaviour. That is exactly what DISC operationalizes in this chapter. Spiro and Weitz (1990) then made adaptive selling measurable with the ADAPTS scale, and two of its five facets map straight onto this step: the confidence to alter your approach during a conversation and the collection of information to adapt. So “confidence” is not a mood; it is a component of a measured selling skill, and adapting your style is a learnable competence, not a personality gift.
Mindset earns its place here too. In a classic longitudinal study of MetLife sales agents, Seligman and Schulman (1986) found that a more optimistic explanatory style predicted higher sales productivity (the more optimistic half reportedly sold around 37% more) and a lower likelihood of quitting. The inner state you walk in with measurably shapes the result, which is why preparing the mindset is not a soft extra. (Treat the exact figure as the study’s reported result, not a guarantee.)
Rapport itself is a real, measurable thing rather than luck in the room. Gremler and Gwinner (2000) established rapport as a distinct construct with two dimensions, an enjoyable interaction and a personal connection, and linked it to satisfaction, loyalty intent, and word of mouth. That is the evidence behind preparing a genuine opening: rapport is built, and it carries the relationship. And the human half of the step, the listening, has its own validated construct, Active Empathetic Listening (Drollinger, Comer and Warrington 2006): precisely the competence the Gem hands back to you at the hand-off line, and the one the next chapter puts to work in full.






ImportantDISC, used honestly



DISC is a useful vocabulary, not a validated test, and the chapter treats it that way on purpose. The D/I/S/C labels trace back to Marston (1928), a pre-scientific work never designed as an assessment. When researchers compare the four-quadrant model to the Five-Factor Model (the Big Five, the validated structure in personality psychology; McCrae & John 1992), the DISC dimensions turn out to correlate with the Big Five rather than stand on their own (Jones & Hartley 2013). The honest reading: DISC is a fast, memorable way to observe and adapt live, but it is not a personality test and never a basis for AI-profiling someone from public data. That is exactly why we keep the type a hypothesis you confirm in the room.








NoteWhy the human moment still matters in the AI era



The market is moving the same way the chapter argues. Gartner (2025) forecasts that by 2030 roughly 75% of B2B buyers will prefer sales experiences that prioritize human interaction over AI, a reversal after years of growth in self-service and AI-driven buying, and strongest exactly where decisions are complex and high-stakes. AI front-loads and sharpens the preparation; the trust still gets built face to face. (A forecast to watch, not a settled number.)

title: 36  Needs Analysis
text:
36.1 Ask before you pitch
On the map above, the customer has moved into Evaluation: the question is no longer whether to act, but “what exactly do we need?”.
This is Step 5 of the sales cycle, and it is the hinge of the whole thing. Up to now you have built a value hypothesis in the research step, reached the right people in the contacts step, and prepared the human side of the meeting in conversation preparation. Now you are inside the meeting itself, at the needs analysis. The goal is not a method to memorize. The goal is a short, sharp set of questions going in, and a confirmed need profile coming out, ready to turn into a value argument in the next step. Questions in, need profile out.
Many conversations lose quality the moment the product comes too early. The seller moves quickly toward the solution, the customer’s need stays half-defined, and the benefit language that follows sounds generic, because it is not yet anchored in anything the customer has actually said. The problem is rarely the feature set. It is the weak bridge between the customer’s reality and the solution story.
The fix is simple in principle and hard in practice: ask first, argue later. Better questions at the start are exactly what make the value story at the end specific and convincing. So you deliberately slow down in the right place, stay in discovery until the need is clear, and only then move toward the solution. Two quiet tools make that happen. One gives you a question order that makes each question sharper. The other gives you a checklist of need dimensions so nothing important slips through. You never name either of them in front of the customer. Together they help you ask well and listen completely.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.2 Two threads you pick up here
text:
36.2 Two threads you pick up here
You do not start cold. From Step 2 you carry the value hypothesis — why Kärcher might matter to NorthStar (the downtime pressure, the fragmented fleet, the wish to standardize) — and that is exactly what you came to test. From Step 4 you carry the DISC read: how Lars and the others like to be engaged, which steers your pace, detail, and tone in the moment. What you hand on from here is no longer a guess, but a confirmed need and a clear profile.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.3 A simple order makes questions sharper
text:
36.3 A simple order makes questions sharper
A strong discovery sequence moves through four steps, in order — SNIP (Situation, Need, Impact, Pay-off), where the Need step is the gap: what is not working well enough. You do not drill the acronym; you keep the discipline of the order:








Step
What it does
The question gathers




Situation
Understand the current setup, environment, and routines
facts


Need
Identify what is not working well enough, and what the customer wants to change
tension


Impact
Make the consequence of leaving that need unresolved visible
a concrete, often quantifiable consequence


Pay-off
Let the customer describe the better future they would actually value
the future the customer wants



First you understand the situation, then surface the need, and only then develop it: the impact if nothing changes, and the pay-off if it does. Impact and Pay-off are the two steps most sellers underuse, even though they create the strongest movement — they turn a vague problem into customer-owned urgency and value, so do not jump to the pay-off before the customer has named the impact. That order is also what makes each question sharper: because each step builds on the last, the customer ends up describing the business consequence in their own words. The later value argument then rests on the customer’s own logic, not just your wording.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
text:
36.4 High-gain questions by role
A high-gain question does more than collect information. It makes the customer think. In practice, high-gain questions share three traits: they invite explanation instead of a yes-or-no answer, they connect a local issue to a broader business consequence, and they help the customer think forward, not only backward.
The four-step order stays fixed across the Buying Center; only the emphasis and the language change by role. Preparing both variants in advance is what lets you adapt to whoever is in the room without improvising: the role decides the wording, the order stays the same.
Lars Meijer, Regional Operations Director — uptime and continuity. Lars is the operational sponsor, so his questions live at the level of uptime, continuity, and rollout, and each one is a direct test of the value hypothesis from Step 2.







Step
Question for Lars




Situation
How are cleaning standards managed across your sites right now?


Need
Where do inconsistent routines create the most pressure?


Impact
What does unplanned downtime cost you across a busy week?


Pay-off
What would reliable continuity make possible at scale?



Milan Bakker, Site Supervisor — daily site routines. Milan judges whether the pilot feels practical under real operating pressure, so his questions stay close to the floor. Same order as Lars, but the language is operational, not strategic.







Step
Question for Milan




Situation
Walk me through a normal cleaning shift on this site.


Need
Where does the current setup slow your team down?


Impact
When a machine goes down mid-shift, what actually happens?


Pay-off
What would make handovers and issue-handling easier day to day?

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.5 Let the DISC read steer the flow
text:
36.5 Let the DISC read steer the flow
You walk in with a prepared question set, but you do not read it like a script. This is where the DISC observation from Step 4 earns its place. The questions are prepared in advance; how you ask them adapts live. If Lars comes across as direct and results-driven, you keep it tight, lead with the cost and the outcome, and do not over-explain. If Milan is more steady and detail-oriented, you slow down, give him room, and stay concrete and practical. Same questions, different delivery.






TipThe funnel is a guide, not a script



DISC stays a working hypothesis you adjust as the conversation unfolds, and the human always leads. Ask the next question only when it fits the moment, not because it is next on the list, and adapt the moment the customer gives you new information. The AI prepared the questions; you read the room.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.6 Six dimensions capture the whole need
text:
36.6 Six dimensions capture the whole need
The question order makes each question sharper. The six need dimensions make sure you capture the whole need. After a meeting, teams usually leave with many notes but no clean view of what the customer values most. A few issues sound strong, some comments feel important, several observations seem useful, but the picture is uneven. The fix is to read the notes against a fixed checklist of six need dimensions. The acronym behind them, SPEICS (Service, Performance, Economy, Image, Comfort, Safety), stays in the background; the checklist is what you use.







Dimension
In plain words




Service
reliability, support, responsiveness, and continuity


Performance
output quality, productivity, and result


Economy
cost logic, business value, and commercial viability


Image
reputation and outward perception


Comfort
usability, routine, and ease in daily operation


Safety
operational risk, continuity, and compliance-related security



The first three usually carry the commercial case and are easy to spot. The second three matter just as much but are easier to miss when notes are messy. The value of a fixed checklist is not only completeness, it is discipline: instead of assuming that one visible issue tells the whole story, you test the account against all six dimensions and keep only what the evidence actually supports.

36.6.1 No single voice covers the whole account
A complete map is not a box-ticking exercise. It is the safeguard against building a one-sided profile, because no single contact speaks for the whole account. In the NorthStar Buying Center, each stakeholder weights the same six dimensions differently:

Lars Meijer (Regional Operations Director) leads with performance and reliability — proof the pilot reduces disruption across sites.
Sophie Verbeek (Procurement) tests economy and value logic — whether the service component is justified and defendable internally.
Milan Bakker (Site Supervisor) judges comfort and daily usability — if onboarding and routines feel impractical, his confidence drops fast.
Eva van Dijk (HSE and Service Lead) needs safety and a clear support path before she will back a rollout.

If you only listen to Lars, you hear performance and reliability and you build a profile that looks finished but is one-sided, and it will fail at the next stakeholder. The checklist forces the useful question: whose need is still missing?


36.6.2 The empty boxes are the point
This is the core discipline of the chapter. The point of a six-point check is not to tick every box. It is to make the empty boxes visible. If a need has thin or no evidence, that is not a weakness in the customer, it is a missing question on your side. In the NorthStar case the strongest signals after a discovery visit tend to sit in Service, Performance, and Economy, while Comfort (Milan), Safety (Eva), and Image (NorthStar is itself a facility-services provider, so its cleaning quality shapes how its own customers judge it) are easy to underplay. The wrong response to a thin dimension is to invent an assumption so the profile looks complete. The right response is to mark it underexplored and prepare exactly one good follow-up, addressed to the person who owns that need:

Comfort gap → ask Milan about operator onboarding and routine simplification across sites.
Safety gap → ask Eva about the escalation path and support response when a unit fails.
Image gap → ask Lars whether service consistency affects how NorthStar’s own customers judge them.

One good question per gap is enough. An honest gap is more valuable than a confident guess. If a dimension genuinely is not commercially relevant at this stage, the right call may be not to force a question at all. The checklist sharpens judgment; it does not replace it.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.7 The Needs-Analysis Gem builds your question set
text:
36.7 The Needs-Analysis Gem builds your question set
You do not type any of this from a blank page. The Needs-Analysis Gem (“Needs-Analysis (Kärcher Sales)”) is a prepared expert that already knows the question order and the six need dimensions. It works in Google Docs, so the output is a living conversation guide you can edit. You attach the NorthStar briefing Doc and the Buying-Center Map Doc, and it does two jobs:

Drafts and sorts a question set in the Situation → Need → Impact → Pay-off order, with at least one high-gain question per role, deliberately aligned to the value hypothesis from Step 2, so the conversation tests what you actually believe.
Tags answers into the six need dimensions after the conversation, quotes the evidence, rates each as strong, weak, or underexplored, and flags what is still thin.

You normally just open the prepared Gem and attach your input — the briefing and the map Docs to draft questions, your visit-notes Doc to tag them.
Prompts you can copy and try. You supply the roles you are meeting and, when you have them, the briefing and Buying-Center Map Docs (before the meeting) or your visit-notes Doc (after it). The questions, and the need profile, are what the Gem produces — never describe the needs yourself.
Start with the simplest version. Just name the roles you are preparing for, and let the Gem draft the Situation → Need → Impact → Pay-off question set. This is the example prompt the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Needs-Analysis Gem



Draft discovery questions for an operations sponsor and a site supervisor.


For a sharper set, attach your account Docs and give the value hypothesis you came to test, so each question is aligned to it:






TipCopy into the Needs-Analysis Gem



Using the NorthStar Account Briefing and Buying Center Map I attached, draft a discovery question set for the NorthStar Facility Services site visit. Value hypothesis from Step 2: a reliable, serviced fleet (starting with a low-risk pilot on the BR 45/22 scrubber driers) would cut downtime and simplify daily operations. I am meeting Lars Meijer (operations director) and Milan Bakker (site supervisor).


After the meeting, switch to the other job: paste or attach your visit notes and let the Gem tag them into the six need dimensions, quoting the evidence and flagging what is thin:






TipCopy into the Needs-Analysis Gem



Using the NorthStar visit notes I attached, tag them into the six need dimensions, quote the supporting line for each, and list the dimensions that are still underexplored.


Behind every one of these prompts sits the same full instruction set, kept in one place (the Gem). You normally never see it; it is shown here for reference:






NoteNeeds-Analysis Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a B2B discovery specialist for Kärcher Professional. You believe in "ask before you
pitch": you stay in discovery until the need is clear, keep questions open and product-free, and read
the customer's answers honestly rather than hearing what you hoped to hear.

Task: Run a sharp needs analysis for one account in two jobs — prepare a discovery question set before
the meeting, and structure the answers into a need profile afterward. Detect which job the input asks
for, then do it. Ask only when a missing fact would change the result; do not invent facts to fill a gap.
  JOB 1 — DRAFT THE QUESTION SET (input: an account briefing, a Buying-Center map, a value hypothesis,
  and the role(s) to prepare for, e.g. operations sponsor and site supervisor):
  1. Draft two open, product-free questions for EACH of the four steps (Situation, Need, Impact, Pay-off),
     grouped under the four step headings, for each named role.
  2. Make at least one question per role a high-gain question: it invites explanation (no yes/no),
     connects a local issue to a broader business consequence, and helps the customer think forward.
  3. Align the questions to the provided value hypothesis, so the conversation tests what the
     salesperson actually believes.
  4. Flag any question that assumes a fact not in the briefing, instead of inventing the fact.
  JOB 2 — TAG THE ANSWERS (input: notes from the discovery visit):
  1. Classify each note into exactly one of the six need dimensions, and quote the exact note that
     supports it.
  2. Give each dimension a strength level: strong, weak, or underexplored.
  3. If a dimension has thin or no evidence, mark it underexplored; do not invent evidence to fill a gap.
  4. List the underexplored dimensions separately so the salesperson sees what is missing, and for each
     suggest one follow-up question addressed to the stakeholder who owns that need.

Context (what you already know, never named to the customer):
  A) The question order (SNIP), in plain words:
     - Situation — the current setup, environment, and routines (gathers facts)
     - Need — the gap: what is not working well enough, what they want to change (gathers tension)
     - Impact — what it costs to leave that need unresolved (a concrete, often quantifiable consequence)
     - Pay-off — the better future the customer would actually value
     Impact and Pay-off are the high-movement steps: they get the customer to state the business consequence
     in their own words. Each step sharpens the next; do not jump to Pay-off before Impact is described.
  B) The six need dimensions, used as a completeness checklist (keep the acronym in the background):
     - Service     — reliability, support, responsiveness, continuity
     - Performance — output quality, productivity, result
     - Economy     — cost logic, business value, commercial viability
     - Image       — reputation and outward perception
     - Comfort     — usability, routine, ease in daily operation
     - Safety      — operational risk, continuity, compliance-related security
  You serve a Kärcher Professional salesperson; the value hypothesis you align to was produced in an
  earlier step, and the confirmed need profile hands forward to the value-argument step.

Format: Questions must be OPEN and NON-LEADING, grouped under the four step headings per role. For the
need profile, give one entry per dimension (the supporting quote, the strength level), then the
underexplored dimensions and their follow-up questions separately. Never surface the acronyms or
framework names to the customer; they run in the background. Return only the requested output, no
preamble. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.

Check:
- Keep discovery before any product talk: flag any question that quietly presupposes the Kärcher
  solution. When tagging, ground each strength rating in the quoted note — reserve "strong" for a note
  that genuinely supports it, and mark weak or underexplored when in doubt rather than overclaiming.
- The salesperson decides what to ask and owns what they heard: you draft more than they need and sort
  fast, but you never decide the need for them and never fill an empty dimension with an assumption.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: work only from what is provided, and never invent
  named real people, figures, or internal facts.



The Gem is genuinely useful: it drafts more questions than you need, which is good, and it sorts messy notes in seconds. But you stay in charge on both sides. On the draft, cut the questions that sound forced or that quietly presuppose your solution, and keep the strongest one or two per step that you would actually be comfortable asking. On the tagging, the model overclassifies — it reads ambition into a passing comment and marks a need as strong when the note barely supports it. So make two passes: first confirm every tag is backed by something the customer actually said and remove the confident guesses; then look at what it flags as empty, because that is your follow-up list. The model proposes; you decide what to ask, and you decide what you really heard, and you verify it before it shapes the conversation, in line with the Module 0 quality gate.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
section: 36.8 You walk out with a confirmed need
text:
36.8 You walk out with a confirmed need
This is the moment the whole thread has been building toward. Back in Step 2, the need and the BANT picture (Budget, Authority, Need, Timing) were a reasoned hypothesis; here, in the conversation, that guess becomes ground truth.







Was a hypothesis (Step 2)
Now confirmed (Step 5)




Likely need: uptime, standardization
A real need, in the customer’s own words


BANT: budget, authority, timing assumed
BANT checked against what the customer actually said



You leave with two artifacts. The first is the conversation guide, the ordered set of questions you took into the meeting. The second, and the one that steers everything after it, is the need profile: the answers tagged into the six dimensions, with the strong needs confirmed and the thin ones named as gaps. That profile is the filter for the next step. In Value Argumentation you only build arguments that map to a confirmed need, and you prioritize them by the dimensions the customer weighted most heavily. A complete, honest profile is what makes the value story specific; a thin one makes every benefit sound generic and easy to match. Better questions beat better pitches: AI drafts and sorts the questions and tags the answers, but it cannot have the conversation for you, and it cannot decide what matters.

title: 36  Needs Analysis
text:
36.9 Try it yourself
Produce the two artifacts you will reuse in the next chapter.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Build the question set with the Needs-Analysis Gem ↗. Attach the NorthStar briefing and Buying Center Map (your own from Steps 2–3, or copy the ready-made Docs — briefing ↗, map ↗ — and attach the copies), give it the value hypothesis you came to test, then name the roles you are meeting. Notice what you do not do: you do not write the questions yourself, and you do not tell the Gem which dimensions matter.






TipCopy into the Needs-Analysis Gem



Using the briefing and the Buying Center Map I attached, draft a discovery question set for the
NorthStar site visit.

Value hypothesis from Step 2: NorthStar loses uptime to a fragmented, mixed-age cleaning fleet across
its sites and wants to standardize. A reliable, serviced fleet, starting with a low-risk pilot on the
three scrubber driers, would cut downtime and simplify daily operations.

I am meeting Lars Meijer (Regional Operations Director) and Milan Bakker (Site Supervisor).


Then cut the draft to the one or two questions per step you would actually be comfortable asking, and delete any that quietly presuppose the solution. Export the set into a Google Doc (see the example ↗) — that is the conversation guide you take into the visit.
2. Tag the visit notes with the same Gem. After the conversation, write your notes up as a Google Doc and attach it (see an example ↗), then let the Gem sort them. Again, notice what you do not do: you do not tell it which box is strong or which is empty, because checking that is the whole exercise. Real notes are messier than a form — your attached Doc might read like this:

Lars: every site runs cleaning its own way, no shared standard; when a machine fails mid-shift, quality drops and he hears about it. Wants one setup he does not have to manage by hand. Milan: shifts are tight, so a breakdown means pulling someone off other work; new operators take a while to get confident on the current mix of machines. Sophie: would need the service component clearly justified before she signs off. (Eva, who owns safety and the support path, was not in the room.)







TipCopy into the Needs-Analysis Gem



Using the visit notes I attached, tag them into the six need dimensions.


Then run the chapter’s two passes: first check every tag against what was actually said and delete the confident guesses, then read the empty boxes as your follow-up list. Name the one dimension most at risk of being missed, and whose need it is. Export the tagged profile into a Google Doc (for example “NorthStar Need Profile” — see the example ↗) — that is the Doc you attach in the next chapter.
B · Do it for real: your own account
3. Sketch four discovery questions for your own next real account, one per step (Situation → Need → Impact → Pay-off), and name the one need dimension you would most want to confirm.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Which question would most change how you sell this account?
Which need dimension is most at risk of being missed, and whose need is it?
Where did a question quietly presuppose the solution?

title: 36  Needs Analysis
text:
36.10 In summary
Needs analysis is compact, but used with discipline it lifts the quality of everything downstream. Ask before you pitch. The Situation → Need → Impact → Pay-off order makes each question sharper and moves the customer from describing facts to naming consequences in their own words. The six dimensions — Service, Performance, Economy, Image, Comfort, and Safety — make sure you capture the whole need and surface the boxes you did not yet hear. Role variants for Lars and Milan adjust the language while the order stays stable, and the DISC read from Step 4 steers the pace, the detail, and the tone live. The Needs-Analysis Gem drafts and sorts the question set and tags the answers, but you decide what to ask and own what you heard.
You hand on a confirmed need, not a guess. The next chapter, Value Argumentation, turns it into a customer-specific value argument — specific precisely because the need underneath it is real.






NoteWhat the research says





The chapter’s central move — ask before you pitch — is one of the best-established findings in selling research. Saxe and Weitz (1982) defined and measured customer orientation (the SOCO scale): the degree to which a salesperson works to help the customer make a good buying decision rather than push for a quick close. Customer-oriented selling is tied to the seller’s actual ability to help and to the quality of the relationship. And it is not just a “nice” posture: Terho, Eggert, Haas and Ulaga (2015) show empirically that sales strategy translates into performance through customer orientation and value-based selling. The need you genuinely understand is the mechanism that turns effort into results.
Why the Situation → Need → Impact → Pay-off order works is the most-studied question in discovery. Rackham’s SPIN Selling (1988), built on Huthwaite’s analysis of tens of thousands of real sales calls, distinguishes four question types — Situation, Problem, Implication, and Need-payoff — and finds that in larger, complex sales it is the Implication and Need-payoff questions (our Impact and Pay-off) that correlate with success, while situation and problem questions alone do not. Implication questions make the consequence of a problem felt; need-payoff questions get the customer to state the value of a solution in their own words. That is exactly why the order is doing double duty: it sharpens each question, and it moves persuasion from your assertion to the customer’s own logic. (Honest scope: the effect is strongest in complex sales; in small transactional deals it matters less.)
Capturing the whole need is a listening problem, and listening is measurable. Comer and Drollinger (1999) framed active empathetic listening as a driver of selling success, trust, and relationship quality; Drollinger, Comer and Warrington (2006) validated it as a scale with three parts — sensing (hearing the unsaid), processing (remembering and connecting), and responding (visibly reacting and probing). That is the human skill behind the six-dimension check: the order gets the customer talking, and disciplined listening is what makes sure you heard all of it, not just the loud parts.
Finally, adapting the questions by role without changing the logic is adaptive selling, the same construct that anchors Step 4 (Weitz, Sujan & Sujan 1986; Spiro & Weitz 1990). The discovery-specific point is narrow: the role decides the language of a question, never the order, and the live DISC read decides the delivery. Your prepared variants for Lars and Milan are that adaptation done on purpose rather than improvised.






NoteWhat discovery calls actually look like



Conversation-intelligence data puts numbers on the craft. Analysing large samples of recorded discovery calls, Gong reports that successful calls tend to ask a moderate number of questions (roughly 11–14 — enough to go deep, not so many it feels like an interrogation), spread them across the call, use follow-up questions that build directly on the buyer’s last answer, and keep a talk-to-listen ratio that favours listening (the seller talking well under half the time). Treat these as practitioner benchmarks to verify, not laws — but they line up neatly with the academic story: fewer, sharper questions and more listening.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
text:
37.1 Why features don’t sell and value does
On the map above, the customer is still in Evaluation, now asking whether your answer is worth it. You turn the confirmed need into a customer-specific value case.
This is Step 6 of the sales cycle, and it is where the whole thread so far pays off. You walked out of the needs analysis with one artifact that steers everything from here: a confirmed need profile, the customer’s answers tagged into the six dimensions, with the strong needs evidenced and the thin ones named. Now you turn that profile into a value argument the customer recognises as their own situation, not a product pitch that drifts straight to price. The input is the need profile, not the catalogue. The output is one customer-specific value block for NorthStar, ready to become a presentation in the next step.
A list of features does not win a deal. You can describe the machine well, state every specification correctly, word it nicely, and the customer will still not feel why the offer matters to their situation. Worse, a pitch that leads with the product sounds like every other vendor, and the conversation drifts straight to price. A product fact only becomes value when you tie it to a need the customer has confirmed. That single link is the whole job of this step.
This matters especially in an account like NorthStar. Service continuity, onboarding quality, and rollout logic carry real commercial weight there. If the conversation starts with equipment alone, those drivers disappear too early, and procurement gets an easy path to reduce everything to unit price, because the broader business logic was never made visible. The objective is not to make you more aggressive or more defensive. It is to make the commercial logic clearer and to lead with what discovery already proved the customer cares about.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.2 The hypothesis from research becomes the argument
text:
37.2 The hypothesis from research becomes the argument
Back in the research step you formed a first value hypothesis about why Kärcher might matter to NorthStar — uptime, service continuity, a low-risk pilot. That was an educated guess that shaped the first email and the discovery questions. In the needs analysis the customer confirmed which of those needs are real. So the argument you build now is no longer a guess; it is a substantiated case, grounded in what the customer actually said.
That arc — hypothesis to confirmed need to proven argument — is what makes this value block credible rather than generic. Every benefit you claim should trace back along that line. If a benefit has no root in the confirmed need profile, it is a feature claim wearing the costume of an argument, and a competitor can match it just as easily.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.3 Start from the need profile, not the catalogue
text:
37.3 Start from the need profile, not the catalogue
The value argument is only as good as the discovery behind it. You build value from what the customer confirmed, not from what the brochure lists. In the NorthStar case, discovery surfaced a few well-evidenced dimensions. The strongest signals sit in Service (response time and continuity across sites), Performance (consistent cleaning results across a fragmented fleet), and Economy (procurement will test whether the service component is justified). So the value story should lead with continuity, reliable results, and a defensible cost logic. Anything that drifts back into generic product claims weakens the logic immediately.
The practical test for every line is simple: can the customer hear their own situation inside the benefit? If yes, the value chain is working. If no, the argument is generic and needs sharpening. The need profile is the filter that makes the argument customer-specific, and it is what protects you from improvising benefits that sound reasonable but trace back to nothing the customer actually said. Same machines, same scope — but told from the confirmed need, the offer reflects the customer’s own situation back to them instead of sounding like every other supplier.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.4 A value chain in four plain steps
text:
37.4 A value chain in four plain steps
The structure behind a strong argument is four plain steps. You do not need to name the framework in front of the customer, and you do not need to drill the acronym to use it. In the background it runs Feature, Advantage, Benefit, Check (FABC).

Feature — what the offer is.
Advantage — what that feature lets the customer do.
Benefit — why that matters to this customer, the explicit value and profit link.
Check — a question that confirms the value actually landed.

It helps to see the four steps as two halves. The first half describes the offer: the feature is the fact, the advantage is the capability it unlocks. Most sales arguments live entirely here, and that is exactly why they feel like a product pitch — the customer can get all of it from any brochure. The second half is where value is created. The benefit makes the link to this customer explicit, and the check is a question that finds out whether the value actually landed instead of assuming it did.
The benefit step is the real centre of gravity, and it is the one most arguments skip. They stop after the feature and the advantage and never translate the offer into customer value.
