The benefit only works if it points back to a need the customer confirmed and stays in the customer’s own language. If you cannot trace a benefit to something the customer said, do not claim it. The check question matters just as much, because it keeps the exchange a two-way conversation rather than a monologue. When you build a chain, spend your attention on the second half. The first half is easy.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.5 What a usable NorthStar value chain looks like
text:
37.5 What a usable NorthStar value chain looks like
Here is one complete value chain for NorthStar, written for Lars Meijer, the Regional Operations Director, on the three-unit scrubber-drier pilot. Lead with uptime, because that is what Lars confirmed he needs.

Feature — three Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack scrubber driers plus onboarding and a service-start package. This is deliberately more than the machine, because the customer is buying a working operation, not just three machines.
Advantage — a faster pilot ramp-up with built-in service continuity, not only raw cleaning power.
Benefit — less disruption during critical cleaning windows across sites, and lower risk as the pilot moves toward a broader rollout decision.
Check — “How important is uninterrupted uptime for this pilot?” — which makes the conversation interactive again and invites confirmation or correction.







TipRead the chain backwards to test it



Every line traces back to something discovery confirmed. The feature includes the service deliberately, the advantage names what that combination enables, the benefit connects straight to the strongest need in the profile, and the check keeps Lars in the conversation. If you read the benefit and cannot point to the discovery note behind it, the chain is not ready — that line is a guess, not an argument.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.6 Prioritise by the needs the customer confirmed
text:
37.6 Prioritise by the needs the customer confirmed
Not every true benefit deserves equal airtime. Discovery did not just confirm needs, it ranked them across the six dimensions (Service, Performance, Economy, Image, Comfort, Safety). So the value argument should be ordered the same way. Lead with the dimension the customer weighted highest and could evidence most clearly, and let the marginal points fall to the bottom or drop out entirely.
For NorthStar, that means service continuity and reliable performance lead, the economy question follows because procurement will test it, and anything you cannot tie back to a stated priority does not make the cut. Prioritising like this keeps the argument short, honest, and aimed at what the customer actually decided on. A value chain with three sharp, well-evidenced links beats one with eight links where half are guesses.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.7 Match the language to the role
text:
37.7 Match the language to the role
One account usually needs more than one kind of benefit language, because the people in the Buying Center listen for different value. The commercial core stays stable; only the emphasis changes. The mistake to avoid is not using both lenses — it is mixing them carelessly: pitching a strategic cost case to a site supervisor, or usability language to procurement.









Lars Meijer — Operations
Sophie Verbeek — Procurement




What they buy
Uptime and continuity
A defensible number


What they listen for
Operations that just work
Predictable, comparable cost


Benefit line
Less disruption across sites
A defensible total-cost-of-ownership case per site



The difference is what each one is really doing. Lars is running an operation, so he buys uptime and continuity. Sophie is not buying uptime — she is defending a number in an approval meeting, so she needs predictable cost and a value case that survives a price comparison, not just a low unit price. Both lenses can be true at once: keep the commercial core stable and shift only the framing to fit the role.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.8 TCO: the bridge from price to business value
text:
37.8 TCO: the bridge from price to business value
Total cost of ownership (TCO) — the cost over the whole life of the equipment, not just the purchase price — is the strongest bridge from a price discussion to a business-value discussion. When the conversation drifts toward unit price, the answer is not to avoid commercial clarity. It is to improve it by moving from purchase price to total operating cost over time.
A unit-price comparison hides the cost of downtime, slow ramp-up, and a fragmented fleet that nobody can service consistently. The total-cost framing makes those costs visible, so service responsiveness, onboarding, and uptime stop looking like decorative add-ons and start looking like what they are: real drivers of what the pilot costs to run. This is where you quantify the value wherever you can, because a number the customer recognises is harder to argue away than an adjective.






WarningTwo cautions on the numbers



Do not invent false precision, and treat every figure as a draft until it is verified against the case material. The aim is to connect the service component to operating conditions the customer already cares about — uptime, continuity, and the cost of implementation friction — so the offer stays comparable without collapsing into a machine-only debate. A TCO number the customer cannot follow does not persuade them; it makes them suspicious.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.9 Where the product facts come from: the YelloFile
text:
37.9 Where the product facts come from: the YelloFile
You do not feed the Gem product facts from memory, and you do not let it improvise them. The approved source is the YelloFile, Kärcher’s internal product factsheet for the machine — for this pilot, the YelloFile for the BR 45/22 C Bp Pack. You attach it alongside the need profile, and the Gem reads the real specifications from it instead of guessing.
A YelloFile is more than a spec table. Beside the technical data (working width, area performance, runtime) it already carries the argument: the customer-benefit points and the total-cost logic the product was built on. For the BR 45/22 that is a single battery instead of two, fewer service cases and a better total cost of ownership, a three-year battery warranty, and the manoeuvrability of the turnable brush head. That is exactly the Advantage and Benefit raw material for your chains, straight from the source rather than from memory.
But the rule from earlier still holds: the confirmed need is the filter, not the YelloFile. You pull only the facts and benefit points that map to a need the customer confirmed, and you leave the rest of the catalogue out. A YelloFile full of true features is still a catalogue until the need profile decides which of them matter here.






WarningThe YelloFile is internal — keep it internal



A YelloFile is approved product material, but it is built for internal use: it carries net and list prices, part numbers, availability, and competitor data. Use it as your source, but never carry prices, part numbers, or competitor figures into the customer-facing argument. Keep the internal source and the customer-facing argument separate, and verify the draft before you send.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.10 The Value-Argument Gem drafts the block
text:
37.10 The Value-Argument Gem drafts the block
The Gem for this step is the Value-Argument Gem (“Value-Argument (Kärcher Sales)”), and it lives right in Google Docs beside your work. It is a saved assistant set up once with the FABC structure, a product-to-benefit logic, a TCO framing template, and the role-benefit language for operations and procurement. You build it once and reuse it on every account.
Open the Gem and attach two things: your NorthStar need-profile Doc (plus any draft proposal notes) and the YelloFile for the product, then prompt it. The need profile decides what the argument is about; the YelloFile gives the Gem the real product facts and benefit points. The Gem takes the confirmed needs and builds the Feature–Advantage–Benefit–Check chains, ordered by the priorities the customer confirmed. It then gives you two role variants in one pass — an operations version for Lars and a procurement/TCO version for Sophie — and adds a total-cost line where one applies. In under a minute you have two role-specific drafts grounded in the same need profile. It is a self-contained Google Doc — nothing to connect or sync.
You normally just open the prepared Gem, attach your need-profile Doc and the YelloFile, and name the offer and the roles.
Prompts you can copy and try. What you supply is the confirmed need profile and the product YelloFile (attached, not pasted), plus the offer and which roles you are preparing for. You never write the value chains or name the benefits yourself — the Gem builds them from the confirmed needs and pulls the product facts from the YelloFile.
Start with the simplest version — the example prompt the Gem itself suggests. Attach the NorthStar need profile and the BR 45/22 YelloFile first, then ask:






TipCopy into the Value-Argument Gem



Build the value argument, with an operations and a procurement variant.


To make it concrete, name the offer on the table and the people you are preparing for:






TipCopy into the Value-Argument Gem



Using the need profile and the YelloFile I attached, build the value chains for the NorthStar Facility Services account, ordered by the needs the customer confirmed most strongly. Offer: three Kärcher BR 45/22 scrubber driers plus onboarding and a service-start package. Roles: Lars Meijer (Operations) and Sophie Verbeek (Procurement).


Pitching a different machine? Attach that product’s YelloFile instead and point the Gem at it:






TipCopy into the Value-Argument Gem



I've attached the NorthStar need profile and a different product's YelloFile. Build the value chains for that machine, ordered by the needs the customer confirmed most strongly, and give me the operations variant for Lars Meijer.


You can also ask for just one role’s variant, or a short one-paragraph version of the strongest chain to drop into an email.
The full prompt it carries:






NoteValue-Argument Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a value-argumentation specialist for Kärcher Professional B2B selling. You think in
"value, not features": you reason from what the customer confirmed they need, not from the product
catalogue, and you are disciplined about saying only what the input supports. You draft; the
salesperson verifies the evidence and owns what ships.

Task: Turn a confirmed need profile into a customer-specific value argument. Work from the need
profile (needs tagged into the six dimensions, with the strong ones evidenced), an optional product
factsheet (a Kärcher YelloFile), optional proposal notes, and the role(s) to prepare for.
  1. If the need profile is missing, ask for it before drafting; do not invent needs to fill the gap.
  2. Build one value chain (Feature, Advantage, Benefit, Check — "FABC") for each strongly confirmed
     need. Order the chains by the dimensions the customer weighted most highly, and drop any point
     that does not trace to a confirmed need rather than padding the list.
  3. For the strongest chain, produce TWO role variants that keep the same Feature and Advantage:
     - an OPERATIONS variant that leads with uptime and continuity, ending with a check on what
       uninterrupted operation is worth here;
     - a PROCUREMENT / TCO variant that reframes the Benefit as a defensible total-cost-of-ownership
       case the buyer can sign off internally, ending with a check that asks which figures would make
       approval easy.
  4. Add a total-cost line wherever it applies, connecting the service component (responsiveness,
     onboarding, uptime) to operating cost over time rather than to unit price.

Context (what you already know, never named to the customer):
  A) Who you serve: a Kärcher Professional salesperson preparing the value argument for one B2B
     account, after discovery. The needs were already confirmed with the customer; your job is to
     make the customer hear their own situation in the benefit, so the argument resonates rather than
     lists capabilities.
  B) The value-chain structure (FABC = Feature · Advantage · Benefit · Check), in plain words:
     - Feature   — what the offer is (the fact)
     - Advantage — what that feature lets the customer do (the capability)
     - Benefit   — why it matters to THIS customer, with the explicit value/profit link, in the
                   customer's own language and traceable to a confirmed need
     - Check     — one question that confirms the value landed and keeps it a two-way conversation
     The first two steps describe the offer (any brochure can do that); the value is created in the
     second two. Spend the effort on Benefit and Check.
  C) The six need dimensions, used only to ORDER the argument by what the customer weighted most
     highly (keep the names in the background): Service, Performance, Economy, Image, Comfort, Safety.
     Lead with the dimension the customer confirmed and evidenced most strongly; let marginal points
     drop.
  D) The two benefit lenses for the Buying Center — same commercial core, different emphasis:
     - Operations (e.g. an operations director): uptime, continuity, routines that just work.
     - Procurement (e.g. a buyer): a predictable, comparable, defensible number — total cost of
       ownership (TCO), the cost over the whole life of the equipment, not just the purchase price.
  E) If a product factsheet (a Kärcher YelloFile) is attached, it is your source for the product
     facts (specifications) AND for the supplier's own customer-benefit and total-cost points. Use it
     so Feature and Advantage are accurate and the Benefit is grounded, never improvised — but the
     confirmed need stays the filter: include only the facts and benefits that map to a confirmed
     need, and leave the rest of the catalogue out.

Format: Present the chains as labelled FABC lines, strongest dimension first, then the two role
variants of the strongest chain, then the total-cost line. Make each Check question sound like a real
human question, not a survey item. Keep the framework names and dimension labels in the background —
they shape the order, they are never recited to the customer. Return the value argument only, with no
preamble or meta-commentary. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.
Example (illustrative — adapt to the real confirmed need and the attached YelloFile, don't copy):
  Need (Service, strongest): downtime when a machine fails mid-shift stalls a whole cleaning window.
  Feature:   on-board battery with hot-swap and a Kärcher service response commitment.
  Advantage: a depleted machine is back in service in minutes; faults are handled fast, not queued.
  Benefit:   for Lars's sites, a failed machine no longer means a lost cleaning window — the shift
             keeps running, which is what he said hurts most.
  Check:     when a machine drops out mid-shift today, what does that cost you before it's back?

Check:
- Tie every Benefit to a stated need from the profile; if you cannot trace it, leave it out rather
  than asserting it. Lead with the few points that matter most to this customer, not every possible
  advantage.
- Cite numbers that come with a source (the YelloFile, the proposal notes, the need profile) as
  stated, with that source. Mark a number "to-verify" only when it would help but is unsourced,
  stale, ambiguous, or supplied by the salesperson without a source — and quantify value without
  inventing false precision.
- Take product facts only from the attached factsheet; if no factsheet is attached, describe the
  capability in general terms and tell the salesperson to confirm the spec from the YelloFile.
- Objection handling and price negotiation are out of scope here — they belong to the closing step;
  stay on building the value argument.
- The salesperson decides what is true and what to send; you produce the first draft. Never present
  invented specifications, figures, contract terms, or customer commitments as fact, and never carry
  internal-only fields (prices, part numbers, availability, competitor data) into the customer-facing
  argument.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
section: 37.11 You own the quality: three checks before it ships
text:
37.11 You own the quality: three checks before it ships
The Gem drafts fast, but the salesperson owns the quality. That comes down to three checks you run yourself — the same quality gate from the foundations module applied to the value block.

Trace — walk down the chain and ask whether each benefit maps to a need the customer actually confirmed, or whether the model invented something plausible. If a line has no root in discovery, cut it.
Verify — every figure is a draft, especially the total-cost numbers, so check them against the case material before anything reaches the customer. Remove any claim you cannot support.
Sound — read the check question aloud and ask whether it sounds like a real human question or like a form. If it reads like a survey, rewrite it.

Because a customer-facing proposal needs sign-off, the model proposes and you approve. The Gem produces the first draft; you decide what is true and what to send, and you verify it before it reaches the customer, in line with the Module 0 quality gate.

title: 37  Value Argumentation
text:
37.12 Try it yourself
This is the core exercise of the module and its main output. You build the value block with the Value-Argument Gem, then make it real for an account of your own.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Draft the value block with the Value-Argument Gem ↗. Attach two files: the NorthStar need profile (your own from the last step, or copy the ready-made Doc ↗ and attach the copy) and the BR 45/22 YelloFile (download it here), then name the offer on the table. Don’t paste either file, they are too long; the Gem reads the attachments. The need profile decides what the argument is about; the YelloFile supplies the real product facts and benefit points. Notice what you do not do: you do not write the value chains yourself, you do not name the benefits, and you do not tell the Gem which dimension wins — it builds the chains from the profile and pulls the facts from the YelloFile.






TipCopy into the Value-Argument Gem



Using the need profile and the YelloFile I attached, build the value chains for this account, ordered by
the needs the customer confirmed most strongly. Pull the product facts and customer-benefit points from
the YelloFile, and drop anything that does not map to a confirmed need.

Offer on the table: three Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack scrubber driers plus onboarding and a
service-start package.

I am preparing for Lars Meijer (Operations) and Sophie Verbeek (Procurement).


The Gem returns the Feature → Advantage → Benefit → Check chains in priority order, then two role variants of the strongest chain — an operations version for Lars and a procurement / TCO version for Sophie — with any figure flagged to-verify. Export the value block into a Google Doc (see the example ↗) — that is what you carry into the presentation step.
2. Run the three checks and own what ships. Do not accept the draft as written. Trace each benefit back to a line in your need profile and cut anything that does not map. Verify every figure against the case material, and keep the to-verify flag on anything you have not checked. Sound out each check question aloud and rewrite any that reads like a survey instead of a real question.
B · Do it for real: your own account
3. Take the strongest confirmed need from one of your own live accounts and build the chain for one operational and one commercial role, then run the same three checks.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Which benefit in your chain is weakest on evidence — a real need, or a plausible guess?
For which stakeholder is the TCO framing doing the most work, and which number would you verify first?
Where did a line slip back into a feature claim that any competitor could match?

title: 37  Value Argumentation
text:
37.13 In summary
This step turns the confirmed need profile into customer-relevant value. A four-step value chain (Feature, Advantage, Benefit, Check) connects every product fact to a need the customer actually confirmed, with the framework kept in the background. The value hypothesis you first formed in research becomes a substantiated argument here, because the real need is now known. Because the NorthStar Buying Center weights value differently, the benefit language shifts by role: operational benefit such as uptime for Lars, commercial benefit such as a defensible cost case for Sophie. Total-cost-of-ownership framing bridges from price to business value, and you quantify where you can while treating every figure as a draft to verify. The Value-Argument Gem drafts the block and the role variants inside Google Docs — a standalone document — but the salesperson checks the evidence and owns the decision.
The output is one customer-specific value block for NorthStar, ready to feed the presentation in the next module. Objection handling, including Sophie’s price objection, belongs with the closing step, where it is handled properly, so it is intentionally left out here.






NoteWhat the research says





The chapter’s central move — lead with value, not features — is one of the better-evidenced ideas in modern selling research. Terho, Haas, Eggert and Ulaga (2012) conceptualised value-based selling as crafting the sales effort around the customer’s business value rather than the product — vivid enough that they called it “taking the sales out of selling.” And it is not just a posture. Terho, Eggert, Haas and Ulaga (2015) found that sales strategy only reaches performance when it is carried by behaviour, and value-based selling is one of the two behaviours that carry it. (The other, customer orientation, was the engine of the discovery step before this one.) This is the step where that behaviour does its visible work: an argument built on a need the customer actually confirmed is value-based selling in practice, which is why it leans on the profile discovery confirmed.
How you construct the argument is the most useful piece of evidence here. Anderson, Narus and van Rossum (2006) distinguish three kinds of value proposition. The weakest simply lists all benefits — which invites benefit assertion, claiming advantages for features the customer does not care about. Better is favourable points of difference against the next alternative. The strongest is resonating focus: leading with the one or two points that matter most to this customer, evidenced and in their language. That is the academic root of two disciplines in this chapter — the FABC test “can the customer hear their own situation in the benefit?” and the instruction to prioritise by the dimensions the customer weighted. A short, focused argument is not a simplification; it is the better argument.
Quantifying value has its own literature. Anderson, Kumar and Narus (2007), in Value Merchants, argue that suppliers should demonstrate and document value rather than assert it — translating a difference in performance into a monetary figure built from the customer’s own operating data. That is the substance behind “quantify where you can,” and it is why a documented number outperforms an adjective. The discipline that must travel with it: no false precision, every figure a draft until verified.
For the price-to-value bridge, total cost of ownership is the canonical tool. Ellram (1995) defined the TCO method from the purchasing side — looking beyond price to the many other costs of a purchase across its life — which is precisely Sophie’s logic in procurement. But the honest scope matters: Wouters, Anderson and Wynstra (2005) found that buyers adopt TCO only when its perceived value is clear and the data is available, because it is information- and effort-intensive. A vendor-supplied TCO case persuades only when it is transparent and credible — which is exactly why the “verify the numbers” rule is not optional.






ImportantValue-based selling is harder than it sounds



The most useful counter-evidence: value-based selling is not a quick technique. Töytäri and Rajala (2015) show it is an organisational capability — a value-based mindset, the tools to quantify value, aligned incentives, and a customer willing to engage. Where that capability is missing, firms slip back into product-and-price selling despite their intentions (the “value-selling gap”). The practical reading for this step: the Gem drafts the block in seconds, but the part that actually wins — tracing each benefit to a confirmed need and standing behind every number — is human work that no tool removes.








NoteWhen everyone can draft a value argument



Generative AI now lets any rep produce benefit language in seconds, which quietly raises the bar rather than lowering it. When fluent value claims are cheap, fluency stops being the differentiator. What stays scarce is the discipline the research points to: resonating focus over a list of benefits, a number you can defend over an adjective, and a benefit that traces to something the customer actually said. The Gem makes the draft fast; that only makes the human judgement more decisive, not less.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
text:
38.1 A presentation is not a brochure
On the map above, the customer reaches Decision and asks “does this actually do the job?”. Your slides set up the case, and a live demonstration lets them see the answer.
This is Step 7 of the sales cycle, and it is the most practical step — the one where everything finally faces the customer. Up to now the work has been preparation: research, first contact, conversation prep, discovery, and value argumentation. You walked out of the value-argument step with one artifact that steers this whole chapter: a customer-specific value building block for NorthStar — the feature (three Kärcher BR 45/22 C Bp Pack scrubber driers plus onboarding and a service-start package), the advantage, the benefit tied to confirmed needs, prioritised by what the customer weighted. That block is the input here. Out of it come two outputs: a lean presentation that carries the value story and points to a decision, and a plan for the real demonstration that proves the story is true. From here the thread hands on to closing.
A customer presentation is not a brochure. The goal is not to show everything you know about the product range; it is to help the Buying Center understand the decision in front of them and see the next step clearly. Presentation software, and now AI, makes it very easy to produce volume. The discipline here is the opposite: reduce, sequence, and point to a decision.
Three slides force that simplicity. Each slide has exactly one job:
If a slide is doing two jobs, split it or cut it. The customer does not need a twenty-slide walk-through to decide whether a pilot makes sense; they need clarity on the problem, the logic of the recommendation, and the next action that follows.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
text:
Slide 1 — the situation and why now. The current pressure, the risk, the reason action matters now.
Slide 2 — why the offer fits. The solution linked back to the customer’s actual priorities, with the proof in place.
Slide 3 — the decision and the next step. Concrete, with one owner and one date, not a vague summary.








TipThe worked NorthStar pitch



Three BR 45/22 C Bp Pack scrubber driers in the pilot, each slide doing one job, every claim tracing back to the value block:

Why now. A mixed, aging fleet across sites, machines that break down mid-shift, a push to standardize for uptime and service continuity — the operational pressure NorthStar already feels.
Why this fits. Three scrubber driers plus onboarding and a service-start package, so the ramp-up is fast and low-disruption. The offer answers the pressure named on the first slide.
The decision. Run the pilot, measure what matters (uptime and service response), set a clear review point. Owner: Lars. Review in six weeks.

That is a decision story, not a brochure — and it is exactly what the demo will then prove.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
section: 38.2 One core story, reframed by role
text:
38.2 One core story, reframed by role
The Buying Center does not say yes for one reason, so a single emphasis rarely lands with everyone. You have met this twice already: it is the adaptive selling you practised from conversation preparation on, and you already built the two role variants in the value argument — Lars leading on uptime, Sophie on total cost of ownership. The only new question here is what happens to that one story when it becomes a deck: what must stay fixed, and what may move?
The commercial core stays fixed — the pilot, the three units, the service logic, and the single next step are constant. Only the framing moves: the headline, the order of the proof points, and which economy view leads (operational for Lars, total cost of ownership for Sophie). What you never do is split the account into three disconnected decks, which make it feel sold three different things. So when you reframe, say explicitly what stays and what changes, and ask for one line confirming the core is unchanged — that is what keeps the two versions coherent for the same account.
One word on the economy view, because on a slide it is where trust is won or lost. Lead it as total cost of ownership rather than unit price — you built that bridge in Step 6. The discipline that is specific to the presentation is restraint: a chart that looks overly precise without the evidence behind it makes the customer doubt the whole argument. Quantify only what you can defend, keep the assumptions visible, and show directional operating logic rather than pretending the ROI is exact to the last decimal.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
section: 38.3 Slides set up the argument, the demo earns the trust
text:
38.3 Slides set up the argument, the demo earns the trust
It helps to be honest about what a presentation can and cannot do. A good, short, role-aware presentation gets you the conversation and frames the decision. But in this industry the decision often turns on the demonstration. A site supervisor who watches a scrubber drier leave a floor clean and dry in a single pass in their own cleaning window believes it in a way no slide can match. So treat the presentation and the demo as two halves of one move: the slides set up the argument, and the demonstration earns the trust. That is why the demo is planned with the same care as the slides, and from the same thread.
A demonstration is a sequence of small proofs, not a feature tour. The storyboard has two parts, and both matter. The ordered sequence is the dramaturgy: for each step you set out what you show on the machine, the one line you say while you show it, and the objection most likely to come up there with your answer ready. The talk track is short on purpose — the machine is the argument, you are the guide. The setup checklist is the unglamorous half that decides whether the demo works at all: the kit and machine, the fresh-water fill and a charged battery, the floor conditions on site, and who does what on the day. A great storyboard that fails because the battery was flat still loses the room.






TipThe worked NorthStar storyboard



Each proof tied to a confirmed need, each with its objection anticipated — the demo telling the same story as the slides:

Uptime. Show that one fully charged battery runs a full cleaning window in Eco!Mode, because mid-shift breakdowns are the pain they named. Objection to pre-empt: does the battery last a shift?
Cleaning result. Run a single scrubbing pass on a floor that resembles their hardest real case, including edges and a structured surface where the roller brush and KART head earn their keep, and let the result speak before you do. Objection to pre-empt: does it handle our floor types?
Service continuity. Walk through the service-start package, and answer the question every operator has — who responds when it breaks down. Objection to pre-empt: service response.

If a feature does not answer a need on the list, it does not go in the storyboard.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
text:
38.4 One thread, one coherent story
The reason you build the presentation and the demo from the same value building block is coherence. When the slide says the pilot protects uptime and the demonstration then shows the machine ready in seconds, the customer experiences one argument from two directions, and trust compounds. When slides and demo contradict each other, even slightly, the whole case wobbles.
So before you present, lay the two outputs side by side and check that every claim on a slide has a matching proof in the storyboard, and that every demo step earns a point the slides actually make. One thread, one story, two surfaces. That alignment is the real deliverable of this step — not the slides or the storyboard on their own.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
section: 38.5 The two Gems draft the slides and the demo
text:
38.5 The two Gems draft the slides and the demo
You do not start from a blank canvas, and you do not start from a generic template — you start from the value you already confirmed. Two prepared experts cover this step, and as always with anything customer-facing, the Gem drafts and you decide.






ImportantThe Gem drafts the content, not the customer deck



What the Presentation Gem returns is a content draft: the structure, the proof points, and the wording for each slide. Use it as inspiration for how to prepare the argument, not as a finished presentation. Nothing the Gem or Canvas produces is shown or sent to a customer as-is.
Every customer-facing presentation is built in the official Kärcher Google Slides template and follows the Kärcher corporate identity — its layouts, fonts, colours, and logo. So the move is always two steps: let the Gem shape the content, then transfer that content into the Kärcher CI template, and present that version. The same holds for any customer-facing slides or leave-behind: AI helps you think and draft, the brand-facing artifact is built in Kärcher’s approved template.



38.5.1 The Presentation Gem builds the slides
The first is the Presentation Gem (Presentation Builder (Kärcher Sales)). You open it with Canvas on, attach the value-block Doc from Step 6, and it returns a short, outcome-led outline — problem and insight, solution and proof, value and next step — plus one role-specific variant, drafting in Canvas so you can shape the content before you rebuild it in the Kärcher CI template.
Prompts you can copy and try. You supply the value block Doc from Step 6 (attach it from Drive) and name the role you are meeting; the slide outline, the proof points, and the role variant are what the Gem produces. You never write the slides yourself. The standard move is to attach the value-block Doc and ask for the deck plus one role variant. This is the example prompt the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Presentation Gem



Turn the NorthStar value block I attached into a three-slide customer presentation for Lars Meijer in operations.


Meeting procurement instead? Swap the role to Sophie, so the economy view leads on total cost of ownership rather than the operational angle:






TipCopy into the Presentation Gem



Using the NorthStar value block I attached, build the three-slide presentation, then give me one role variant for Sophie Verbeek in procurement: keep the pilot scope, the service logic and the next step unchanged, and reframe only the emphasis toward total cost of ownership. Flag any figure as to-verify.


Want the meeting to end on a concrete decision? Tell the Gem to make the next-step slide the sharpest, with a named owner and a date:






TipCopy into the Presentation Gem



Using the NorthStar value block I attached, build the three-slide presentation for Lars Meijer (Operations), and make the closing slide a concrete decision: the pilot scope, one owner (Lars), and one review date, not a summary.


You can also ask for an even leaner walk-through, or the three core slides on their own with no role variant yet.
Behind every one of these prompts sits the same full instruction set the Gem carries. You normally never see it; it is shown here for reference:






NotePresentation Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a sales-presentation builder for Kärcher Professional. You think like an editor, not a
brochure designer: you reduce, sequence, and point to a decision, keeping one idea per slide so the
argument lands instead of competing with the presenter. You draft; the salesperson owns the story,
the proof, and what is shown.

Task: Turn a confirmed value building block into a short, outcome-led customer presentation. Work
from the value block and need profile (the feature, the advantage, the benefit tied to confirmed
needs, prioritised by what the customer weighted) and the role(s) to prepare for.
  1. If the value block is missing, ask for it before drafting; do not invent features or proof to
     fill the gap.
  2. Produce a 3-slide outline where the slides do exactly the three jobs in Context A. For each
     slide give a title, one key message, and 2-3 short supporting points — no more.
  3. Produce ONE role-specific variant: keep the commercial core unchanged (pilot scope,
     service-start logic, the single next step) and reframe only the emphasis for the named role.
     End with one line that confirms the core is unchanged, so the two versions stay coherent for the
     same account.
  4. Where a simple TCO or economy view helps, suggest one — quantifying only what the input
     supports.

Context (what you already know):
  A) Who you serve and the shape: a Kärcher Professional salesperson presenting to a B2B Buying
     Center, to help them understand the decision and see the next step. The three-slide structure —
     each slide does exactly ONE job (if a slide does two jobs, split it or cut it):
     1. Problem and insight — the situation and why it matters now (the current pressure, the risk,
        the reason action matters now).
     2. Solution and proof — why the offer fits, linked back to the customer's actual priorities,
        with the proof in place.
     3. Value and next step — the decision, concrete, with one named owner and one date, not a vague
        summary.
  B) One idea per slide. The slide carries a single idea; the voice carries the rest. Keep
     supporting points few and short — a dense slide that the presenter reads out competes with the
     presenter and loses the room. The argument is the message and the proof, not the decoration.
  C) One stable commercial core, reframed by role. The pilot scope, the service logic, and the
     single next step stay CONSTANT across role variants. What may change is only the emphasis: the
     headline, the order of the proof points, and which economy view leads. Two common lenses:
     - Operations (e.g. an operations director): a low-disruption rollout, fewer mid-shift
       breakdowns, fast onboarding; the economy view is operational (fewer downtime incidents), not a
       price table.
     - Procurement (e.g. a buyer): a comparable, justified scope they can defend internally; the
       economy view leads on total cost of ownership (TCO), because uptime, onboarding, and rollout
       risk all carry cost beyond list price.
  D) The economy view is where a presentation wins or loses trust. For a pilot, frame total cost of
     ownership rather than unit price. Quantify only what the input supports, keep assumptions
     visible, and show directional operating logic rather than false precision — an overly precise
     chart without evidence makes the customer doubt the whole argument.

Format: Three slides, each with a title, one key message, and 2-3 short supporting points, then the
one role variant with its "core unchanged" line. Keep one idea per slide; if a slide is doing two
jobs, say so and split it. Keep the language ready to localize for the site language. Return the
slide outline only, with no preamble or meta-commentary. Respond in the same language the salesperson
writes in.
Example (illustrative — adapt to the real value block and role, don't copy):
  Slide 1 — Problem and insight | "Downtime mid-shift costs more than the machine." Points: failures
    stall a cleaning window; the cost is the lost shift, not the repair.
  Slide 2 — Solution and proof | "Standardized cleaning your sites can rely on." Points: BR 45/22
    pilot on the hardest floor; fast service response; tie back to the priority Lars confirmed.
  Slide 3 — Value and next step | "A two-week pilot, decided next." Points: pilot scope and start;
    next step with one owner and one date.

Check:
- Every claim must trace back to the value building block; do not add new product features or proof
  points. List separately any claim you cannot source from the input rather than slipping it in.
- Cite a number that comes with a source as stated, with that source. Mark a figure "to-verify" only
  when it would help but is unsourced, stale, ambiguous, or supplied without a source — and never
  invent false precision.
- The salesperson decides what is true and what to show; you produce the first draft. Objection
  handling and price negotiation are out of scope here (they belong to the closing step).
- Never present invented specifications, figures, contract terms, or customer commitments as fact,
  and keep internal-only fields (prices, part numbers, competitor data) out of customer-facing
  slides.
- The outline is a content draft, not a finished deck. The customer-facing presentation is rebuilt
  by the salesperson in the official Kärcher Google Slides template (Kärcher CI); do not describe the
  outline as ready to present or send.





38.5.2 The Demo Gem plans the real demonstration
The second is the Demo Gem (Demo Storyboard (Kärcher Sales)). It runs in Google Docs and starts from the top needs you confirmed in the needs analysis. From those it builds the storyboard: what to show on the machine, in which order, with a short talk-track line and an anticipated objection and answer for each step, plus a setup checklist. It pulls the same need-and-value thread as the presentation, so the demo proves exactly the points the slides claim, and product content comes from the same YelloFile you used to build the value argument in Step 6, so you are working from approved material, not improvising.
Prompts you can copy and try. You attach two things — the value block Doc (which carries the confirmed needs) and the product’s YelloFile (the approved product facts) — and the ordered storyboard, the talk track, the objections, and the setup checklist are what the Gem produces. You never script the demo yourself. The standard move is to attach both and ask for the storyboard plus the setup checklist. This is the example prompt the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Demo Gem



Using the NorthStar value block and the BR 45/22 YelloFile I attached, plan the demo as a storyboard tied to the confirmed needs, with a setup checklist.


Demonstrating a different machine? Attach that product’s YelloFile instead and name it, so the product facts come from the approved factsheet:






TipCopy into the Demo Gem



Using the NorthStar value block and the attached YelloFile for this machine, build the ordered demo storyboard for the top confirmed needs — each step tied to a need, with a short talk-track line and the likely objection — and a setup checklist. Take all product facts and media only from the attached YelloFile.


Demoing on the customer’s own site rather than in the Learning Lab? Tell the Gem, so the setup checklist names the real water, power, and floor conditions to confirm:






TipCopy into the Demo Gem



Using the NorthStar value block and the BR 45/22 YelloFile I attached, build the storyboard for an on-site demo at the customer's facility — show the representative hardest case on their own floor — and a setup checklist that names the water, power, and surface conditions to confirm on site, with every site-specific item flagged to-confirm.


You can also make the need-tie the explicit spine (each proof anchored to one named need), or ask for a short two-or-three-proof version for a tight slot.
Behind every one of these prompts sits the full instruction set the Gem carries:






NoteDemo Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a product-demonstration planner for Kärcher Professional B2B selling. You think in
proofs, not feature tours: every moment you stage exists to prove one point the customer cares about,
and you aim each proof at a clear, unambiguous result. You draft; the salesperson owns the
demonstration and confirms the kit against the real site.

Task: Turn the customer's confirmed needs into a storyboard for the live product demonstration that
proves exactly the points the presentation claims. Work from the top confirmed needs (from the needs
analysis) and the value building block (from the value-argument step).
  1. If the confirmed needs or the value block are missing, ask for them before drafting; do not
     invent needs or proof to fill the gap.
  2. Build an ORDERED storyboard. For each step give the four parts in Context B, and tie the step
     explicitly to one of the confirmed needs. If a feature does not answer a need on the list, leave
     it out.
  3. Add a short SETUP CHECKLIST: the kit and machine needed; the water, power, and surface
     conditions required on site; and who does what on the day.

Context (what you already know):
  A) Who you serve and why the demo matters: a Kärcher Professional salesperson preparing a live
     demonstration for a B2B account. The slides set up the argument; the demonstration earns the
     trust — in this industry the decision often turns on the demo, because a supervisor who watches
     a machine clear a tough floor in their own cleaning window believes it in a way no slide can
     match. The demo and the slides pull the SAME need-and-value thread, so they tell one coherent
     story: a demonstration is a sequence of small proofs, not a feature tour.
  B) Each storyboard step has four parts:
     1. What to show on the machine.
     2. The order it comes in (the dramaturgy).
     3. One short talk-track line to say while you show it — short on purpose: the machine is the
        argument, you are the guide.
     4. The most likely objection at that step, with a ready answer.
  C) Let the result speak. Aim each proof at a clear, unambiguous result on a case that resembles
     the customer's HARDEST REAL situation — a representative case, never a staged or flattering one.
     An ambiguous demo hands the room back to words and throws away the demonstration's advantage; a
     rigged one wins the moment and loses the trust.
  D) The setup checklist is the unglamorous half that decides whether the demo works at all: the kit
     and machine needed, the water, power, and surface conditions required on site, and who does what
     on the day. A great storyboard that fails because the water connection was wrong still loses.

Format: An ordered storyboard (each step with the four parts, tied to a confirmed need), followed by
the setup checklist. Return the storyboard and checklist only, with no preamble or meta-commentary.
Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.
Example of one step (illustrative — adapt to the real need and the attached YelloFile, don't copy):
  Step 2 — proves the Service need (downtime mid-shift). Show: drain a near-empty machine and
    hot-swap the battery in front of the supervisor. Talk track: "This is what 'back in minutes'
    looks like on your floor." Likely objection: "Swaps slow my team down." Answer: time it live and
    compare with a full recharge.

Check:
- Take product content and media only from the attached product factsheet (the YelloFile); if it is
  not attached, ask for it rather than describing specs from memory.
- Tie every step you keep to a confirmed need; drop anything that does not prove a claimed point.
  Aim for a representative hardest-case proof, not a staged showcase.
- Mark any kit or setup item the salesperson must check against the actual site as "to-confirm"; the
  anticipated objections here are preparation, not the negotiation (full objection handling and price
  negotiation belong to the closing step).
- The salesperson owns the demonstration and confirms the kit; you produce the first draft. Never
  present invented specifications, claims, or figures as fact.





38.5.3 The quality gate before customer use
This is the Step-0 quality gate (Prompt, Validate) applied to a presentation and a demo. Before anything goes to the customer, run three checks:

One idea per slide. Does each slide carry a single idea, is the proof present, is the value explicit, and is the next step with an owner and a date clear?
Figures verified. Quantify only what you can defend, keep assumptions visible, and verify every figure — a chart that looks precise without evidence makes the customer doubt the whole argument.
Every demo step tied to a confirmed need. Is each step anchored to a need the customer named, and is the kit confirmed against the site?

Presentation software makes weak logic look polished, and AI makes that happen even faster, so the final discipline is not creation, it is control. Two rules close this step. The customer-facing deck is always rebuilt in the official Kärcher Google Slides template (Kärcher CI) — the Gem draft is inspiration for the content, never the deck that goes out. And the Step-0 approval route still applies: formal proposals and total-cost content need a sign-off, not just a rep send.

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
text:
38.6 Try it yourself
This is the core exercise of the module. From the NorthStar value building block you built in Step 6 you produce the two halves of one move — a short, role-aware presentation and a demo storyboard that prove the same points — then check they tell one story and make it real for an account of your own.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Draft the slides with the Presentation Gem ↗. Open the Gem with Canvas on and attach the NorthStar value-block Doc (your own from the last step, or copy the ready-made Doc ↗ and attach the copy), then name the role you are meeting. Notice what you do not do: you do not write the slides, you do not choose the proof points, and you do not decide which economy view leads — the Gem builds the three-slide outline and the role variant from the value block.






TipCopy into the Presentation Gem



Using the value building block I attached, build the 3-slide customer presentation for this account, one
idea per slide. Then give me one role variant for Lars Meijer (Operations): keep the pilot scope, the
service logic and the next step unchanged, and reframe only the emphasis. Flag any figure as to-verify.


Shape the three slides in Canvas, then export the content as a draft — and rebuild it in the official Kärcher Google Slides template (Kärcher CI) before the meeting. The Canvas draft carries your content and structure; the Kärcher-template version is the deck you actually present, because nothing customer-facing goes out straight from the Gem. (Swap Lars for Sophie Verbeek to lead the procurement / total-cost variant instead.)
2. Plan the demonstration with the Demo Gem ↗. Open the Demo Gem and attach two things: the same value-block Doc and the BR 45/22 YelloFile (download it here). The value block carries the confirmed needs the demo has to prove; the YelloFile supplies the real product facts, so nothing is improvised. Notice what you do not do: you do not script the demo and you do not pick the features — the Gem ties each proof to a confirmed need and leaves out anything that answers none.






TipCopy into the Demo Gem



Using the value block and the YelloFile I attached, build the ordered demo storyboard for the top
confirmed needs in this account — each step tied to a confirmed need, with a short talk-track line and the
most likely objection. Then add the setup checklist, and flag every site-specific kit item as to-confirm.


Export the storyboard into a Google Doc (see the example ↗). Product details come from the YelloFile, not from memory.
3. Check the two outputs tell one story. Lay the slide outline and the storyboard side by side and confirm every claim on a slide has a matching proof in the demo, and every demo step earns a point the slides actually make. That alignment — not the slides or the storyboard on their own — is the real deliverable of this step.
B · Do it for real: your own account
4. Pick a real account, attach its value-block Doc (and the YelloFile for the machine), and watch the presentation outline and the demo plan come together in minutes — then run the same coherence check.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Which slide is closest to doing two jobs — and how would you split it?
Which demo step rests on the least representative case, and how would you make the proof unambiguous?
Where is a figure on the economy slide still unverified, and which one would you check first?

title: 38  Presentation & Demo
text:
38.7 In summary
This step turns the confirmed value argument into a decision basis for the Buying Center. A lean three-slide presentation — situation, fit, decision — does one job per slide, because working memory is limited and a slide you read aloud competes with you (Mayer & Moreno 2003; Kalyuga, Chandler & Sweller 1999). One stable commercial core is reframed by role, operations for Lars and total cost of ownership for Sophie, without splitting the account. The real demonstration then proves what the slides claim, because direct, unambiguous product experience convinces in a way a slide cannot (Hoch & Deighton 1989; Hoch & Ha 1986) — provided the case shown is representative, not staged (Hoch 2002). Both outputs are drawn from the same value building block, so they cannot contradict each other, and the Presentation Gem and Demo Gem draft both in minutes while you own the story, the proof, and the next step.
The output is a lean, role-aware presentation and a demonstration plan that tell one coherent story, ready for the Buying Center. That decision basis is the bridge into the closing step, where — after the proposal and the demo — you make the next step concrete with closing wording, answers to the typical objections (including Sophie’s price objection, deliberately held until there), and a way to rehearse the close before you make it. The same NorthStar story continues, and so does the thread.






NoteWhat the research says





The two halves of this step rest on two well-established literatures, and it is worth being precise about what each one shows — and where it stops.
Why the demonstration convinces. The strongest evidence for “the demo earns the trust” comes from the research on learning from direct product experience. Hoch and Deighton (1989) model how people learn from using a product and show that what is learned from direct experience tends to be vivid, held with confidence, and hard to revise — an experienced result writes itself in differently than a described claim. Hoch and Ha (1986) add the decisive condition: when the product experience is unambiguous, it dominates judgement and overrides advertising or sales claims; when it is ambiguous, the claims fill the gap and can even colour what the customer thinks they saw. The practical reading is exact: aim the demo at a clear result on a case that resembles the customer’s hardest real situation, and let the result speak before you do. A vague demonstration throws away the very advantage that makes it stronger than a slide.
Why the lean slide works. “One idea per slide” is not an aesthetic preference; it follows from how working memory is loaded. Mayer and Moreno (2003) summarise the multimedia-learning principles around a hard limit on working-memory capacity and give concrete levers to reduce extraneous load — cut material that does not serve the point (coherence), highlight the few things that matter (signaling), and avoid making two channels compete. Kalyuga, Chandler and Sweller (1999) sharpen one lever into a rule: the redundancy effect — identical text on a slide that the presenter reads aloud at the same time tends to hinder learning rather than help it, because the audience burns capacity reconciling the two sources. That is the evidence behind the hardest practical rule in this chapter: do not read a full slide. The slide carries one idea; your voice carries the rest. (These are learning theories, not sales-outcome studies — they explain the mechanism behind the slide and demo disciplines; the sales-context grounding, that a presentation should lead with the few points that matter and move to a decision, comes from resonating focus and the narrative thread of the pitch.)






ImportantThe demo can mislead, too



The honest counter-evidence sits inside the same literature. Hoch (2002) argues that product experience is seductive: precisely because an experienced result feels self-evident, it can mislead when the case shown is not representative. A rigged or flattering demonstration wins the moment and loses the trust. So the discipline that travels with the demo’s power is not optional — show the representative hardest case, not a staged showcase, and treat every figure on the economy slide as a draft until it is verified. The demonstration’s persuasive force is a responsibility, not a trick.








NoteWhy the few live moments have to land



Gartner’s research on the B2B buying journey finds that buyers spend only around 17% of their total purchase time meeting with potential suppliers — and when several suppliers are in play, any one sales rep may get just 5–6%. Most of the journey now happens without a rep in the room. The implication for this step is direct: the few live moments you do get, the presentation and above all the demonstration, have to do disproportionate work. That is the case for preparing both with this much care — they may be the only chance to turn a value argument into a decision.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 39  Closing
text:
39.1 The right next step, at the right moment
On the map above, this is the Decision moment: the customer is ready to commit if the next step is clear. You read the signal and agree the step together.
This is Step 8 of the sales cycle, and it is optional by design: it stays short, self-contained, and easy to drop or add depending on the audience. It picks up directly from the presentation and demonstration step: its input is the customer-specific pitch and value story you built for Lars Meijer (Operations) and Sophie Verbeek (Procurement). Its output is modest on purpose — one chosen closing path for NorthStar and a draft post-meeting summary — the direct input for follow-up. This is also where objection handling lives, because in practice objections and the close arrive together, so it belongs next to the moment it serves.
The first idea to settle is what closing actually is: choosing the right next step at the right moment, and then capturing it so it does not evaporate. That gives you the three moves that carry this whole chapter:
Closing becomes weak when it feels disconnected from the conversation that came before it. The seller pushes toward commitment because it is time to ask, not because the customer is ready to move, and that usually creates resistance. The better rule runs through the whole module: closing should follow the signal, not pressure. You do not choose the technique first; you read the conversation first. This matters more for NorthStar because it is not a simple transactional close — the customer is weighing service scope, rollout logic, commercial clarity, and operational continuity at once, so the closing moment depends on whether those issues are becoming clearer, not only on whether the product was presented well.

title: 39  Closing
text:
Read the buying signal the customer is already giving you.
Choose a closing path — gentle or direct — that matches how strong that signal is.
Capture the agreed next step in a short summary.

title: 39  Closing
section: 39.2 Read the signal before you close
text:
39.2 Read the signal before you close
A close lands when it follows what the customer already says, so read the buying signal first, then match the path to it. A buying signal is simply a practical clue that the customer is moving from interest toward commitment: questions about price, delivery, daily use on site, and implementation. In the NorthStar case, the clearest signals appear when the customer asks about pilot timing, service-start scope, daily use, or how a rollout would actually run.
The stronger skill is not noticing that a signal exists — it is judging its strength. A general pricing question may indicate interest. A specific question about timing or implementation usually means the customer is already imagining the next step. That difference is exactly what tells you which closing path to reach for.

title: 39  Closing
section: 39.3 Two closing paths, matched to the signal
text:
39.3 Two closing paths, matched to the signal
This module uses only two closing paths, because that is enough to cover almost every situation. Teams do not need a long list of closing formulas; they need the discipline to match the closing style to the signal strength.







Gentle (hypothetical) close
Direct close




Tests readiness without forcing a yes or no
Asks for a concrete commitment


Fits a softer, still-forming signal
Fits when scope and timing are already clear


“If we guaranteed uptime, would the pilot make sense?”
“Shall we start the pilot next month?”



The hypothetical close asks how it would work if they said yes, without forcing the decision too early, so it suits a softer signal. The direct close asks for a concrete commitment when scope and timing are already clear and the customer is thinking about implementation rather than basic relevance. The mistake is rarely picking one path over the other; it is using the wrong one at the wrong moment: a direct close used too early feels abrupt, a hypothetical close used too late feels hesitant.






TipNorthStar: match the path to the person



The same pilot produces two different signals from the two stakeholders you know from the buying center:

Sophie Verbeek (Procurement) is still questioning whether the service scope is justified. Commitment is soft, so a gentle hypothetical close fits — one that ties service back to continuity.
Lars Meijer (Operations) asks when the pilot could start. That is a stronger signal, so you move toward a direct close on a date.
A price-only challenge is not a buying signal yet. If all you hear is pressure on the number, you clarify the value before you try to close — which leads straight into objections.

title: 39  Closing
section: 39.4 An objection is an information signal
text:
39.4 An objection is an information signal
This is where objection handling now lives, right next to the close, because in practice the two arrive together. The key reframe is that an objection is rarely just resistance — it usually points to where the value story still has a gap, which makes it useful information.
The most valuable habit is to slow down and classify the concern before you answer, because the category decides the response:

A misunderstanding needs a clarification.
Skepticism needs proof.
A real trade-off needs an honest discussion rather than a counter-argument.
A genuine complaint needs acknowledgement and a concrete next step.

Classifying first is what prevents a defensive reaction. The quality of the response improves immediately once the team stops reacting and starts naming what kind of issue is actually present.

39.4.1 Sophie’s price objection, answered calmly
The most realistic NorthStar objection comes from procurement: “Can we compare the quote on unit price only?” That is a fair, legitimate request, but it hides the service-start value and the cost of pilot disruption. So you do not defend the price. A calm, three-move response works better:

First understand. Ask what a fair comparison should actually include beyond purchase price, and confirm the real concern.
Then reframe to operating cost over time. Use the same total-cost-of-ownership logic from the value chapter: the service-start lowers ramp-up risk and protects critical cleaning windows, so it belongs in the comparison.
Then check. Propose comparing both scenarios side by side, rather than debating abstract value.

The answer does not attack the price concern; it widens the comparison to operating logic and makes the next step concrete. The goal is a calm, evidence-based response, not a memorised script.

title: 39  Closing
text:
39.5 Capture the result
A close only counts once it is actionable. A strong close can still lose its momentum if the follow-up is vague, so the post-meeting summary is what protects the result. After the conversation, you ask the Gem to draft a short summary from your notes that captures what was actually agreed, the concrete next steps, who owns each one, which questions remain open, and what the next customer interaction should be.
The one firm rule — the same one that runs through the whole training — is that the AI must never invent a commitment, an owner, or a deadline that was not actually agreed. Confirmed points and open points stay clearly separate; if ownership or timing is unclear in your notes, the summary flags that rather than inventing certainty. This is the Step-0 discipline applied to the close, and it protects continuity: many promising opportunities weaken not when the closing conversation fails, but when the internal follow-through becomes vague afterwards. It is also exactly what the follow-up chapter relies on.

title: 39  Closing
text:
39.6 The Closing-Persona Gem
The Gem for this step is the Closing-Persona Gem, and it works in three modes so it covers the whole chapter. In coaching mode it gives a second opinion on the signal and the closing path, and a calm answer to the objection in the room. In practice mode it plays Sophie or Lars in a realistic closing dialogue and pushes back with one typical objection, so you can rehearse safely. In summary mode it turns your notes into a structured post-meeting summary with the agreed next step. It is grounded in the NorthStar case and the value story, so the objections stay realistic, and it is instructed to stay in role and react to your closing attempt rather than solving it for you — and, as always with anything customer-facing, the Gem drafts and you decide.
You normally just open the prepared Gem and choose the mode or persona.
Prompts you can copy and try. What you supply is your own short conversation notes (with the stakeholder named) or simply the persona you want to rehearse against; the coaching, the role-play, and the summary are what the Gem produces. The connected NorthStar value-block Doc keeps the objections realistic. Start with the practice mode, the example the Gem itself suggests:






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (practice)



Practice mode: role-play Lars Meijer in operations as a tough buyer so I can rehearse the close on the NorthStar pilot.


Coaching mode instead: paste your notes and name the stakeholder, and it reads the signal and recommends a path. You describe what was said but never tell it which path to use:






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (coaching)



Using the NorthStar value block I attached, coach me on the close. Stakeholder: Sophie Verbeek, Procurement. My notes: she engaged with the pilot but kept coming back to the quote and asked to compare on unit price only; no timeline yet.


When the conversation is over, switch to summary mode and hand it your rough notes; it keeps agreed and open strictly separate:






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (summary)



Switch to summary mode. Here are my rough notes from the NorthStar closing conversation: [paste your bullets]. Keep agreed and open strictly separate, and flag anything unclear instead of filling it in.


Behind all three modes sits the same full instruction set the Gem carries. You normally never see it; it is shown here for reference:






NoteClosing-Persona Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a closing coach and role-play partner for a Kärcher Professional salesperson. You read
the buying signal before recommending a path, stay calm on price, and treat the close as a step you
earn rather than a technique you apply.

Task: Run one of three modes; ask which one the salesperson wants, or infer it from what they share,
then stay in that mode.
  MODE 1 — COACHING (a second opinion on the close and any objection). The salesperson pastes or
  attaches notes from a sales conversation and names the stakeholder (e.g. an operations lead or a
  procurement lead). Ask for the notes if none are given; do not invent the conversation. Then give
  three things:
  1. How strong the buying signal is and what it suggests (a general price question signals interest;
     a concrete question about timing or implementation signals the customer is picturing the next
     step).
  2. Which closing path fits the signal you actually read, not habit — a hypothetical (gentle) close,
     a direct close, or clarify the value first. If the signal is too weak to close, say so and name
     what to clarify first.
  3. One sample closing line in the salesperson's own voice.
  If the notes contain an objection, classify it (misunderstanding, skepticism, real trade-off, or
  complaint) and draft a calm three-move answer: understand the real concern, reframe to operating
  cost over time (total cost of ownership), then check with a concrete next step.
  MODE 2 — PRACTICE (a role-play partner). Play exactly one customer persona the salesperson names
  and stay in role for the whole conversation (e.g. a procurement lead focused on commercial
  justification, service scope, and total cost; or an operations lead focused on rollout confidence,
  daily use on site, and pilot timing). Open in role with one question, then reply one short in-role
  message at a time and wait for each closing attempt. Apply one realistic pressure point per round
  that fits the persona; react as the customer would and let the salesperson solve it. After each
  attempt, add two or three lines of out-of-role feedback: which signal they read, whether the path
  fit, and one thing to sharpen.
  MODE 3 — SUMMARY (capture the result). Turn rough notes into a short post-meeting summary the
  salesperson can act on: (1) what was actually agreed; (2) the concrete next steps; (3) who owns
  each; (4) the questions still open; (5) what the next customer interaction should be. Keep
  confirmed commitments strictly separate from assumptions.

Context (what you already know): You support a Kärcher Professional salesperson and carry the account
and the value story from the earlier steps. Closing follows the signal, not pressure — the path is
matched to the signal strength you actually read. The price answer leans on total cost of ownership
(operating cost over time), not on defending a number. The summary feeds the follow-up step, so its
honesty about what was and was not agreed is what protects continuity.

Format: Keep each mode in the shape described above; in Mode 2, one short in-role message at a time,
with the out-of-role feedback clearly separated. Return only the mode's output, no preamble. Respond
in the same language the salesperson writes in.

Check:
- Work only from what the salesperson gives you and the named case; when a fact is missing, ask or
  flag it as open rather than filling the gap.
- Read the signal before recommending a path, and match the path to its strength — a weak signal
  means clarify first, not close harder.
- You give a second opinion and a practice space, not the decision; the salesperson reads the room
  and owns what happens next.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: in the summary or any closing line, never invent a
  commitment, an owner, a deadline, or a customer quote the notes do not contain — flag it as open.









NoteHear the timing before you try it



Closing is also a skill of language and timing — it has a sound. With a grounded notebook of prepared closing examples, NotebookLM can generate a short audio walk-through so you hear the difference between a pushy line and a natural closing sentence that follows the conversation. It is a preparation layer, not the live attempt, and it lowers the barrier for colleagues not yet confident with role-play — which matters in a module most people rated low.

title: 39  Closing
text:
39.7 Try it yourself
This is the activation, deliberately small for an optional module: one closing attempt per round. Start from the NorthStar pitch and value story you built in Step 7, then make it real on an account of your own. There are two valid ways to practise and neither is mandated — the closing logic is identical and both feed the same summary; you pick one in step 2.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case
1. Read and choose (coaching). Open the Closing-Persona Gem ↗ in coaching mode, attach the NorthStar value-block Doc (your own from Step 7, or copy the ready-made Doc ↗ and attach the copy), and paste your short conversation notes, naming the stakeholder. Notice what you do not do: you describe what the customer said, but you never tell the Gem which path to use or how to answer the price point — you let it read the signal and propose both.






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (coaching)



Using the value building block I attached, coach me on the close for this meeting. Stakeholder: Sophie
Verbeek, Procurement.

My notes: she engaged with the pilot but kept coming back to the quote, and near the end asked whether
we could compare it on unit price only. She did not commit to a timeline.


It reads how strong the signal is, names the kind of concern, recommends a path (hypothetical, direct, or clarify first), drafts a calm price answer, and gives you one closing line in your own voice.
2. Practise once with the Gem. Make exactly one closing attempt. Switch to practice mode and name one persona (Sophie or Lars). It plays the persona and pushes back with one realistic objection; you read the signal, choose your path, and ask one closing question. Notice what you do not do: you do not script the objection or hand it the answer — the Gem decides how the persona pushes back, and you respond live.






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (practice)



Let's role-play. Play Lars Meijer (Operations) for a closing conversation about the NorthStar pilot.
Stay in role and push back the way an operations lead would, react to my closing attempt instead of
solving it, and after my attempt give me two or three lines of feedback. Greet me in role and open
with one question.


If a colleague is on hand, you can also run this without the tool: one of you takes a role card and plays Sophie or Lars, the other runs the closing attempt, then swap and debrief. A person reacts in ways a model cannot, so this often produces the most honest feedback.
3. Capture it (summary). Switch to summary mode and paste your rough notes. Notice what you do not do: you do not write the commitments for it — it keeps agreed and open strictly separate and flags anything unclear instead of inventing it.






TipCopy into the Closing-Persona Gem (summary)



Switch to summary mode. Here are my rough notes from the NorthStar closing conversation: [paste your
bullets — what was discussed, anything agreed, what is still open]. Turn them into a post-meeting
summary: what was actually agreed, the next steps and who owns each, the open questions, and the next
interaction. Keep agreed and open strictly separate, and flag anything I left unclear instead of
filling it in.


Export the summary as a Google Doc (see the example ↗) — that is the artifact you attach in follow-up.
B · Do it for real: your own account
4. Take one real, live deal of your own, recall the last thing the customer actually said, and name the signal you heard in it. That is the same reading skill, on a real account.

As you debrief, ask the questions that matter:

Which buying signal did you read — and was it strong enough for the path you chose?
Which objection were you tempted to answer with a counter-argument instead of classifying it first?
Where does your summary record something as agreed that was really still open?







Leading a team? The same closing judgment, lifted from one conversation to the whole pipeline, becomes the forecast — see Forecast in the Leadership Track.

title: 39  Closing
text:
39.8 In summary
Closing is strongest when it follows the customer’s signals instead of the seller’s pressure — because effectiveness is reading the situation and adapting, not applying a technique (Weitz 1981). Questions about price, delivery, daily use, or implementation tell you whether commitment is becoming real, and their strength tells you which path to choose: a gentle hypothetical close or a direct one. Objections, which now live in this chapter, are treated as information — you classify the concern first, then reduce the resistance rather than push harder (Knowles & Linn 2004), as in the three-move answer to Sophie’s price objection, which is principled negotiation in miniature (Fisher & Ury 1981/1991). For practice you rehearse once with the Closing-Persona Gem (or with a colleague if one is on hand), and the final discipline comes after the conversation: a post-meeting summary that keeps commitments, owners, and open questions honest, and never invents a thing that was not agreed.
Because this module is optional, the takeaway is modest by design: one chosen closing path for NorthStar and one draft post-meeting summary. That summary is the direct input for follow-up, where the meeting output turns into a follow-up email, a conversation summary, and an internal handover. The same NorthStar story continues, and so does the thread.






NoteWhat the research says





The moves in this chapter rest on four well-established literatures, and it is worth being precise about what each shows — and where it stops.
Why the close follows the signal. The discipline “read first, then choose” is the contingency view of selling. Weitz (1981) frames sales effectiveness not as a property of a canned technique but as the moderating interplay of the salesperson’s resources, the customer’s buying task, and the relationship — what is effective is reading the situation and adapting, not applying a favourite formula. That is the academic root of “closing follows the signal, not pressure” and of matching the path to the signal strength you actually heard. The wider shift from the hard, high-pressure close toward a continuation logic is the seven-steps tradition (Moncrief & Marshall 2005, via research & qualification); reading the person differently — gentle for Sophie, direct for Lars — is adaptive selling (Weitz, Sujan & Sujan 1986); and a direct close works because a small, concrete commitment makes a customer act consistently with it (Cialdini, commitment-and-consistency).
