Why an objection is information, not a wall. The reframe “classify first, then reduce the resistance” rests on the resistance-and-persuasion research. Knowles and Linn (2004) distinguish alpha strategies, which add force to the approach (more arguments, more pressure), from omega strategies, which reduce the resistance itself (acknowledge the concern, lower reactance, reframe, leave the choice). Their point is that against resistance, removing it often works better than pushing harder — more counter-arguments tend to produce more counter-resistance. Naming the kind of objection first and then calmly addressing it is exactly an omega move; reacting with a reflexive counter-argument is the alpha move that hardens the room.
Why the price objection is handled by widening, not defending. The three-move answer to Sophie is principled negotiation (Fisher & Ury 1981/1991): separate the people from the problem, focus on interests rather than positions, and use objective criteria. “Unit price only” is a position that hides an interest — a fair, defensible comparison. So you surface the interest, reframe to total cost of ownership (the objective criterion, from the value chapter), and propose a concrete check, instead of defending the number.






ImportantWhat the evidence does — and does not — prove



These are mechanisms, not a closing-outcome study. Weitz (1981) offers a contingency framework; Knowles & Linn (2004) and Fisher & Ury (1981/1991) come from persuasion and negotiation research, not a B2B-closing trial. They give the trustworthy why (read rather than push, reduce resistance, interests over positions), not a closing rate — the sales-context validity comes from the adaptive- and value-selling sources already in the book. The hypothetical and direct close are practitioner structures, like FABC in Step 6 or SPEICS in Step 5: valued for the discipline of matching style to signal, not as validated constructs. And the close stays a human judgment — the AI gives a second opinion and a practice space, never the decision.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.1 Why follow-up protects the deal
On the map above, the customer moves into Adoption, asking “how do we get everyone on board?”. Your follow-up keeps the momentum and makes the choice stick.
This is Step 9 of the sales cycle, and it closes the NorthStar account journey. It picks up directly from the closing step: its input is the post-meeting summary you drafted for the NorthStar pilot — what was agreed, the open points, the next step. Everything in this chapter turns that summary into follow-up. The value you are protecting is the value-hypothesis thread you have carried since research: the customer-specific value you argued in Step 6, presented in Step 7, and committed to in Step 8. A good meeting can still go cold, and this is the step that keeps it from doing so. AI helps you do it fast, but you still decide what matters, who owns the next step, and what happens next — and you do all of it in a Sheet you already keep.
A strong meeting can still go cold. The customer was engaged, the pilot looked likely, everyone left optimistic — and then nothing happened. No one was quite sure who owned the next step, the follow-up never got specific, and a week later the energy was gone. The deal did not die in a dramatic way; it quietly went cold. That is the most common way good opportunities slip, and it is almost completely avoidable.
That is why follow-up earns its own step in the cycle. The purpose is not to automate communication for its own sake, and it is certainly not to add administration. The purpose is to keep commercial momentum from dissolving after the conversation, and to make the next step easy to execute. In the NorthStar case this matters even more, because the account combines upside and fragility. A successful three-unit scrubber-drier pilot can open a multi-site rollout across the Benelux, but the value depends heavily on service continuity and clean onboarding. So follow-up here cannot be a generic recap. It has to protect trust, execution quality, and the account’s growth path at the same time.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.2 One meeting, three outputs
The discipline for this chapter is deliberately small, because the work is easy to skip when it feels heavy. From one meeting you create three things, and the routine is short enough to repeat after every meeting — about ten minutes, which is exactly why it actually gets done.
One output is customer-facing:

A follow-up email with one clear next step.

Two are internal:

A short handover note a colleague can act on.
One next step with a named owner.

This structure turns a vague task called “follow up” into three small artifacts that each solve a different problem. The email protects customer-facing continuity. The handover note protects internal continuity. The owned next step is what actually carries the account forward. None of it needs special tooling: it is a short routine you run with one Gem and a simple Sheet, and the mail itself is finished in Gemini in Gmail, right next to the inbox where you would send it anyway.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
section: 40.3 Start with the email to Lars
text:
40.3 Start with the email to Lars
The first output is the follow-up email — in the NorthStar case to Lars Meijer, the Regional Operations Director and operational sponsor for the pilot you met in the buying center. A good follow-up is not a long transcript of the meeting. It gives Lars a short recap of what was agreed, names a clear owner, and makes one concrete next step easy to confirm. That matters most when service, training, or timing is part of the decision, which is exactly the NorthStar situation.
You give the Gem your post-meeting summary as context and ask for a tight draft. The most important lines in the prompt are the guardrails: the model must not invent dates, owners, or commitments that were never agreed, and it must flag anything you should verify. A follow-up email becomes dangerous the moment it creates artificial certainty. So you read it once, confirm the facts, and only then send. This is the quality gate from the foundations module — prompt, validate — applied to a real customer message.






TipNorthStar: a follow-up that protects, not just recaps



For Lars, the follow-up does three jobs at once: it confirms the pilot scope and timing you agreed, it makes the service-start ownership explicit (the part the whole account hinges on), and it proposes one next step he can confirm in a single reply — not a list of five. Because the value story for NorthStar was always service continuity, a recap that stays silent on service ownership would quietly weaken the very thing you sold.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.4 A short internal handover note
Not all follow-up is a message to the customer. The NorthStar pilot involves service start-up and onboarding, so a colleague in service or another team member may have to pick up part of the work. The internal handover note protects that continuity.
This is not heavy admin. It is a short note in your own notes or a shared Sheet, with four lines:

the issue or situation,
the owner of the next step,
the next step itself,
the one open question that still needs an answer.

The customer experiences one Kärcher, not the handoff behind it, so the quality of that internal note directly affects customer trust. The Gem turns your raw meeting notes into that clean four-line summary in seconds, but the value comes from the discipline of the structure, not the tool. The test is simple: could a colleague act on the note alone, without asking you for context?

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
section: 40.5 Light segmentation: which accounts to work next
text:
40.5 Light segmentation: which accounts to work next
Once the follow-up is out, the next decision is where your attention belongs after this account. This is light segmentation — simply deciding which accounts get your time next. You do not need a dedicated segmentation tool. You use a lightweight account Sheet, one row per account, and ask the Gem to read two simple signals so you can rank what comes next:

Upside — how much could this account grow, and is there rollout or service whitespace you have not worked yet?
Execution risk — how much depends on clean delivery, and is service or ownership still unclear?

A strong follow-up decision asks both at once. It does not only ask, “How valuable could this account become?” It also asks, “How much execution quality will it take to turn that value into reality?” The point is not the label; it is the priority the label gives you.
When you run those two signals on NorthStar, it lands clearly in the high-upside, high-risk group. The upside is real, because the three-unit pilot can open a multi-site Benelux rollout. But the value depends heavily on service continuity and clean onboarding, so it is fragile at the same time. The priority that comes out of that is clear: protect execution first, keep the service story tight, and only then push the expansion.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
section: 40.6 The mindset: AI drafts, you own the trust
text:
40.6 The mindset: AI drafts, you own the trust
AI drafts the email, structures the handover note, and ranks the accounts, which removes most of the drafting work. The parts that protect the relationship stay with you: the timing, the owned next step, and verifying every fact before it reaches the customer.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.7 The Follow-up & Handover Gem
The Gem for this step is the Follow-up & Handover Gem. You open it, paste your meeting notes, and it returns the pieces you need next: a follow-up email draft for Lars, a short internal handover note, one owned next step, and a suggested segment label (see the example ↗). Paste an account Sheet instead, and it runs the two-signal segmentation across your accounts. Everything it produces fits into a normal email and a simple Google Sheet — and the email itself is finished and personalised in Gemini in Gmail.
You normally just open the prepared Gem and name your input.
Prompts you can copy and try. What you supply is your raw input — your meeting notes, or an account Sheet; the email, the four-line handover, the next step, and the segment labels are what the Gem produces. Open the Gem, paste one in, run it, then try a variation.
Start with the standard move: paste your meeting notes and ask for all three outputs at once. The single bracket marks where you drop in your own notes:






TipCopy into the Follow-up & Handover Gem



Here are my meeting notes with Lars [paste them] — draft the follow-up email, a four-line handover, and the next step.


When the meeting involved more than one contact, name them so the recap stays accurate (or ask for just one piece, like the four-line handover note on its own):






TipCopy into the Follow-up & Handover Gem



Here are my notes from the NorthStar meeting with Lars Meijer (operations) and Sophie Verbeek (procurement) [paste them]. Draft a follow-up email to Lars, a four-line handover, and one owned next step.


Switch to the segmentation task by pasting your account Sheet instead of notes:






TipCopy into the Follow-up & Handover Gem



Here is my account Sheet, one row per account. Rate each on upside and execution risk, group them, and give me one follow-up priority for each high-upside, high-risk account like NorthStar Facility Services.


The full prompt it carries:






NoteFollow-up & Handover Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a follow-up and account-segmentation partner for a Kärcher Professional salesperson.
You protect the value of a meeting after it ends and help decide where attention belongs next, and
you are scrupulous about not inventing certainty that was never agreed.

Task: Run one of two tasks; infer which from what the salesperson pastes (meeting notes → Task 1; an
account list or table → Task 2), or ask if it is unclear. If notes or a list are missing, ask for
them rather than inventing the content.
  TASK 1 — FOLLOW-UP (from the notes of one meeting). Produce four small things:
  1. A follow-up EMAIL (e.g. to an operations lead): a short recap of what was agreed, one clear
     owner, one concrete next step the customer can easily confirm, and a friendly close. Keep it
     tight — a follow-up, not a transcript.
  2. An internal HANDOVER NOTE a colleague could act on alone, in exactly four lines: the situation,
     the owner of the next step, the next step itself, and the one open question still to resolve.
  3. One NEXT STEP with a named owner (who does what, by when — only if a date was actually agreed).
  4. A suggested SEGMENT LABEL for this account in the salesperson's Sheet (using Task 2's two
     signals), with a one-line reason.
  TASK 2 — LIGHT SEGMENTATION (from an account Sheet, one row per account). For each account, rate
  two signals from 1 to 5 and explain each in one line:
  - UPSIDE: room to grow — rollout or service whitespace not yet worked.
  - EXECUTION RISK: how much depends on clean delivery; whether service or ownership is still unclear.
  Then group the accounts into high-upside/high-risk, high-upside/low-risk, and the rest. For the
  high-upside/high-risk group, give one concrete follow-up priority per account. Flag any row with
  missing data instead of guessing.

Context (what you already know): You support a Kärcher Professional salesperson and carry the account
and the value story from the earlier steps. Everything you produce has to fit a normal email and a
flat Google Sheet — no new system. The follow-up email is finished and
personalised later in Gemini inside Gmail, so you draft the substance, not the final polish.

Format: Task 1 returns the four items above (the handover note stays exactly four lines); Task 2
returns the two ratings, the grouping, and the per-account priorities. Return only the requested
items, no preamble. Respond in the same language the salesperson writes in.
Example for Task 1 (illustrative — adapt to the real notes, don't copy):
  Email (to Lars Meijer): Hi Mr Meijer, thanks for the time today. We agreed I would send the trial
  plan for the BR 45/22 on your two largest sites; you will confirm the start date internally. Shall
  we pencil a short call next week to lock it in?
  Handover note (four lines):
    Situation: Trial of the BR 45/22 discussed for the two largest sites; budget owner is Lars Meijer.
    Owner of next step: Martin Bauer sends the trial plan.
    Next step: Trial plan to Lars by end of week; he confirms the start date internally.
    Open question: Which two sites, and is service scope already agreed? (to verify)

Check:
- Work only from what the salesperson gives you and the named case; when something isn't in the
  notes, flag it as "to verify" rather than filling the gap.
- You draft; the salesperson decides the timing, owns the next step, and verifies every fact before
  it reaches the customer.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent a date, an owner, a commitment, or a
  quote that is not in the notes — a follow-up turns dangerous the moment it manufactures certainty.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.8 Try it yourself
Start with NorthStar, then make it real on an account of your own.

A · Practice on the NorthStar case

Draft the three outputs. Open the Follow-up & Handover Gem ↗, paste the ready-made NorthStar post-meeting summary ↗ (your own summary from Step 8 works just as well), and produce the follow-up email to Lars, the four-line handover note, and one concrete next step with a named owner.
Check before you trust. Read the outputs and confirm the model did not invent a date, an owner, or a commitment that was never agreed. If it did, that is a Revise, not a Go.

B · Do it for real: your own accounts

Segment. Paste your account Sheet and let the Gem rate upside and execution risk, so you know which account deserves your time next.
Send one real follow-up. Take an account you actually met recently, run the same routine, send that one next step within twenty-four hours, and update one segment label in your Sheet. That single next step is what carries the account into the thirty-day action plan.


As you debrief, ask the two questions that matter most:

Is the next step concrete and owned — and could a colleague act on the handover note alone?
This was made-up NorthStar data, so you prompt freely; on a real account the same task is fine in our approved tools, as long as you verify before you send.







Leading a team? The same follow-up toolkit scales from one account to coaching and steering a whole team — see Coaching & Steering in the Leadership Track.

title: 40  Follow-up & Steering
text:
40.9 In summary
Follow-up protects the value of the meeting after the conversation ends. From one meeting you create three small artifacts with the Follow-up & Handover Gem: a follow-up email with one clear next step, a short internal handover note a colleague can act on, and one owned next step, finished in Gemini in Gmail. A light read of two signals in a simple Google Sheet — upside and execution risk — then tells you which accounts deserve your time next. The structure works because disciplined, concrete relationship investment is what actually strengthens a relationship (Palmatier et al. 2006), and because keeping an existing account is one of the highest-return things you can do (Reichheld & Sasser 1990); differentiating your attention by account importance is key account management in miniature (Homburg, Workman & Jensen 2002). AI reduces the drafting work and makes the routine easy to keep, but the judgment, the timing, and the trust stay firmly with you.
That closes the NorthStar account journey. Next, the final chapter turns everything into a thirty-day action plan; leaders have their own Leadership Track that lifts this same toolkit up to portfolio altitude.






NoteWhat the research says





The moves in this chapter rest on three well-established literatures, and it is worth being precise about what each shows — and where it stops.
Why follow-up earns its own step. That “follow-up” is a real stage of selling — not an afterthought — is the seven-steps tradition (Moncrief & Marshall 2005, via research & qualification), where the step has shifted from a one-off after-sale task to a relationship-and-continuation logic. What makes that follow-up effective is the relationship-marketing evidence. In their meta-analysis, Palmatier, Dant, Grewal & Evans (2006) find that relationship investment has a large, direct effect on a seller’s objective performance — disciplined, concrete follow-up pays off — but also that not all contact is equal: performance is driven most by relationship quality (trust and relationship strength) and least by bare commitment, and the effect is stronger when the relationship is critical to the customer (services, business markets) and when it is built with an individual person rather than only with the selling firm. Read straight onto this chapter: a follow-up that carries a clear, owned next step and a kept promise strengthens the relationship, while mere frequency of contact without substance does little.
The economics of staying with it. Why is this such a high-leverage habit? Reichheld & Sasser (1990), in the customer-retention classic Zero Defections, show that keeping an existing customer is far more valuable — and cheaper — than winning a new one, and that small improvements in retention lift the long-run value of a customer disproportionately. For a seller, that reframes follow-up: keeping the momentum and trust of an almost-won account is not administrative tidying-up; it is one of the highest-return activities you have.
Why a two-signal read is enough to prioritise. The light segmentation — deciding which accounts deserve your time next — is the practitioner short form of key account management. Homburg, Workman & Jensen (2002) show that firms must treat their most important accounts differently and more intensively than the rest, and that how a firm concentrates effort on key accounts produces significant performance differences. Differentiating your own scarce attention by an account’s strategic importance and growth potential — instead of treating every account the same — is exactly that idea, scaled down to a single seller and a simple Sheet.






ImportantWhat the evidence does — and does not — prove



These are mechanisms, not a follow-up-outcome study. Palmatier et al. (2006) is a meta-analysis of relationship marketing in general, not a trial of a follow-up-email routine; it gives the trustworthy why (investment works, quality carries it, person beats firm), not a follow-up conversion rate. Reichheld & Sasser (1990) is an HBR retention classic, not a controlled experiment. And the upside × execution-risk 2×2 is a practitioner heuristic — like FABC in Step 6 or SPEICS in Step 5: valued for the discipline of asking both questions at once, not as a validated construct. Follow-up stays a human judgment; the AI drafts and ranks, you decide.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.1 You did the hard part. Now make it stick.
This is the closing bracket of the whole map above. You have walked all five buyer stages and all nine sales steps with one customer; the job now is to make the habit stick after you leave the room.
There is nothing new to learn here. Over one customer — NorthStar Facility Services — you walked the entire Kärcher sales cycle and produced AI-supported output at every step, and in the leadership track you saw the same toolkit lift to the whole portfolio. So this final chapter is not about more content. It is a plan to make the work stick, because the biggest risk after a workshop is not too few ideas — it is ideas that stay broad and that nobody owns, so daily work takes over and the momentum quietly fades.
The learning is done. The win after a workshop is not more ideas; it is one small habit, with a clear owner and a date. That beats ten good intentions every time, because the most common way a training fades is not disagreement — it is diffusion. The ideas stay broad, nobody owns a specific next action, and within a week the inbox has taken over. So everything in this chapter is deliberately narrow and personal: your account, your toolkit, your next step.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
section: 41.2 One customer, nine steps, one connected story
text:
41.2 One customer, nine steps, one connected story
Before you commit to anything, connect the dots. You did not learn a stack of separate tools. You followed one customer through the whole cycle, and every step produced output that fed the next. Read as one story, it runs like this.
Before the meeting, the groundwork came together. Acquisition used expert positioning — an internal industry knowledge base and the short posts drawn from it, each sharing an insight and inviting a conversation — so an inbound inquiry became the lead. Research and qualification turned that lead into a structured account briefing with a BANT read and a Go/No-Go, and this is where the first value hypothesis formed: the first answer to why Kärcher might matter to NorthStar. First contact turned that hypothesis into a role-appropriate first email to Lars Meijer, written to win the meeting, not the deal. Conversation preparation added a DISC observation read live from his reply behaviour and a fitting opening.
Then the meeting and everything after it. Needs analysis used SNIP for sharper questions and SPEICS to capture the whole need, and this is where the value hypothesis was confirmed: the need, and BANT, became real. Value argumentation turned that confirmed need into a customer-specific FABC case with a total-cost-of-ownership view, so the hypothesis from Step 2 became a substantiated argument. Presentation and demonstration became role-aware slides plus a storyboard for the real demo, both pulling the same need-and-value thread. Closing made the next step concrete and answered the price objection, and follow-up protected the momentum with a summary, an internal handover, and a named next step.
That single thread — from the value hypothesis in Step 2 to the substantiated argument in Step 6 — is the spine of the whole programme. It is also your workflow library, already built. You are not starting from a blank page.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
section: 41.3 Each step shipped a nugget you can reuse
text:
41.3 Each step shipped a nugget you can reuse
Here is why a 30-day plan is realistic and not a big ask. Every step of the cycle shipped as a finished bundle, and each bundle left you a small reinforcement nugget you can reuse: the Gem to open, the short prompt to paste, and the human check to run before anything goes to a customer.
On their own, these are tiny. The point is not to adopt all of them at once. Spaced out over the weeks after the training, one focus at a time, they turn from a workshop memory into a working habit. That spacing is deliberate, because a little practice every week beats one heroic push that has faded by Friday. The plan below simply sequences those nuggets so you are never trying to do everything in one go.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.4 Your 30-day action plan
Pick the step where AI saves you the most, and spread the work across four focused weeks. Each week reuses one nugget you already practised, and the rhythm matches the cycle itself, so the plan reinforces the order you just learned.



Week
Focus
What you run
The nugget you reuse




Week 1

Account-Briefing Gem (Deep Research, then verify)


Week 2
First contact & discovery
Draft the role-fit first email, then your discovery question set
First-Contact Gem, then the Needs-Analysis Gem (SNIP/SPEICS)


Week 3

Week 4

You do not have to do all four. Even two of these weeks, run for real, will give you evidence at the checkpoint. Start from structure in week one instead of a blank page, walk in prepared in week two, make the value specific in week three, and close the loop in week four. The plan works because it is small enough to actually run.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.5 Commit to one real account
Now make it yours. NorthStar was the safe training ground — a made-up account you could prompt with freely. The transfer step is to swap NorthStar for one real account from your own pipeline and run the same four-week plan against it.
When you do, the data rules from Module 0 apply: in our approved tools you can work with the real customer’s data, so your job is to verify the output, not protect the data. Verify anything you pull from public sources rather than trusting it, and keep the human check in the loop at every step. Then write the plan down where you will see it, with an account name and a checkpoint date. A commitment you can see is the one that actually starts. Choose the account now, in your head, so the plan has a real target the moment you leave the room.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
section: 41.6 Now look one step beyond chat
text:
41.6 Now look one step beyond chat
So far, every tool in this training has worked the same way: you open it, you prompt it, it drafts, and you review — all in the moment. That is exactly right for most of what you do, and it should stay your default for a long time.
But some work is repetitive and predictable — the same handful of steps every time a certain kind of email lands. For that, there is one realistic step beyond chat: standing automation, a routine that runs on a trigger in the background while you keep the decision. The pattern is simple to picture: when an email from a certain person, or about a certain topic, arrives, then do a defined thing. Treat this as an outlook, not today’s homework. It is named here so you know the path exists when you are ready, and so the manual habit you just committed to comes first.

41.6.1 One-off stays in chat, recurring becomes a trigger
The line between your everyday default and standing automation is clean, and it matters. Two different jobs, two different tools — use the simplest one that fits.









One-off
Recurring




The job
A single email, drafted now
A pattern that repeats


The tool
Gemini in Gmail
Workspace Studio


How it runs
You prompt, you review, you send
It runs on a trigger, you still approve


Reach for it when
Your everyday default
A standing task, not a single mail



A one-off email, like a single follow-up to Lars, stays where it has been all day: Gemini in Gmail — you prompt, review, and send. Workspace Studio is for the other case, where the same kind of message arrives again and again and triggers the same few steps. The test is one question: a single message you are writing now stays in Gmail; the same routine every time a certain email lands is when Workspace Studio earns its place. Most people here will live in the first column for a long time, and that is completely fine.


41.6.2 Workspace Studio: when X arrives, do Z
Workspace Studio is the no-code way to set up a standing routine that runs on a trigger, so a repeatable task happens without you starting it by hand. You describe the trigger and the action in plain language; there is no technical build. It has three parts:

The trigger — the incoming pattern: an email from a specific contact, or about a specific subject.
The action — the repeatable step you would otherwise do yourself: summarise the message, draft a standard reply, or file it to the right place.
The approval stays yours — a draft waits for your decision rather than going out on its own. The human check never leaves the seller.

It is no-code and trigger-based, not a developer task. One order rule keeps it healthy: standing automation comes after the habit, not before it. First you make the manual workflow clean and reliable, by hand, for a few weeks. Only once a task has proven itself repetitive and stable does it make sense to put it on a trigger. Automating a messy process just makes the mess faster — so the habit comes first, always, and the standing automation is the reward you unlock later.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.7 Two moments close the loop
The workshop does not end at the closing slide. It ends with two moments that close the loop, one now and one in thirty days.
Right now, fill in the short feedback form: what helped today, what was missing, what you would change. That makes the next run of this training better for your colleagues. Then, in thirty days, there is a brief check-in — and it is not about whether you liked the day. It looks at what actually ran. Did the week-one briefing happen? Did a follow-up go out with a named next step? What got faster or clearer, and what still failed? That review looks at real evidence, which is a stronger test than asking whether the workshop was enjoyable. So before you leave, put one checkpoint date in your calendar, thirty days out. A date in the calendar is what turns a good intention into a kept one.

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.8 Try it yourself
The closing move is concrete and takes only a couple of minutes:


Name the account. Pick the one real account from your pipeline that you will start with.
Write the four-week plan against it, week one through week four, and pencil in one checkpoint date thirty days out.
Commit out loud. Submit the feedback form and share your commitment with your team or your manager, because a commitment spoken out loud is far more likely to happen.


That is the bridge from this room into your daily sales work.
As you commit, ask the questions that keep the plan honest:

Which single week, run for real, would give you the clearest evidence at the checkpoint?
What changes when week one touches a real customer instead of NorthStar — what will you verify before anything you draft reaches them?
Which repetitive task, once the habit is stable, might later earn a Workspace Studio trigger — and which must stay a human decision?

title: 41  30-Day Action Plan & Transfer
text:
41.9 In summary
You walked one customer through the entire Kärcher sales cycle, from a cold name to a committed next step, and you now have an AI-supported workflow for every stage — plus, from the leadership track, a clear view of the same toolkit at portfolio altitude, and a sense of where standing automation fits once a task becomes routine. The 30-day plan sequences the nuggets you already practised into four focused weeks, run against one real account of your own. The automation outlook stays light on purpose: one-off emails stay with Gemini in Gmail, recurring patterns become a Workspace Studio trigger, and the approval always stays with you.
You already did the hard part. From here, the value depends on one practical move: one real account, one four-week plan, one checkpoint. The cycle is yours and the toolkit is yours. Go win NorthStar’s real-world equivalents.






NoteWhat the research says





This chapter is about transfer, and the move it makes — sequence small, spaced, owned actions instead of a single push — is one of the better-evidenced ideas in the learning and behaviour-change literature. As in Step 7, these are mechanisms from learning science, not sales-outcome trials; they explain why the plan is shaped this way, and the sales-context validity comes from the cycle the plan reuses.
Why a transfer step is needed at all. Baldwin & Ford (1988), in the canonical review of transfer of training, make the uncomfortable point that the value of training is realised only when what was learned is actually used and maintained on the job — and that transfer depends as much on the work environment and the opportunity to apply the skill as on the training itself. A workshop that ends at the closing slide is exactly the failure mode they describe. The whole point of a 30-day plan, an owned account, and a checkpoint is to engineer the transfer conditions deliberately rather than hope for them.
Why the practice is spaced over four weeks. Distributing practice over time produces markedly better long-term retention than massing it into one session — the spacing effect, one of the most robust findings in the study of memory (Cepeda, Pashler, Vul, Wixted & Rohrer 2006). One nugget a week for four weeks is not a scheduling convenience; it is the shape the evidence favours over a single heroic push that has faded by Friday.
Why the plan names an owner, a step, and a date. A vague good intention transfers poorly; a concrete implementation intention — an “if/when situation X arises, I will do Y” plan — reliably improves whether people actually act on their goals (Gollwitzer 1999). That is precisely why this chapter refuses to end on “use AI more” and insists instead on one real account, one next step with a named owner, and a checkpoint date in the calendar. It is also, neatly, the same when-X-do-Z shape as the Workspace Studio trigger — a manual implementation intention first, an automated one only once the habit is stable.






NoteWhat this evidence is, and is not



These are general learning and behaviour-change mechanisms, drawn from training, memory, and motivation research rather than from a study of AI-supported B2B selling. They justify the form of the plan — make it owned, space it out, state it concretely — not a specific adoption rate for this training. The substance the plan reinforces is the cycle itself, where each step’s discipline rests on its own sales-context sources. The honest reading: the plan is built the way the transfer evidence says habits actually form, and the proof for your own work is what the 30-day checkpoint shows actually ran.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 42  Leadership Track
text:
42.1 Leaders do not sell, they steer
This track does not sit on a single step of the map above. It runs alongside the whole cycle at a higher altitude: where a seller walks one customer from left to right, a leader works on the whole book of accounts at once.
The nine steps describe the account journey — the path a seller walks with one customer, with the NorthStar account as the running example. This track is for the people who do not walk that path themselves: directors, area and national sales managers, centre managers. They steer. And steering has its own repeatable AI rhythm, built from exactly the same tools the cycle uses. Nothing here is a new system; it is the cycle’s toolkit lifted to portfolio altitude. It gathers the leadership view of three steps — research, closing, and follow-up — into one self-contained track, so a leader has everything in one place.
This overview page gives you the shape of the track and the mindset behind it. The detail — the artifact, each Gem, and the hands-on exercise — lives on its own page, so you can read straight through or jump to the one you need.
Selling moves one customer forward. Steering shapes where the whole team spends its week. That single difference is the whole track, so it is worth holding the two roles side by side, because everything else follows from it.
A key account manager works in the account journey: one customer, step by step, with the NorthStar account as the artifact that travels through the cycle. A leader works on the portfolio: many accounts at once. A director or sales manager does not move a single deal forward in person. They decide where the team’s limited time should go across the entire book, spot the risks before they turn into missed quarters, coach the people, justify the forecast upward, and secure adoption of the tools and methods the team is meant to use. Their artifact is not one account but a portfolio view.
The only thing that changes between the two columns is altitude. The Gems, the Role-Task-Context-Format-Check scaffold, the prompt-validate quality gate, the data rules — all of it carries over unchanged. The same tool that drafts a briefing for one account can read a pipeline of forty; the same logic that qualifies one lead can prioritise the entire book. Same toolkit, higher vantage point.

title: 42  Leadership Track
text:
Cluster A — selling

title: 42  Leadership Track
text:
42.2 How this track is organized
The track is light on purpose: it does not mirror all nine steps. Leadership work has a natural equivalent at exactly three points in the cycle, and each one reuses a Gem you can recognise from the seller’s version of the step, lifted to portfolio scale — one Gem each. In practice you run all three from the same sheet in the same week.
Read the pages in order, or jump to the one you need:

The Leadership Control Sheet — your cockpit: one Google Sheet that the whole track runs on. Start here.
Pipeline-Steering (Step 2 for leaders) — prioritise the whole book by potential and flag the risks.
Forecast (Step 8 for leaders) — derive a defensible, deal-by-deal number you can stand behind.
Coaching & Steering (Step 9 for leaders) — turn team figures into a coaching cue per person and a weekly plan.
Try it yourself — run the three moves in order on the prepared portfolio, then on your own.

The three Gems map onto the cycle like this:



Cycle step
Seller’s job
Leader’s equivalent
The Gem




Step 2

Pipeline-Steering


Step 8
close one deal

Coaching & Steering

title: 42  Leadership Track
section: 42.3 AI reads the portfolio. You decide where the team spends its week.
text:
42.3 AI reads the portfolio. You decide where the team spends its week.
That line is the mindset for the whole leadership track, and it mirrors the one from the planning rhythm exactly, by design. AI is excellent at reading the portfolio: scanning every account, ranking by potential, spotting the aging deal and the quiet account, rolling up a forecast, drafting a coaching cue. But the decision about where your team actually spends its week stays with you. The signals inform the call; they do not make it. That is the right division of labour at every altitude of selling, and it is what keeps a leader irreplaceable even as the routine analysis gets automated.

title: 42  Leadership Track
text:
42.4 In summary
The leadership track is the second common thread of the programme: the same AI toolkit you used across the cycle, lifted to portfolio altitude. Leaders do not sell, they steer, so their leading artifact is not one account but the Leadership Control Sheet — a single Google Sheet holding the pipeline, the risks, the coaching notes, and the next decisions. The track does not add nine new steps; it anchors at three points in the cycle, with one Gem each: the Pipeline-Steering Gem prioritises the whole book at Step 2, the Forecast Gem gives a defensible number at Step 8, and the Coaching & Steering Gem turns team figures into coaching and a concrete plan at Step 9. At every point the division of labour is the same: AI reads the portfolio, the leader decides where the team spends its week.
Next, the final chapter turns everything — the cycle and this track — into your own thirty-day action plan, so the habits stick after the workshop.






NoteWhat the research says





The leadership track rests on three separate literatures, one for each anchor point, and they are well-established enough that the track is built on evidence rather than on a steering fashion. It is worth being precise about what each shows — and where it stops.
Prioritising the pipeline is key account management in miniature. Homburg, Workman & Jensen (2002) show that firms must treat their most important accounts differently and more intensively than the rest, and that how a firm concentrates effort on key accounts produces significant performance differences. Differentiating scarce attention by an account’s strategic importance and growth potential — instead of treating every row the same — is exactly that idea, scaled to one leader and a flat Sheet. The supporting move, letting a model rank prospects rather than ranking them by gut, has a long track record (D’Haen & Van den Poel 2013), with the discipline that the ranking must stay explainable so the leader can sanity-check it rather than trust a black box (Bohanec et al. 2017), and that the human keeps the judgement while the model prepares the prioritisation (Paschen et al. 2020).
A defensible forecast is a decomposed one. Judgmental sales forecasts are genuinely useful but systematically optimistic — anchored, prone to wishful weighting — and what reliably improves them is structuring and decomposition: weighing each deal explicitly rather than trusting one global figure, and combining the parts (Lawrence, Goodwin, O’Connor & Önkal 2006). That is precisely how the Forecast Gem reasons, deal by deal, surfacing the optimism instead of hiding it. And forecast quality turns out to rest on process and accountability, not individual heroics (Davis & Mentzer 2007), which is why the division of labour is explicit: the Gem structures the evidence and makes the per-deal reasoning visible; the leader owns the call and stands behind the number.
Steering works best as coaching, not result-reading. The sales-force control literature contrasts outcome-based control (measure only the result, push the risk onto the salesperson) with behavior-based control (work the process and the skills behind the result — observe, guide, coach). Anderson & Oliver (1987) argue the behaviour-based path has more favourable effects on competence, motivation, and customer orientation; Cravens, Ingram, LaForge & Young (1993) test this across 144 sales organisations and find behaviour-based control goes with markedly more management involvement — monitoring, guidance, and coaching — and a limited role for pure incentive pay. That coaching actually moves the needle is shown directly by Pousa & Mathieu (2014): manager coaching raised salespeople’s customer orientation and performance and reduced purely deal-driven behaviour. This is the academic root of the Coaching & Steering Gem: derive a cue from each person’s own numbers, and steer by coaching the process, not by reading out a result.






ImportantWhat the evidence does — and does not — prove



These are mechanisms, not a study of an AI steering routine. Homburg et al. (2002) is a key-account-management taxonomy, not a test of a one-Sheet pipeline ranking; it gives the trustworthy why (differentiate effort by importance), not a portfolio-ranking conversion rate. The forecasting evidence (Lawrence et al. 2006; Davis & Mentzer 2007) shows decomposition helps and that judgmental forecasts stay optimistic — so the Gem’s job is to surface that optimism, not to remove it. The control sources (Anderson & Oliver 1987; Cravens et al. 1993) justify a coaching philosophy, not a specific Gem, and Pousa & Mathieu (2014) is a real coaching study but in retail banking (B2C, Canada), so the mechanism transfers while the exact effect size does not. The sales-context validity comes from the anchors already in the cycle — the same Gems, the same quality gate, one altitude up. Steering stays a human judgement; the AI ranks, forecasts, and drafts, and you decide.






Sales Enablement | AI in International Sales at Kärcher

title: 43  The Leadership Control Sheet
text:
43.1 Your NorthStar is a whole portfolio
This is the page to read before the three Gem tiles, because all three run on this one sheet. The Leadership Track overview explains the altitude shift; this page makes the working data concrete.
Across the cycle, Cluster A spends the day reasoning about one account. For the leadership track we widen the lens. Your working data is not a single case but a portfolio: NorthStar sits inside it, alongside five to eight further example accounts, all in one pipeline view, on the same data everyone else in the room is using.
That matters because steering decisions are comparative by nature. You cannot decide which account deserves the team’s Monday by looking at one row; you decide it by looking across the whole list, comparing potential against potential and risk against risk. So the leadership exercises all run on this small portfolio, the same way Cluster A’s exercises all run on NorthStar. Same data discipline, wider frame.

title: 43  The Leadership Control Sheet
text:
NoteGive the Gem only the fields it needs — the portfolio is the safe ground



The shared seminar portfolio is made up for the training, so you can prompt with it freely. When you later point these Gems at your own real pipeline, the Module 0 data rules apply: in our approved tools you may work with the real pipeline. As good practice, give the Gem only the fields the task actually needs. To rank opportunities the model needs stage, value, and dates — not a customer’s phone number. Less data is both sharper and tidier, and you still verify the output.

title: 43  The Leadership Control Sheet
text:
43.2 What the sheet holds
This is the leading artifact for the whole track, the Cluster-B counterpart to the seller’s NorthStar account, so it is worth being concrete about what it is. The Leadership Control Sheet is a single Google Sheet — nothing more exotic than that. It is your cockpit: what NorthStar is for a seller, this sheet is for you. It holds four things:

Pipeline and segments — every account in one place, so the whole book can be ranked rather than reviewed account by account.
Risks — the deals that need a human decision, made visible instead of buried in the middle of a list.
Coaching notes — what each team member needs, kept next to the numbers that justify it.
Next management decisions — the real output of steering work, written down rather than carried in your head.

Everything the three leadership Gems produce lands back here. For a seller, the artifact that travels through the cycle is the NorthStar account; for a leader, the artifact that travels through this track is this sheet.

title: 43  The Leadership Control Sheet
section: 43.3 The whole data model is a flat list
text:
43.3 The whole data model is a flat list
A common worry at this level is that steering with AI needs a data warehouse or heavy new tooling. It does not. The Control Sheet is a flat list, one row per account, with a handful of columns you can already maintain: account and segment, stage and value, last contact, and a short note. The last-contact date is the single most useful field, because it lets the model see which accounts have gone quiet. That is the whole data model, and it is enough for Gemini to read the entire portfolio and surface the three signals that matter for steering:

Stage aging — which deals have sat too long in one stage. Long aging is not always bad, but it often signals a stalled deal, unclear ownership, or low urgency.
Momentum — which accounts are moving and which have gone quiet. A good account with low momentum still needs a nudge, even when the value looks attractive.
Whitespace — where there is unworked room to grow. In the Kärcher context this usually means service opportunity, rollout potential, or installed-base potential that has not yet been turned into an active motion. It is so often hidden service revenue.

There is one signal worth checking before any clever ranking: the follow-up gap, the accounts with no recent contact and no defined next step. They are not lost, they are simply forgotten, and they leak revenue quietly. Asking the model to list every account that has gone quiet without a plan is often the fastest win available in a given week.
With the sheet in place, the three Gem tiles each read one part of it: the Pipeline-Steering Gem ranks the whole book, the Forecast Gem reads the closing outlook, and the Coaching & Steering Gem reads the team-KPI tab.

title: 44  Pipeline-Steering: prioritise the whole book
text:
44.1 What it does
This is the first of the three leadership moves. It reads the flat pipeline list in your Control Sheet and turns a vague quarter target into a focused set of moves for the week. Part of the Leadership Track.
At Step 2 the seller’s Account-Briefing Gem qualifies a single lead. The leadership equivalent applies that same qualification logic to the entire portfolio. The Pipeline-Steering Gem reads the flat pipeline list in your Control Sheet and does the comparison work for you: it ranks accounts by potential and flags the risk accounts and the potential accounts so they stop hiding in the middle of the list. You hand it two things — the portfolio in the sheet and your real target for the quarter — and it returns a short ranked list rather than a wall of data: the top accounts to push, the risk accounts that need attention now, and the follow-up gaps where momentum has quietly died. That turns a vague quarter target into a focused set of moves for the week, across the whole team’s book. A common use is the quarterly acquisition plan, derived from the same list. You stay the one who decides; the Gem just does the sorting and surfaces what you would otherwise miss.

title: 44  Pipeline-Steering: prioritise the whole book
section: 44.2 Prompts you can copy and try
text:
44.2 Prompts you can copy and try
What you supply is the flat pipeline list from your Leadership Control Sheet (account, segment, stage, value, last contact, note) and your real target for the quarter; the ranking, the risk flags, and the follow-up gaps are what the Gem produces. Vary only the question you ask of the same connected sheet.
Start with the shortest version, the example prompt the Gem itself suggests, run against the pipeline you have connected:






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



Rank this pipeline by potential and flag the risk accounts.


Give it your quarter target so the ranking sorts against what you actually need to hit:






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



Here is my pipeline from the Leadership Control Sheet, and my target this quarter is to grow service revenue in the Benelux. Rank the whole book by potential against that target, and flag the risk accounts and the follow-up gaps separately. Use only what is in the list.


When the week is short, ask only for the accounts that have gone quiet, the fastest win in the sheet:






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



From this pipeline, list only the stalled accounts: the ones with no recent contact and no defined next step. For each, give one line on why it stalled and the next steering action. Use only what is in the list.


Narrow to one segment or one owner when you want a focused view rather than the whole book:






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



Rank only the BSC accounts in this pipeline by potential, including NorthStar Facility Services, and flag the risk accounts among them. Use only what is in the list.


Or ask for a short top list when you want the few accounts the team should push first:






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



From this pipeline, give me the top five accounts by potential, with one line of reasoning each, plus the two risk accounts I should act on this week. Use only what is in the list.








NotePipeline-Steering Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a portfolio-level pipeline analyst supporting a Kärcher Professional sales leader.
You reason about a whole book of accounts rather than a single deal, you keep every recommendation
transparent and explainable so the leader can sanity-check it, and you leave the steering call to
the leader.

Task: When the leader shares a flat pipeline list (one row per account, with columns such as
account, segment, stage, deal size or potential, last activity date, owner):
  1. Read where each account sits: its stage, how long it has been in that stage (age), and
     whether it is moving or stuck (momentum).
  2. Surface whitespace and unrealized potential — accounts or segments where Kärcher's equipment,
     service, and standardization could grow but the pipeline is thin.
  3. Prioritize the whole pipeline by potential, and flag the risk accounts (stuck, aging, or
     slipping) and the high-potential accounts explicitly.
  If a column the ranking depends on is missing or unreadable, say what you would need rather than
  guessing; work from the rest. If an optional fit reference (ideal customer profile, market
  context) is attached, judge potential and whitespace against it, not just the numbers in the sheet.

Context (what you already know): The leadership question is not "is this one lead worth my time?"
but "which accounts deserve the team's time first, where is the risk, and where is the untapped
potential?" The leader works from a flat list
(typically a connected Google Sheet), and the output feeds the Sales Leadership Control Sheet.
Prioritization is a hypothesis for the leader to confirm, not a verdict.

Format: Return a ranked overview ready to paste into the Leadership Control Sheet: each account
with its rank, a short reason for why it ranks where it does, and the next steering action. Group
or label the risk accounts and the high-potential accounts so they stand out. Return only the
ranked overview — no preamble. Respond in the same language the leader writes in.

Check:
- Work only from the pipeline data provided (and any attached fit reference); when a field is
  missing, name what you would need instead of inferring it. Keep each ranking explainable, so the
  leader can sanity-check the reasoning rather than trust a black box.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent accounts, numbers, or account
  history that the data does not contain.



The quality check before you act on it: is the ranking actually backed by what is in the sheet, or by a hopeful assumption? Are the risk accounts genuinely the ones you would worry about? Did the Gem flag missing data rather than fill it in? You keep the strongest part of the ranking, fix the weakest, and then steer.

Recipe · Prioritise the pipeline

Makes

a prioritised pipeline overview — the top accounts to push, the risk accounts, and the follow-up gaps — for the whole book.

Open

Pipeline-Steering Gem ↗.

Do

Point it at the flat pipeline list in your Leadership Control Sheet (account, segment, stage, value, last contact, note) and name your real target for the quarter. Then sanity-check the ranking against what is actually in the sheet.

Prompt

Here is my pipeline from the Leadership Control Sheet, plus my target this quarter: [your real target]. Rank the whole book by potential against that target, and flag the risk accounts and the follow-up gaps separately. Use only what is in the list.

Save as

write the ranked overview back into the Leadership Control Sheet — your cockpit.

Then

that ranked book is the input for the forecast and the coaching plan.

title: 45  Forecast: justify a defensible number
text:
45.1 What it does
This is the second of the three leadership moves. It reads the closing outlook across the pipeline and produces a forecast you can actually stand behind in a review. Part of the Leadership Track.
At Step 8 the seller is working the close on one deal. The leadership equivalent is the forecast, and forecasting is where leaders most often feel exposed, because they have to defend a number upward. The Forecast Gem reads the closing outlook across the pipeline — the stage, the value, and the close probability on each account — and derives a reasoned forecast you can actually stand behind in a review. The point is not a more optimistic number; it is a defensible one, where every figure traces back to a specific account and a stated assumption rather than a gut feel. That is exactly the verify habit from the quality gate, applied to reporting. The Gem keeps its assumptions visible — which deals it counted, at what probability, and why — so you can do the part only a leader can do: challenge the shaky deals before the number leaves your desk.

title: 45  Forecast: justify a defensible number
section: 45.2 Prompts you can copy and try
text:
45.2 Prompts you can copy and try
What you supply is the closing outlook from your Leadership Control Sheet (account, stage, value, close probability, any buying-signal notes); the weighted number, the per-deal reasoning, and the optimism flags are what the Gem produces. Vary only what you ask of the same connected sheet, never the forecast itself.
Start with the shortest version, the example prompt the Gem itself suggests, run against the pipeline you have connected:






TipCopy into the Forecast Gem



Turn this pipeline into a weighted forecast and flag where I'm being optimistic.


Ask for the full decomposition when you need to defend the number in a review:






TipCopy into the Forecast Gem



Here is my pipeline from the Leadership Control Sheet, with stage, value, and close probability per deal. Derive a reasoned forecast for the quarter, decomposed deal by deal, with the assumption behind each one visible. Flag the optimistic risks. Use only what is in the list.


When you only need the near-term picture, scope it to the deals expected to land this quarter:






TipCopy into the Forecast Gem



From this pipeline, forecast only the deals likely to close this quarter. Weight each by stage and signal strength, show the assumption per deal, and flag any that are carried by a big number but a weak signal. Use only what is in the list.


Group the outlook by stage when you want to see where the value is concentrated:






TipCopy into the Forecast Gem



Take this pipeline and group the forecast by stage. For each stage show the weighted value and which deals drive it, then flag the aging or stalled deals separately. Use only what is in the list.








NoteForecast Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a sales-forecasting analyst for a Kärcher Professional sales leader. You turn a whole
pipeline into a reasoned, defensible call rather than a gut number, weighing each deal on its merits
and naming where optimism is creeping in.

Task: When the leader shares a pipeline list (one row per deal, columns such as account, stage, deal
size or potential, last activity, owner, and any notes on buying signals):
  1. If no pipeline is shared, ask for the Sheet first; do not invent deals or history to fill a gap.
  2. Read each deal's closing outlook from where it sits (stage), how long it has sat there (age),
     whether it is moving or stuck (momentum), and the strength of the buying signal in the notes.
  3. Weight each deal explicitly by stage and signal strength rather than treating every open deal as
     equally likely — decompose the forecast into defensible parts, deal by deal, and name the
     assumption behind any weighting.
  4. Derive a reasoned forecast (expected to land this period, with a short reason per deal) and flag
     the optimistic risks: deals that are aging, stuck, or carried by a weak signal but a big number.

Context (what you already know): You serve a Kärcher Professional sales leader and read a flat pipeline
list straight from the connected Google Sheet (the Sales
Leadership Control Sheet). The leadership question is the closing question scaled up: of all the open
deals, which will actually land, and when? You apply the same signal-strength logic the team uses on a
single deal across the whole portfolio. Judgmental forecasts are useful but run optimistic, which is
why the reasoning is decomposed and the optimism is surfaced.

Format: Return a ranked overview ready to paste into the Leadership Control Sheet, with the per-deal
reasoning visible so the leader can defend the number in reporting. Return only the overview, no
preamble. Respond in the same language the leader writes in.

Check:
- Work only from the pipeline data provided; where a field is missing, say what you would need rather
  than guessing it.
- Keep the reasoning explicit and decomposed, so the leader can sanity-check each deal instead of
  trusting one black-box figure.
- The forecast is a reasoned hypothesis for the leader to own and stand behind, not a verdict, and it
  is prone to optimism — surface that, don't hide it.
- Keep this to deal-level facts and ownership; no personal performance data about named individuals.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent a deal, a number, a close date, or a
  history the Sheet does not contain.



The value here is not that AI forecasts for you. It is that it lays out the reasoning fast, so your judgement is spent on the deals that actually swing the quarter, not on assembling the spreadsheet. A confident forecast is not a verified forecast: keep the assumptions visible, because the number is only as good as its reasons.

Recipe · Justify a forecast

Makes

a reasoned, defensible forecast for the quarter, decomposed deal by deal with the assumptions visible.

Open

Forecast Gem ↗.

Do

Point it at the closing outlook in the Leadership Control Sheet. It weighs each deal by stage and signal strength rather than guessing one global number. Pressure-test the shakiest deals before the number goes up the line.

Prompt

Here is my pipeline from the Leadership Control Sheet, with stage, value, and close probability per deal. Derive a reasoned forecast for the quarter, decomposed deal by deal, with the assumption behind each one visible. Flag the optimistic risks. Use only what is in the list.

Save as

the forecast lands back in the Leadership Control Sheet, ready for reporting.

Then

carry the defensible number into your review; carry the people into the coaching plan.

title: 46  Coaching & Steering: from numbers to a plan
text:
46.1 What it does
This is the third of the three leadership moves. It bridges the gap leaders quietly struggle with: getting from a revenue number to what someone should actually do on Tuesday. Part of the Leadership Track.
At Step 9 the seller follows up with one customer. The leadership equivalent is steering and coaching the team, and this Gem bridges a gap that leaders quietly struggle with: getting from a revenue number to what someone should actually do on Tuesday. The Coaching & Steering Gem reads the team KPIs and produces a coaching cue per team member, grounded in their own numbers rather than a generic tip. Then it does the translation that is easy to skip: it turns the revenue figures into concrete weekly planning, visit planning, and clear steering priorities. The output is a steering plan you can take straight into a one-to-one or a team meeting.

title: 46  Coaching & Steering: from numbers to a plan
section: 46.2 Prompts you can copy and try
text:
46.2 Prompts you can copy and try
What you supply is the team-KPI tab of your Leadership Control Sheet (per person: revenue against target, pipeline, visits or activity, win rate); the coaching cues, the weekly plan, and the steering priorities are what the Gem produces. Vary only what you ask of the same connected sheet, never the cues themselves.
Start with the shortest version, the example prompt the Gem itself suggests, run against the team KPIs you have connected:






TipCopy into the Coaching & Steering Gem



Give me one coaching cue per salesperson and a plan for the week.


Ask for the full picture when you are preparing one-to-ones and the team meeting together:






TipCopy into the Coaching & Steering Gem



Here are my team KPIs from the Leadership Control Sheet. Give one coaching cue per person grounded in their own numbers, then turn the figures into a weekly and visit plan plus the team's top steering priorities. Base everything on the data I gave you.


When you want to focus the week, ask only who is behind target and what should change:






TipCopy into the Coaching & Steering Gem



From these team KPIs, show me only the people behind their revenue target. For each, name the pattern in their numbers and one coaching cue that points at the activity behind it. Base everything on the data I gave you.


Or look at coverage rather than people, to see where the pipeline is thin against target:






TipCopy into the Coaching & Steering Gem



From these team KPIs, show me pipeline coverage by salesperson against their target, and flag anyone whose pipeline is too thin to make the number. Base everything on the data I gave you.








NoteCoaching & Steering Gem — full prompt





Role: You are a coaching and steering partner for a Kärcher Professional sales leader. You coach the
behaviour and the process behind a number rather than the number alone, and you keep every cue
anchored in the person's own figures so it is something they can act on, not a verdict.

Task: When the leader shares the team KPIs, produce two things:
  1. COACHING — for EACH team member, one specific coaching cue grounded in THAT person's own numbers
     (not a generic tip), with a one-line reason drawn from the figures (e.g. "pipeline strong but
     win rate low → work the close, not more leads"). Point to the activity or skill behind a weak
     number, so the cue is actionable.
  2. STEERING — translate the revenue figures into a concrete plan for next week, a visit plan (who on
     the team to visit or join on a call, and why), and three clear steering priorities for the team.
  If no KPIs are shared, ask for the Sheet first; do not invent figures, and where a figure is
  missing, flag it rather than guessing.

Context (what you already know): You serve a Kärcher Professional sales leader and read the team's KPIs
straight from the connected flat Google Sheet (one row per team
member: name, revenue vs. target, pipeline, visits or activity, win rate, and any notes). This is the
same toolkit as a single follow-up, pointed at the whole team: instead of one set of meeting notes the
input is the team KPIs, and instead of one follow-up email the outputs are a coaching cue per person
and a plan for the week. Behaviour-based coaching — working the process, not just the result — is what
moves competence and customer orientation, which is why each cue reaches behind the figure.

Format: A coaching cue (with its one-line reason) per team member, then the steering block (next
week's plan, the visit plan, three priorities). Return only those two blocks, no preamble. Respond in
the same language the leader writes in.

Check:
- Base every cue and priority on the numbers provided; where a figure is missing, flag it rather than
  guessing.
- Coach the process, not only the outcome — point to the activity or skill behind a weak number, so
  the cue is something the person can act on.
- You prepare; the leader owns the coaching conversation and the final call on priorities. Offer a
  second opinion and a starting structure, not a decision about a named person.
- Keep personal performance data inside the team; this is for the leader's own steering, not for
  sharing.
- Reserve a hard stop for what is costly if wrong: never invent a number, a date, an owner, or a
  commitment that the Sheet does not contain.



What the Gem cannot do is the human part. It does not know that one rep just had a hard quarter, or that another is ready for a stretch account. You coach the people, not the spreadsheet; the Gem just clears the desk work so you have the time and the evidence to do it well.

Recipe · Coach and steer the team

Makes

one coaching cue per team member, grounded in their own numbers, plus a concrete weekly and visit plan.

Open

Coaching & Steering Gem ↗.

Do

Point it at the team-KPI tab of the Leadership Control Sheet. It turns the figures into a steering plan; you own the coaching conversation and the final call on priorities.

Prompt

Here are my team KPIs from the Leadership Control Sheet. Give one coaching cue per person grounded in their own numbers, then turn the figures into a weekly and visit plan plus the team's top steering priorities. Base everything on the data I gave you.

Save as

write the cues and the plan into the Leadership Control Sheet.

Then

take the steering plan into your one-to-ones and the team meeting. Re-run the three moves as a weekly cadence.

title: 47  Try it yourself: the steering rhythm
text:
Walk out holding a prioritised portfolio and a short steering plan you could use on Monday. Run the three leadership moves in order on the prepared portfolio Sheet, then point them at your own pipeline.

This is the hands-on close of the Leadership Track. It brings the three Gems together — Pipeline-Steering, Forecast, and Coaching & Steering — into one weekly rhythm you run from the same Control Sheet.
The point of the track is to walk out holding a prioritised portfolio and a short steering plan you could use on Monday. Start on the prepared portfolio Sheet, then run the three moves in order.

1. Prioritise the pipeline. Open the Pipeline-Steering Gem ↗, point it at the Control Sheet, and name your real target for the quarter. Read the prioritised overview it produces.






TipCopy into the Pipeline-Steering Gem



Here is my pipeline (pasted below from the Leadership Control Sheet): one row per account, with
account, segment, stage, value, last-contact date, and a short note. My target this quarter is
[name your real target, e.g. grow service revenue in the Benelux].

Rank the whole pipeline by potential against that target and give me a short ranked overview — the
top accounts to push, with one line of reasoning each. Then flag separately the risk accounts
(stalled, aging, or gone quiet) and the follow-up gaps (no recent contact and no next step). Use
only what is in the list, and flag any row with missing data instead of guessing.


2. Pick the two risk accounts you would act on this week, and be ready to say why.
3. Coach from the numbers. Open the Coaching & Steering Gem ↗, point it at the team KPIs, and note one coaching cue per team member.






TipCopy into the Coaching & Steering Gem



Here are my team KPIs (pasted below): per team member, revenue against target, active pipeline value
and stage mix, win rate where available, and recent activity such as visits or last-contact recency.

Give me one specific coaching cue per team member, grounded in their own numbers — name the pattern
in the data that prompts it, not a generic tip. Then translate the numbers into a concrete steering
plan for the coming weeks: who focuses on which accounts, a visit and weekly plan, and the two or
three steering priorities for the team. Base everything on the data I gave you and mark anything you
inferred.


As you go, apply the questions that keep the work honest:

Is the ranking actually backed by what is in the sheet, or by a hopeful assumption?
Which forecast deal looks shakiest, and would the total survive without it?
Does each coaching cue genuinely fit that person, or is it generic?

4. Now make it real. Point the same three Gems at your own pipeline and your own team. In our approved tools that real pipeline is fine to work with (see the Module 0 data rules); as good practice, give the Gems only the fields the task needs — stage, value, dates — and verify the output. This is the rhythm you repeat, a weekly steering cadence, not a one-off exercise.
